Introduce the traditions of Chinese New Year to your children with our 'Show and Tell' educational video, perfect for children ages 5-11.

Children can join the Chinese dragon as he enters his celebratory scrap book and shows the children all about how the event is celebrated in his country.

Perfect for use in the classroom for teaching EYFS, KS1, and KS2, our video explores the celebration of Lunar New Year and how it's celebrated.

Children will learn about the customs surrounding red envelopes, Chinese lanterns, and the significance of the Year of the Rabbit.

Watch as we learn how Chinese New Year is celebrated, and how to understand Chinese culture and traditions.

This video is a perfect tool for teaching Chinese New Year and offers a great learning resource.

It is a great classroom video that offers a fun way to educate your students about Chinese Celebrations and culture.

To support the video, we have a whole host of supporting resources are available to download - ranging from writing and drawing activities, to colouring and discussion cards.

We suggest using the video before using any of the supporting activities, to increase learning opportunities and open discussions in your classroom.

title: What Chinese New Year Is Really About - Origins, Legends & 30 Days of Traditions | Spring Festival
description:
🎉 What is Chinese New Year really about? Is it just firecrackers, red envelopes, and dumplings? Or is there something deeper behind Chinese New Year traditions — something ancient, mystical, and just a little terrifying?

In this video, we’ll explore what Chinese New Year is really about, from its origin in star-gazing rituals to a modern month-long extravaganza of ancestor worship, kitchen gods, fireworks, and cosmic accounting. This isn’t just another “how to celebrate Chinese New Year” video — it’s a full-blown crash course in Chinese New Year mythology, Lunar New Year customs, and the spiritual logic behind why your mom becomes a cleaning warlord in January.

🌕 We begin with the origin of Chinese New Year, tracing how ancient Chinese people observed the Big Dipper to decide when a new year begins. We'll uncover the astrological roots of the Chinese zodiac and how the changing sky signaled a time for renewal — or judgment.

Then comes the legend of Nian, the punctual demon of New Year’s Eve. With its glowing eyes, sharp claws, and taste for humans, it wasn’t just a story — it was the myth behind Chinese firecrackers, red paper, and staying up all night. This Nian monster legend explains why Lunar New Year began as a monster-repellent strategy, not a celebration.

And let’s not forget the Kitchen God, the divine tattletale who reports your family’s behavior to the heavens. That sticky candy? Not just a snack — it’s hush money. This video dives into Kitchen God rituals, revealing how incense offerings, malt candy, and a clean stove might just secure you some extra celestial brownie points.

💥 From there, we go day by day, decoding all the Chinese New Year rituals from Laba Festival to the Lantern Festival. Learn about:

Ancestor worship customs (yes, ghosts are part of the guest list)

Why people burn incense to call home great-grandma’s spirit

The meaning behind red decorations, dumplings, and fish dishes

How to properly “sweep out bad luck” without offending the divine

The day you honor rocks. (Seriously. It’s called Stone Day.)

And when Lantern Festival finally arrives on the 15th day of the first lunar month, you’ll know why people eat tangyuan, guess lantern riddles, and watch dragon dances — not just because they’re pretty, but because they close the ritual loop started weeks earlier.

🔍 Featured Topics in This Video:

Chinese New Year origin and history

Nian monster legend

Kitchen God ritual explained

Chinese ancestor worship traditions

Day-by-day Chinese New Year ritual calendar

Meaning behind Chinese red decorations and firecrackers

What tangyuan symbolizes during Lantern Festival

Why Lunar New Year isn’t just one day

How the Chinese zodiac ties into the calendar

The secret meaning of sweeping and cleaning

Stone Day, Human Day, and other hidden traditions

Red envelopes and their spiritual roots

🧧 Whether you're curious about the history of Chinese red envelopes, looking to understand Spring Festival etiquette, or want to impress your relatives with obscure facts about Tofu Day, Pig-Slaughtering Day, or why sons-in-law get treated like emperors on the 2nd day — this is your guide.

project_name_en: Spring Festival, social practices of the Chinese people in celebration of traditional new year
title: Legend Of Nian -Lunar New Year
description:
Chinese New Year, also known as the Spring Festival or Lunar New Year, is the most important traditional Chinese holiday. It celebrates the beginning of a new year on the traditional lunisolar Chinese calendar.

According to Eastern mythology there was a horrible beast called Nian which lived on the mountains.
Towards the end of Winter when there was nothing to eat, Nian would come on the first day of New Year to the villages to eat livestock, crops, and even villagers, especially children.
The villagers would live in terror over the Winter, but over time they learned that the ferocious Nian was afraid of three things: fire, and noise and  the colour red.  So when the New Year was about to come, the villagers would hang red lanterns and red spring scrolls on windows and doors.  They also used firecrackers to frighten away the Nian. From then on, Nian never came to the village again.

That is the story of #chineseNewyear .

Thank you for watching.

project_name_en: Spring Festival, social practices of the Chinese people in celebration of traditional new year
title: Happy Chinese New Year! A selection of the 2026 CMG Spring Festival Gala
description:
The 2026 Spring Festival Gala, an annual televised celebration hosted by the China Media Group (CMG), was broadcasted live on the eve of the Chinese New Year, as families come together to ring in the Year of the Horse.
Since first broadcast in 1983, the gala has been a cherished part of Chinese people's celebration of the Spring Festival. The gala recognized by Guinness World Records as the most-watched annual television program on the planet.
The Spring Festival itself was inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity in December 2024.

title: Chinese New Year in China 🇨🇳 INSANE Midnight Fireworks in Dali
description:
I wasn’t prepared for what happened at midnight…
I celebrated Chinese New Year in Dali Ancient Town, in China’s Yunnan province, and it turned into one of the most intense nights of my life.
Before midnight, the streets were already alive. Fireworks echoed through the ancient town, families gathered together, and my kind inn hosts made traditional dumplings to celebrate the new year.
I felt incredibly grateful to be welcomed into such a special moment.
But when midnight arrived…
Everything exploded.
Fireworks filled the sky, the streets became chaos, the noise was deafening, and the entire town came alive in a way I have never experienced before.
I even lit my own fireworks in China.
This is the real Chinese New Year.
Not what you see on TV.
This is something you feel.
📍 Dali Ancient Town, Yunnan, China
🧧 Spring Festival Eve
—
China has completely changed my life, and I’m sharing the real experience here.

title: Chinese New Year for Kids 🧧Lion Dance 🦁 Lunar New Year Celebrations 🌙 Educational Videos for Kids
description:
Oliver and Lucas celebrate China's most important festival, Chinese New Year! 

Chinese New Year, also known as Lunar New Year or Spring Festival, is annual 15-day festival in China that marks the beginning of the lunar new year in the Chinese calendar. 

The Chinese calendar is based on the phases of the moon and so the Lunar New Year is celebrated on the first new moon of the year.

Celebrated by Chinese communities around the world, Chinese New Year is a time when families come together to wish each other good luck, happiness, and prosperity for the coming year.

It is custom to bring in the New Year with a traditional Chinese Lion dance. It is believed that a Lion dance will attract prosperity and good fortune.

The Chinese Lion Dance is a traditional dance in Chinese culture that is performed as part of the New Year celebrations. 

Lions and dragons are a symbol of luck in Chinese culture. So, by performing a lion or dragon dance, it is believed that it will scare away evil spirits and bring good fortune and happiness to those who watch it.

In celebration of Chinese new year it is customary that a Lion or a dragon dance is performed  It creates a festive atmosphere and is said to bring good luck and happiness for the New Year. 

Happy Chinese New Year from Oliver and Lucas!

Join Isabel as she celebrates Chinese New Year. Isabel receives a lucky red envelope from her family in China to begin the celebrations for a brand new year. We watch Isabel get creative as she helps her mum and grandma make lanterns to decorate the house with. Once the lanterns are complete, they head to a Chinese School to learn all about the year of the pig and how to do some traditional Chinese paper folding. 

After some crafts Isabel goes to a farm with mum where she visits some real pigs and even gets to feed them. When Isabel and mum return home they enjoy the New Year with all of their friends and family. Isabel finishes the celebrations by leading the dragon in a Chinese New Year
parade.

description:
The 2026 Spring Festival Gala, an annual televised celebration hosted by the China Media Group (CMG), got underway at 20:00 Monday (Beijing Time) on the eve of the Chinese New Year, as families come together to ring in the Year of the Horse at midnight. 

Aside from the main stage in Beijing, four sub-venues were set up across the country in Heilongjiang, Yiwu, Hefei, and Yibin. Starting in 1983, the hours-long gala has been part of the New Year celebrations of the Chinese people. It was also recognized by Guinness World Records as the most-watched annual television program on the planet.

project_name_en: Spring Festival, social practices of the Chinese people in celebration of traditional new year
title: Chinese New Year Is NOT Lunar New Year… Here’s Why
description:
Should it be called Chinese New Year… or Lunar New Year?

In this video, I explore the real story behind the Spring Festival, from the calendar science that determines the date each year to the cultural traditions that make it the most important holiday in China. While many people around the world use the term “Lunar New Year,” the festival is actually based on a complex lunisolar calendar system that keeps it tied to winter and the agricultural seasons.

I also take a look at how the holiday is celebrated across China — from reunion dinners and red envelopes to the Spring Festival Gala, the world’s largest annual migration, and the shared traditions that bring families together. Finally, we touch on the debate around naming, origin, and how this festival has spread globally.

No matter what you call it, one thing is certain: it’s a time for family, luck, and a fresh start.

title: How to celebrate Chinese New Year the local way: A day-by-day guide
description:
"Becoming Chinese" isn't about nationality. It's about stepping into a rhythm of life shaped by food, family, rituals and time. Chinese New Year isn't just one night of fireworks. It's a 15-day experience, and every day has its own meaning, mood and tradition. It ranges from cleaning your home and sharing a reunion dinner to temple incense, family visits, slow days, money rituals and lantern-lit nights. This is the Spring Festival, day by day. As "Becoming Chinese" trends across global social platforms, Gen Z is discovering the beauty of Chinese everyday life. This is Chinese New Year – not as a holiday, but as a way of living.

This is the biggest day of the Lunar New Year celebrations in New York City, where dozens of lion dance troupes bless local businesses to ring in the Year of the Horse.

If you've never been, it’s beautiful chaos—expect firecrackers, incredible athleticism, and the best cultural energy in NYC. We’ll be hitting Mott Street, Bayard Street, and Pell Street to catch the most acrobatic performances.

title: Year of the Dragon. Chinese New Year Dragon Dance 2024 農曆新年舞龍. 恭喜發財！
description:
Year of the Dragon. Chinese New Year Dragon Dance 2024 農曆新年舞龍. 恭喜發財！

May the Year of the Dragon bring everyone plenty of wealth, good health and new adventures!

Chinese dragons are believed to bring good luck. It symbolises wisdom, power, and wealth.

The Dragon Dance is a form of traditional dance typically seen during festive celebrations.

The dragon consists of a large dragon head and a long serpentine body with a number of sections on poles and a tail. The dragon dance is performed by a skilled team, moving the dragon to bring it to life.

A person holding a rod with a big ball (called the 'Pearl of Wisdom') at the top leads the dragon during the dance. The Dragon follows the movements of the ball around, its body moving in waves, appears to be dancing.

Chinese New Year is painted red, do you know why? Because it’s Xin nian…  ‘Nian’ in Chinese stands for the mythical monster and also happens to be the word for ‘year’. The old nian is believed to be scared of red. Spread the weeks with bright red and welcome the new year with your arms wide open.

Chinese New Year song is filled with Red and gold colors, and people have rice dumplings and long noodles for long life. From the 3rd week of January to the first week of February, the Chinese celebrate for two weeks straight.

This song is for you if you want to learn more about The Chinese New Year.

project_name_en: Spring Festival, social practices of the Chinese people in celebration of traditional new year
title: Chinese New Year of the Fire Horse: Feb 2026
description:
The Chinese New Year of the Fire Horse arrives on February 16th, 2026 in the Southern Hemisphere and February 17th in the Northern Hemisphere, marking a rare cycle that occurs only once every 60 years, last seen in 1966. Governed by the element of Fire, this year ignites profound transformation, momentum, and rapid change—amplified by a total Solar Eclipse on the New Year itself. Like the strike of a match, Fire rises, calling individuals into their inner power and urging the release of people, patterns, and attachments that drain energy or no longer serve one’s highest good. Symbolically aligned with the Tarot archetype of movement and triumph, the Fire Horse embodies freedom, truth, beauty, passion, strength, flow, and the courage to move forward fully aligned with one’s purpose.

The Fire Horse is also a symbol of endurance and extraordinary potential, echoed in the ancient Chinese image of the Thousand-Mile Horse (Qianlima), capable of galloping vast distances with unwavering resolve. This metaphor speaks to innate talent, destiny, and the call to live up to one’s true capacity—while also reminding us that such gifts must be recognized and wisely guided. The two satchels carried by the Fire Horse traditionally signify abundance, yet here they hold deeper treasures: KT and NIE, Know Thyself and Nothing In Excess. Together, they offer the essential wisdom of this year: self-mastery, discernment, and balance. Whether young or old, like the legendary warhorse that still longs to run a Thousand Li or 250 miles, the Fire Horse year invites bold aspiration, disciplined power, and the courage to claim a long-awaited path forward.

title: 🧧 Singapore Chinese New Year 2026 | People Park Complex CNY Market | Year Of Horse🏮🐴🇸🇬
description:
🧧 Singapore Chinese New Year 2026 | People Park Complex CNY Market | Year Of Horse

People’s Park Complex Chinese New Year Market 2026 runs from 18 January to 16 February, celebrating the Year of the Horse with dazzling decorations, festive stalls, and nightly crowds in the heart of Chinatown.

🧧 Overview

Location: People’s Park Complex, Chinatown, Singapore
Dates: 18 January – 16 February 2026
Hours: 11:00 AM – 10:30 PM daily (later on weekends and CNY Eve)
Theme: Year of the Horse, with horse-inspired motifs and auspicious greetings

The market is one of the most iconic parts of Singapore’s Chinatown festivities, drawing locals and tourists alike for its vibrant atmosphere and cultural immersion.

🛍️ Shopping & Food
Festive Essentials: Shoppers can stock up on decorations, couplets, and lucky charms.
Street Food: Hawkers serve traditional bites such as tangyuan, dumplings, and roasted chestnuts.
Modern Touches: Trendy stalls offer fusion snacks and contemporary CNY merchandise, appealing to younger crowds.

📸 Why Visit
Photo Opportunities: The horse-themed installations and lantern-lit streets make for striking backdrops.
Community Spirit: The market is not just commerce—it’s a gathering space where families, friends, and visitors share festive joy.
Accessibility: Located in Chinatown, it’s easily reached by MRT (Outram Park or Chinatown stations).

⚠️ Things to Note
Crowds: Expect heavy foot traffic, especially on weekends and the eve of CNY.
Extended Festivities: While the People’s Park Complex market closes on 16 February, the broader Chinatown Festival continues until 18 March 2026, with street light-ups and cultural showcases.
Best Time to Visit: Evenings, when the lights are fully illuminated and performances are in full swing.

✨ In short, the People’s Park Complex CNY Market 2026 is a centerpiece of Singapore’s Lunar New Year celebrations—an immersive blend of tradition, shopping, food, and artistry that captures the spirit of the Year of the Horse.

title: Chinese New Year 2026 Turned This Mega City Into a Ghost Town  🇨🇳 (Where is Everyone?)
description:
#china #shenzhen 
We came to Shenzhen for Chinese New Year expecting massive crowds… but instead, the city felt completely empty. 😳

Where did everyone go?

Chinese New Year (Spring Festival) is the biggest holiday in China — millions travel across the country to reunite with their families. But seeing a megacity like Shenzhen suddenly turn quiet and almost ghost-like was something we didn’t expect at all.

In this video, we explore:

Empty streets and malls

What happens to migrant workers during Spring Festival

Why China’s busiest cities slow down overnight

What Chinese New Year really looks like on the ground

Shenzhen normally has over 17 million people — but during the holiday, many return to their hometowns. This massive migration is often called the largest annual human movement in the world.

As a YouTube couple experiencing it for the first time, we were honestly shocked.

Have you ever seen a city completely change like this during a holiday?

Let us know in the comments 👇

🎥 If you enjoy real, on-the-ground travel experiences in China, subscribe for more videos about life, culture, and unexpected moments.

title: China Celebrate the New Lunar Year 2026 Like Never Before
description:
China Celebrate the New Lunar Year 2026 in a way the world has never seen before. From massive firework displays to deep-rooted ancient rituals, China Celebrate the New Lunar Year 2026 represents unity, prosperity, and cultural dominance on a global stage. This isn’t just a holiday—it’s a powerful symbol of identity and influence.

title: 春节到底是Chinese new year还是Lunar newyear？党为什么一定要逼外国人改口？春節到底是Chinese new year還是Lunarnewyear？黨爲什麼一定要逼外國人改口
description:
#春节翻译之争 #春節翻譯之爭 #ChineseNewYear #LunarNewYear #文化霸权 #文化霸權 #历法起源 #曆法起源 #汤若望 #湯若望 #大衍历 #大衍曆 #郭守敬 #郭守敬 #文化主权 #文化主權 #纳粹圣诞节 #納粹聖誕節 #破四旧 #破四舊 #跨国维权 #跨國維權 #民族主义教育 #民族主義教育


简介：

本期节目深度拆解近年来愈演愈烈的“春节译名之争”。当“Chinese New Year”变成一种变相的政治忠诚度测试，当无数海外品牌与明星因为没加“Chinese”而遭到网络出征，这背后究竟是文化自信的觉醒，还是权力意志的无限延伸？节目通过详实的史料事实核查，揭秘中国农历背后鲜为人知的“外援史”：从唐代印度天文学家的知识产权官司，到元代阿拉伯技术的深度嵌入，再到明清欧洲传教士的系统性重构。我们质疑：如果连历法与生肖都是文明交流的产物，那么强行垄断冠名权的逻辑是否还能立得住？结尾更将对比纳粹德国对圣诞节的“政治改造”与文革时期“砸烂春节”的历史真相，带你洞察权力是如何在“砸”与“抢”之间反复横跳，将传统文化异化为宣誓主权的怪胎。

本期節目深度拆解近年來愈演愈烈的「春節譯名之爭」。當「Chinese New Year」變成一種變相的政治忠誠度測試，當無數海外品牌與明星因為沒加「Chinese」而遭到網路出征，這背後究竟是文化自信的覺醒，還是權力意志的無限延伸？節目透過詳實的史料事實核查，揭秘中國農曆背後鮮為人知的「外援史」：從唐代印度天文學家的知識產權官司，到元代阿拉伯技術的深度嵌入，再到明清歐洲傳教士的系統性重構。我們質疑：如果連曆法與生肖都是文明交流的產物，那麼強行壟斷冠名權的邏輯是否還能立得住？結尾更將對比納粹德國對聖誕節的「政治改造」與文革時期「砸爛春節」的歷史真相，帶你洞察權力是如何在「砸」與「搶」之間反覆橫跳，將傳統文化異化為宣誓主權的怪胎。

🇲🇾 CHINESE NEW YEAR 2026 🧧 Traditional Lion Dance at TRX Mall Kuala Lumpur, 4k HDR, Chinese New Year 2026 celebrations come alive in Kuala Lumpur with a vibrant traditional Lion Dance performance at TRX Mall. The lion dance symbolizes good luck, prosperity, strength, and the driving away of bad spirits, making it one of the most important and exciting traditions of Lunar New Year. Accompanied by powerful drumbeats, cymbals, and gongs, the performance fills the mall with energy, rhythm, and festive atmosphere.

During Chinese New Year, Kuala Lumpur transforms into a city-wide celebration. Red lanterns line streets and shopping malls, fireworks light up the skyline, and families gather for reunion dinners, temple visits, and cultural performances. Major areas across the city host lion and dragon dances, cultural shows, festive markets, and decorations celebrating the Lunar New Year and welcoming prosperity for the year ahead.

This video captures the excitement, crowd energy, and festive spirit of Chinese New Year 2026 in Kuala Lumpur, showcasing how modern city life blends seamlessly with centuries-old traditions. Whether you’re interested in Chinese culture, travel in Malaysia, or experiencing Lunar New Year celebrations around the world, this is a true snapshot of KL during its most colorful season.

title: Caillou and Lunar New Year | Caillou Cartoon
description:
► Click to Subscribe to CAILLOU: http://bit.ly/23vIRz4 

Visit our website! http://www.caillou.com/

Follow us for all the latest Caillou news!
► Facebook https://www.facebook.com/CaillouOfficial
► Twitter https://twitter.com/cailloudhx


Meet Caillou, the lovable 4 year old with a big imagination! Caillou experiences all the wonders of being a child that audiences around the world can relate to; first day of school, caring for a pet, learning a new sport or spending time with family.  Preschoolers will feel right at home with trusted pal Caillou, his sister Rosie and all their family and friends as they experience the everyday adventures of childhood. 

Caillou lives in a blue house at 17 Pine Street with his mother, father, and his younger sister, Rosie. He has many adventures with his family and friends and uses his imagination in every episode.

Caillou, nicknamed The Prince of Imagination, is the title character of the show.

Caillou is an average, imaginative, bald four-year-old boy with a love for forms of transportive machinery such as rocket ships and airplanes. A dreamer, Caillou is prone to frequent dream sequences in some episodes, visualizing his daydreams and hopes, and many episodes chronicle his normal daily experiences with his parents, friends, and neighbours. Caillou particularly loves his stuffed dinosaur Rexy and teddy bear Teddy, along with his pet cat Gilbert, all of whom are depicted as puppets in segments featured in the earlier episodes.

Caillou's family

Mommy – Caillou's mother. She is a busy homemaker most of the time but is seen to work in an office too. Caillou occasionally helps his mom with various chores and she often takes time to involve Caillou and his friends in activities such as crafts and baking. She is predominantly dressed in a red blouse with yellow trim, blue headband, blue ankle-length pants and blue shoes with green soles.

Daddy – Caillou's father. He and Caillou occasionally work on projects around the house. He wears a green sweater with a red trim, and blue pants. In the episode "Caillou The Chef" he says he once worked at a restaurant and made pizza.

Rosie (French name: Mousseline) – Caillou's lively younger sister who is a typical toddler. She always wants to take part in the same activities as Caillou. In later seasons of the series she becomes more talkative and independent. She wears a blue dress, red socks and blue Mary Jane shoes. Rosie sometimes fights with Caillou, but they still love each other. She appears to be the only family member with red hair.

Grandma – Caillou's paternal grandmother. Grandma is a very active adult who loves the arts and the outdoors. She passes that love on to Caillou. Grandma often comes up with creative ideas to solve Caillou's problems. She and Caillou paint and go birdwatching together.

Grandpa – Caillou's paternal grandfather. He takes Caillou on adventures, often going on walks and riding the bus throughout the town where they reside. In "Caillou's Hiding Place", he showed Caillou a hidden area inside a tree in the backyard. In the episode "Caillou Goes Camping", he and Caillou camp in the backyard. He is Daddy's father and loves to tell stories about Caillou’s Daddy when he was a little boy. He wears a blue shirt.

Caillou's friends and neighbours
Mr. Hinkle (French name: Monsieur Lajoie) – Caillou's neighbour, he has a gold tooth. In the episode "Farmer for the Day", it is said that his first name is Paul.

Leo – Leo started out as a mean boy in the 1999 episode "Caillou Goes to Day Care", but quickly befriended Caillou in the same episode. They have been inseparable since. According to Caillou's Holiday Movie, Leo is Jewish and celebrates Hanukkah. That was mentioned back in 1999 in the episode "Caillou Goes to Day Care".

Clementine – Clementine was the first to befriend Caillou in the 1999 episode "Caillou Goes to Day Care". She can get rather bossy sometimes, but all in all she is pretty understanding.

Sarah – Caillou's friend who he first met in "Caillou Goes Round the Block". She is of Chinese heritage and celebrates Chinese New Year. She has a cousin in an episode where she invites Caillou to celebrate Chinese New Year. In another she invites him to school for "Bring Your Younger Siblings to School Day" because she has no siblings. Sarah has a pet cat named Olly and a dog named Murphy.

Gilbert – Caillou's pet cat. He is the leader of the group. He has a greyish-blue body with black stripes and loathes dogs with a passion. He especially hates the bulldog in the neighbourhood. In the puppet segments of the program, Gilbert often composes odes.

Preschoolers will feel right at home with trusted pal Caillou, his sister Rosie and all their family and friends as they experience the everyday adventures of childhood.

title: Chinese New Year in Chinatown NYC | Celebrating Lunar New Year 2026
description:
In Manhattan’s Chinatown, Chinese New Year (Lunar New Year) unfolded in a cascade of red, gold, and celebration. Along Mott Street, families and friends arrived in fresh red garments — symbols of renewal and fortune — as lion dancers moved from storefront to storefront. With each rhythmic bow and leap, they chased away lingering misfortune and offered blessings for prosperity. Spectators reached forward to touch the lion’s head, slipping red envelopes into its mouth in exchange for luck in the year ahead.

The streets themselves had transformed into a festival ground — confetti blanketing the pavement, poppers dispensing confetti through the air, percussion echoing the streets. Children darted through the streets with silly string, clutching silk lion puppets, and lighting bright smoking sparklers. This walk captured not only a holiday, but a living expression of joy, culture, and community — Chinese New Year as celebrated on Mott Street in New York City’s Chinatown.


📍 
Start: Mott Street | Hester Street
End: Mott Street | Pell Street

title: Chinese New Year 2026 (24 Hours Before)… And Things You Should NEVER Do
description:
This footage was taken about 24 hours before Chinese New Year in Chinatown… and the energy was something else.

The streets were packed. Decorations everywhere. Red lanterns taking over the skyline. Markets overflowing with people making last-minute purchases before reunion dinners.

But what really stood out to me wasn’t just the crowd. It was the atmosphere.

Because as vibrant as everything looks, Chinese New Year isn’t just about celebration. It comes with intention. There are things you’re not supposed to do once the new year officially begins. The beginning of the year is treated carefully.

In this video, I walk you through what Chinatown looks like right before Chinese New Year begins. We look at the buildup, the symbolism, the crowd behavior, and the unspoken rules that shape this celebration. You can feel the anticipation in the air before everything slows down for reunion dinners and family time.

Because Chinese New Year is both a celebration and a reset.

And 24 hours before it begins… you can feel it.

If you’ve ever experienced Chinese New Year like this, I’d love to know what it felt like for you.

title: Inside Kolkata’s Chinese New Year: Lion Dances, Food & A Heritage That Lives On
description:
The Chinese New Year falls on February 17, 2026. Kolkata’s Chinatown, India’s oldest, established around 1778 by tea trader Tong Atchew, lights up for the Spring Festival, marking the Year of the Fire Horse. Kolkata’s Chinatown, India’s oldest, established around 1778 by tea trader Tong Atche,w comes alive with colour, sound, and flavour. In this video, we travel across the city to uncover the stories behind the celebrations. We explore the centuries-old Chinese temples of Tirretta Bazaar, famous street-side Chinese breakfasts, dive into Kolkata’s legendary Indo-Chinese cuisine, the sauces and flavours that define the city’s unique culinary identity, and meet the young performers who keep lion dance traditions alive. Generations of the local Chinese-Indian community share their stories, showing how heritage and celebration continue to thrive. From young performers keeping lion dance traditions alive to generations preserving Indo-Chinese culture, we uncover the stories behind the celebrations, showcasing the multicultural threads that make the City of Joy sparkle.

description:
Celebrate Lunar New Year 2026 with a festive twist on the classic Eric Carle book! 🧧✨

Join Teacher Rob on BrainSnax for a special holiday version of "Brown Bear, Brown Bear, What Do You See?" In this new song, our favorite animals are celebrating Chinese New Year in Chinatown! 🏮

🐻 In this video, you will see:

Red Bird with a lucky Red Envelope (Hong Bao) 🧧

Yellow Duck finding a Golden Ingot 💰

Blue Horse leading the 2026 New Year Celebration 🐴

Green Frog playing the Cymbals 🎵

Purple Cat wearing a traditional Red Silk Vest 👘

White Dog lighting the Firecrackers 🧨

Black Sheep holding the Lucky Money 🪙

Goldfish swimming under the Lanterns 🏮

🎨 Art Style: Enjoy beautiful, hand-crafted illustrations inspired by the tissue-paper collage style of Eric Carle, set against a cheerful Chinatown background.

🏫 Educational Value: This song is perfect for circle time, preschool, and kindergarten classrooms! It helps children learn:

Holiday Vocabulary: Red Envelope, Lanterns, Firecrackers, Year of the Horse.

Colors & Animals: Reviewing the classics with a fun twist.

Cultural Traditions: Why we use red, upside-down "Spring" characters (Chun), and loud noises to celebrate!

description:
Chinese New Year is just around the corner, and this year we celebrate the Year of the Horse!

For centuries, horses were the most important means of transport—long before engines, railways and airplanes. They played a crucial role in human history, fighting alongside warriors on battlefields and shaping civilizations. Horses have also inspired countless works of art, literature and culture around the world.

What’s your favorite fact about horses? Share it with us in the comments!

title: Chinese 2026 New Firehorse Year | 1 Hour Chinese New Year Festive Music | Lunar New Year Celebration
description:
Welcome to Chinese New Year 2026 — the Year of the Fire Horse 🐎🔥
Enjoy 1 hour of Chinese New Year festive music, created to bring joy, prosperity, and a vibrant celebratory atmosphere to your Lunar New Year celebration.

This long-play video features traditional Chinese orchestral music with erhu, guzheng, pipa, yangqin, dizi flutes, and lively ceremonial drums. The sound is festive, warm, colorful, and dynamic, designed for Chinese New Year 2026 celebrations, background ambiance, lantern festivals, family gatherings, and cultural events.

Inspired by the symbolism of the Fire Horse, this music reflects movement, happiness, renewal, and positive energy — without heroism, battle tension, or aggressive drama. It flows smoothly for a full hour, making it perfect for relaxed celebration, decorations, long listening, and festive background music.

🎶 Perfect for:

Chinese New Year 2026 celebration music

Lunar New Year background music

Year of the Fire Horse atmosphere

Festive home, café, and restaurant ambiance

Lantern Festival music

Cultural videos and streams

Relaxing yet joyful long-play music

🔥 Year of the Fire Horse 2026:

The Fire Horse symbolizes energy, freedom, optimism, and forward movement. This music captures that spirit through joyful melodies, playful rhythms, and rich traditional textures — creating a grand festive celebration soundscape for the Lunar New Year.

🎎 Let this Chinese New Year music 2026 be the soundtrack of your celebration.
Wishing you happiness, prosperity, and good fortune in the Year of the Fire Horse!

title: 2026 Spring Festival Gala: A heartwarming celebration of humanity and cultural heritage
description:
This year's Spring Festival Gala, an annual celebration produced and broadcast by China Media Group, commenced on Feb 16, the eve of the Chinese New Year, as Chinese people worldwide celebrated the Spring Festival, the most important Chinese festival centering on family reunions.

Known as "Chunwan," the gala, the first since China's Spring Festival was inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity, has not only continued its tradition as the must-watch show for Chinese families on Chinese New Year's Eve since 1983 but also reached new heights in showcasing human warmth and cultural heritage.

title: 🧧 Singapore Chinese New Year 2026 | Sengkang Central CNY Market Tour🏮🇸🇬🐴
description:
🧧 Singapore Chinese New Year 2026 | Sengkang Central CNY Market Tour

Sengkang Central Chinese New Year (CNY) Market 2026 will be a lively neighborhood bazaar filled with festive goods, street snacks, and cultural activities, creating a vibrant hub for residents to shop, celebrate, and soak in the Year of the Horse spirit.

🎉 Atmosphere & Setting

Location: Sengkang Central, a bustling residential hub in northeast Singapore, transforms into a festive corridor during CNY season.

Ambience: Lanterns, red-and-gold decorations, and traditional music set the tone, while stalls line the streets offering everything from snacks to household goods.

Community spirit: Families, friends, and neighbors gather here not just to shop, but to enjoy the festive buzz together.

🛍️ Market Highlights

Festive essentials: Pineapple tarts, bak kwa, love letters, and other traditional treats are widely available.

Decorations: Red couplets, lanterns, zodiac-themed ornaments (Year of the Horse), and auspicious plants like puzzy willow and mandarin orange trees.

Fashion & gifts: Cheongsams, festive outfits, and small gift items perfect for visiting relatives.

Street food: Satay, fried snacks, bubble tea, and local delights add to the night market vibe.

📅 Timing & Practical Notes

Dates: Typically runs for several weeks leading up to Chinese New Year (late January to early February 2026).
Operating hours: Evening till late night, echoing the pasar malam tradition.
Accessibility: Conveniently located near Sengkang MRT and bus interchange, making it easy for residents and visitors to drop by.

Sengkang Central CNY Market 2026 is more than just shopping—it’s a community celebration of heritage, prosperity, and togetherness. For residents, it’s a chance to stock up on festive goods without heading downtown, while for visitors, it offers a glimpse into Singapore’s neighborhood traditions during one of the most important cultural festivals of the year.

title: 4K·HDR｜Chongqing 2026 New Year Countdown: 8000 Drones, Lasers & Light Show
description:
Happy New Year 2026 from Chongqing, China! 🎆

In this video, I captured the spectacular New Year's Eve celebration in Chongqing. The scale was massive, featuring river boat lasers, water curtain projections, and a record-breaking fleet of **8000 drones** lighting up the sky!

📍 **A quick note:**
The crowds in Chongqing tonight were absolutely insane! Because there were so many people, I couldn't fight my way to the absolute best vantage point, so some parts of the video might not have the "perfect" angle. I appreciate your understanding!

🚀 **Please watch until the end!**
The **8000-drone finale** was absolutely breathtaking. The countdown and the visuals in the sky were shocking. I hope you enjoy this festive atmosphere!

If you enjoyed this video, please like and subscribe for more walking tours and vlogs.

title: America LOST: Unitree's Chinese New Year Gala Robots Performance PROVES IT!
description:
The AI war is over—and America lost. Watch as we break down China's 2026 Spring Festival Gala robot performance and expose why the US containment strategy has completely failed. The data is undeniable: China shipped 10x more humanoid robots than America in 2025, and their robots are already working in factories while ours are still in labs.

In this video, we react to Unitree's WuBOTs performing unprecedented kung fu routines—3-meter backflips, weapon handling, and synchronized formations at 4 m/s—and analyze what this really means for the future of AI and robotics. We also address the critics who dismissed last year's performance and show why 2026 proves them wrong.

Key Topics Covered:

•Unitree G1 and H1 humanoid robot performance at 2026 Chinese New Year Gala
•Market dominance: 10x more robots shipped by China vs. US in 2025
•Price comparison: $16,000 Unitree G1 vs. $100,000+ Figure AI
•Real-world deployment in NIO, BYD, and Geely factories
•How US chip sanctions backfired and China pivoted to open-source AI
•Why robotics, not chatbots, is the real AI race
•Alibaba's Qwen overtaking Meta's Llama globally
•The failed containment strategy and what it means for the future

description:
#alone #lifestyle #vlog #travel #china #newyear #solitude #chinesefood 

Hi, I’m Zaza.Today, I’m finally heading back to my hometown to get ready for Chinese New Year.

This journey takes me from a big city in southern China to a small county town in the north —a 1,500 km trip that lasts over 12 hours.

Along the way, there are unexpected moments, small surprises,and a strong contrast between different places and lifestyles.

I hope you enjoy this journey home.

title: The Legend of Nian 年兽的传说: How Chinese New Year Traditions Began | Whispered Bedtime Tales
description:
Dive into the thrilling tale of “The Story of Nian,” a classic Chinese New Year legend! 🌟

Long ago, a ferocious beast named Nian terrorized a small village, emerging once a year to wreak havoc. But one brave traveler and a clever old woman discovered the secret to driving the monster away using firecrackers, the color red, and loud noises. This captivating story reveals the origins of beloved Chinese New Year traditions, from hanging red lanterns to setting off dazzling fireworks.

🎆 Learn how courage, kindness, and unity saved the villagers and brought prosperity to their lives. Perfect for the whole family, this heartwarming tale is brought to life with vibrant visuals and a festive spirit.

Seven days to go before the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics open on Feb 4! State-level musicians from the China National Opera & Dance Drama Theater and figure skaters are here to celebrate the Beijing Winter Games and the Year of the Tiger with our Spring Festival special performance "Battle Drums", in Beijing Wtown (Simatai Great Wall).   This particular performance lion dance, which requires immense balance and coordination. Watch as a group of vibrant and colorful lions queue up for their grand entrance 2.10! This spectacular scene showcases the variety of traditional lion designs and the incredible energy they bring to the celebration at Lalaport.
 In Chinese culture, the lion symbolizes power, wisdom, and good fortune. The dance is performed to chase away evil spirits and bring prosperity for the new year. I hope this video gives you a closer look at this magnificent tradition!
If you enjoyed this performance, please like, comment, and subscribe for more cultural highlights and original recordings

title: Lion Dance in Singapore 🦁🥁 | Chinese New Year Celebration 2026
description:
The streets came alive during Chinese New Year celebrations in Singapore with an exciting Lion Dance performance 🧧

The Lion Dance is a traditional performance believed to bring good luck, prosperity, and ward off bad spirits for the new year. Accompanied by powerful drums and cymbals, every jump and movement symbolizes strength, fortune, and happiness.

This is one of the most iconic parts of Lunar New Year festivities across Singapore — from Chinatown to community celebrations — and seeing it live is unforgettable. From the acrobatic high-pole (Meihuaquan) stunts to the traditional Cai Qing (Plucking the Greens), the energy in Chinatown and the local malls is unmatched.

The precision, strength, and coordination required for these gravity-defying jumps are absolutely insane. In 2026, the performances are even more high-octane to match the "Fire Horse" spirit. This clip captures the iconic "Cai Qing" (Plucking of Greens) ritual—stay till the end to see the lion's big reveal!

💬 Question:
Did the lion make you jump too? 😄
Have you ever seen a Lion Dance in person?

description:
#SingaporeChineseNewYear #LionDance #CNY2026
Singapore Chinese New Year 2026 lion dance at NEX, Serangoon.

The performance opens with eight lions, including a rare appearance by two Northern lions, followed by traditional war drums. Two colourful LED lions later take centre stage alongside a lively mascot segment. The programme continues with a single lion high pole act and concludes with a vibrant twin dragon dance finale.

Location: NEX, Serangoon
Event: Singapore Chinese New Year 2026

If you enjoy lion dance performances in Singapore, consider subscribing for more festive season coverage.

Video recorded with DJI Pocket 3 on 23 February 2026 at NEX, Serangoon Central.

title: World Premiere: Hong Kong Orchestral Manoeuvres for Chinese New Year (Feb 2021)
description:
This rousing CNY performance from the flagship Hong Kong Chinese Orchestra is not to be missed: to ward off evil spirits; to clear the way for good fortune to come home; and to usher in a prosperous Year of the Ox with abundant blessings! From today (Feb 9), this 4-minute Musical Red Couplets, featuring 80 musicians, 32 types of instruments and two dancing lions, is sure to bring an upbeat mood at this festive time of the year.

What to look out for:

Bianzhong (bronze bells) - rarely seen percussion set of bronze bells (weighing about 1.6 tonnes);

Jiangu (big drum) - a massive drum from Jiangzhou of Shanxi province (1.3 metres in diameter and 1 metre deep); and

Eco-huqins, developed by the Hong Kong Chinese Orchestra

title: 🦁 Ion Orchard Road Prosperity Lion Dance | Chinese New Year 2026🇸🇬🧧
description:
🦁 Ion Orchard Road Prosperity Lion Dance | Chinese New Year 2026

ION Orchard is bringing festive energy to Singapore’s premier shopping boulevard this Lunar New Year with a series of lion dance performances and cultural showcases. Here’s what’s happening:
Key Highlights

Dates & Times:
Lion Dance shows on 17, 18, 21, 22, 23, 28 February & 1 March 2026
Performances start at 3:08 PM

Venue:
ION² (Level 1 Outdoor Plaza) – the open-air space at ION Orchard, perfect for large festive gatherings

Performers:
The renowned Wen Yang Lion Dance Troupe will headline, known for their precision, thunderous drum rhythms, and acrobatic flair

Cultural Add-ons:
Calligraphy sessions at the B1 Concierge Counter (13–19 February 2026) let visitors take home auspicious blessings written in traditional brushwork

Atmosphere & Significance
The lion dance is more than spectacle—it’s a ritual believed to ward off bad luck and invite prosperity.
At ION Orchard, the combination of luxury retail and traditional performance creates a striking contrast: modern city life infused with heritage.
Expect crowds, vibrant energy, and symbolic gestures like “plucking the greens” (cai qing), where the lion retrieves lettuce to symbolize wealth and growth.

Why It Stands Out
ION Orchard’s lion dance isn’t just another mall performance—it’s staged at one of Singapore’s most iconic retail landmarks, blending heritage artistry with cosmopolitan flair. The outdoor plaza setting makes it feel communal, while the troupe’s reputation ensures a high-energy, authentic display.

title: 🐊 🦂 🐢 Chinese New Year Market in Guangzhou: From Traditional Goods to Unique Ingredients
description:
Hello everyone, I'm Alin 🤗
This episode of our culinary journey takes us to Guangzhou to explore local traditional markets.

What's particularly special is encountering unique ingredients like alligator meat, turtles, scorpions, and starfish.
These ingredients are traditionally believed to have nourishing properties and are often used in slow-cooked soups or medicinal dishes.

With the Chinese New Year approaching, follow my lens to soak up the festive atmosphere and see what New Year's goods Chinese families are preparing.

This year, my aunt said she wanted to cook with me, so our family started a "one person, one dish" New Year's Eve dinner. Everyone made their favourite signature dish... With so many dishes, we decided to arrange them into a Peacock Feast. In Yunnan, people often use peacocks to symbolize beauty and good things. The Peacock Feast carries the meaning of having a beautifully prepared meal for the New Year.

project_name_en: Spring Festival, social practices of the Chinese people in celebration of traditional new year
title: This is what LEGO Lunar New Year sets have been lacking...
description:
LEGO 80121 Ancient Moon-Gazing Inn and 80120 Prosperity Carp Leaping are the latest LEGO sets aimed deliberately at the Chinese market with fundamentally Chinese subject matter - in this case, a historical play diorama and a display piece centred around an ancient myth respectively.

The past couple of years have seen Lunar New Years, ostensibly part of the same sino-focused subtheme, that have failed to deliver on play, and featured central gimmicks that don't necessarily feel like the best use of the LEGO System. Thankfully, these two new sets feel like a direct response to this criticism, and one can only hope that designers of other themes - such as Pokémon - pay attention.

These sets were provided for review by the LEGO Group.

Curious about how to brew different types of Chinese teas using the traditional Gongfu brewing method? In this video, we take you through an exciting journey as we try Jesse's Gongfu Tea Sampler and brew some of the most iconic Chinese teas, including Longjing (Dragon Well tea), Keemun black tea, Gongmei white tea, Shou Puerh, Jasmine Puerh, Gushu Sheng Puerh, Tie Guanyin (Iron Goddess of Mercy), and Shou Mei. Whether you're new to Gongfu Cha or looking for a tutorial on perfecting your technique, this video has you covered!

We’ll guide you step by step through the Gongfu tea brewing process and the Chinese tea ceremony, teaching you the art of this ancient ceremony that brings out the rich flavors and aromas in each type of tea. From brewing oolong in Gongfu style to preparing delicate white and aged Puerh teas, you’ll learn how to enhance your tea experience at home.

☯️ In this tutorial, you'll learn:

How to master the Gongfu tea brewing technique
The specific brewing methods for Longjing (Dragon Well tea) and Keemun black tea
How to brew Gongmei white tea, ripe (shou) Puerh, and Jasmine Puerh
Unlocking the complexity of Gushu Sheng Puerh and the earthy notes of Tie Guanyin (Iron Goddess tea)
The secret to perfectly brewing Shou Mei and other gongfu teas

📦 Watch the full video to discover the deep history, unique flavors, and proper techniques behind these incredible Chinese teas using Gongfu Cha, a traditional brewing style that elevates the experience with every sip.

title: How To Plan A Chinese Tea Ceremony
description:
In Chinese culture, newlyweds often include a traditional Chinese tea ceremony, called “敬茶” (ging3 caa4) in Chinese, as a part of their wedding day festivities. The Chinese tea ceremony is a way for you to show gratitude to your parents and elders while addressing them by their new titles for the first time. This also gives the elders an opportunity to personally bless your marriage and shower you with gifts.

If you’re thinking of including a Chinese tea ceremony on your wedding day, we’ve put together this video with a list of everything you need to know.

www.norrisfilms.com
https://www.norrisfilms.com/blog/chinese-tea-ceremony/

title: Hong Kong teamaker revives ancient Chinese tea ceremony
description:
Subscribe to our YouTube channel for free here: 
https://sc.mp/subscribe-youtube

Janice Zhu, a co-founder and tutor of the Hong Kong tea shop Yuentea, holds regular public classes on performing a Chinese tea ceremony. The process includes a special technique for rapidly whipping tea after water is added and how to paint on its surface in a manner reminiscent of the Japanese practice of chado, which has a surprising resemblance to modern latte art. The ancient tea ceremony known as dian cha was originally established in China during the Song dynasty. Dian cha requires significantly more time and effort than modern tea brewing methods and involves complex cultural traditions.

title: How to Brew Chinese Tea the Right Way
description:
How do you make tea? Chances are you’ve been doing it wrong.

For many people in China, brewing tea is a ritual akin to meditation. And there is a proper ceremony called gongfu cha.

Gongfu means skill, and cha means tea. It’s a form of Chinese tea service that dates back to the 14th century in Fujian. It places emphasis on the tea’s taste, temperature, and quality.

In this video, we’ll run you through how to do the tea ceremony correctly.

title: [ASMR] Traditional Chinese Tea Ceremony
description:
Sound design & Foley while having Chinese Tea Ceremony using Audio Technica at4053b recorded into Zoom f8n Pro.

Traditional Chinese Tea Ceremony popularized in the Tang dynasty has a long history and culture that ties to its spiritual and nourishing elements. 

Many credit Shen Nong as the first person to discover the effects of tea and spread the tea culture. 

There are many forms of Chinese tea ceremony. Here, I am practicing what my grandma has taught me and referred to it as Lao Ren Cha 老人茶 (old person's tea). It is now more commonly referred to as Gong Fu Tea.

For me, there's always a certain calmness and enjoyment in brewing Lao Ren Cha. The process lets the tea brew to its fullness and allows the full potential of tea tasting 品茶.

Different type of tea have different brewing process. Here, I am brewing Alishan tea, which is a type of Oolong tea, from Taiwan.


Filmed on Panasonic GH5s with Canon FD 50mm f1.2, Canon FD 24mm f2.8

title: Gongfu Cha with Yixing Teapot
description:
The Chinese Kung Fu Tea Ceremony, or Gongfu Cha, is an ancient Chinese functional art form. The exact origins of the Chinese Tea Ceremony are unknown, but the Chaoshan/Chaozhou region is credited with making it a part of daily life. There are officially 21 steps to the process, though following the steps is more of a meditation than a practical way to serve tea.

You can see a step-by-step explanation of the ceremony here:

https://cainatea.com/gongfu-cha/

The name of the ceremony is a play on the words Kung Fu, based on the methodical steps that are required to properly perform the art.

Unlike the more recent Japanese Tea Ceremony, the Chinese Gongfu Tea Ceremony focuses not on the ceremony itself, but on the proper preparation of tea.

It requires a certain skill and discipline to carry out each purposeful and measured movement so that participants not only remain present, but are also able to fully appreciate the complex tastes and aromas.

Cara, the founder of Altitude Tea in Sydney, has been practicing and perfecting the traditional tea ceremony using a Gongfu set for several years, transforming the 21-step process into a fine art. description:
🍵 Shop gaiwans, teapots and everything for your first Gongfu Cha session: Moychay Netherlands (Main Shop): http://moychay.nl

Gongfu Cha is the traditional Chinese tea ceremony that turns ordinary leaves into an extraordinary tasting experience through multiple short infusions. In this guide, tea expert Sergey Shevelev walks you through every step of the Gongfu brewing process — from choosing the right gaiwan and water temperature to dialing in steeping times for oolong, puer and green tea. By the end you'll understand why short, repeated infusions reveal flavors a single long steep never will, and how to run a calm, confident session at home.

After our initial zoom call back in 2022, I've been counting down the days for Pyin & Stephen's wedding and it lived up to all expectations. The day began with Stephen having to complete the most ridiculous tasks set by the bridal party in order to see Pyin, which he took in his stride and absolutely smashed. This was followed by a traditional Chinese tea ceremony, prior to the wedding ceremony, which was held at Stephens local church and officiated by a close friend. All the guests then hopped on board a privately hired classic double decker bus, travelling through London back to the beautiful Morden Hall where the celebrations carried on late into the night.
Thank you for being such a fun couple to work with!

title: Tips for a tea ceremony on your wedding怎樣準備敬茶儀式——茶禮（中式婚禮的重要儀式）
description:
[Overseas Chinese Wedding]Tips for a tea ceremony on your wedding.HOW To Have A Tea Ceremony On Your wedding |Give red envelopes or receive red envelopes?

Copyright @veri.com.au
 All Rights Reserved.No part of this video may be reproduced in any form or means.If you want to watch the actual guest film, please visit:  www.veri.com.au

The wedding ceremony of overseas Chinese still retains the tea ceremony. Toasting tea is the most important ceremony in a Chinese wedding. By offering tea, the newlyweds pay their respects to their parents and thank them for their kindness in raising their other half. At the same time, the parents also sent good wishes to the newlyweds, wishing them a happy marriage and a long life together.
When serving tea, you should serve it with both hands, not with one hand. In addition to tea leaves, two red dates and lotus seeds should be placed in each tea serving, which means that you will have a good son early, and many sons will be blessed. And both red dates and lotus seeds must be complete.
The tea ceremony is performed twice, once when the groom goes to the bride's house to pick up the relatives, he will serve tea to his parents-in-law. Once when the bride was brought back to her husband's house, she served tea to her husband's family.
Before the tea is served, the newlyweds kneel in front of their parents according to the male left and the female right. After receiving the tea, the groom will serve tea first, and then the bride will serve tea.
After the tea is served, the parents will prepare a red envelope for the newlyweds, and the newcomers have to take it with both hands.
After that, the newlyweds have to serve tea to their grandparents, uncles, aunts, uncles and aunts, older brothers and sisters, and cousins, cousins, and cousins.
After the bridegroom picks up the marriage, when he arrives at the groom's house, he will also serve tea to the groom's parents, grandparents, uncles, aunts, uncles and aunts, elder brothers and sisters, cousins, cousins, and cousins ​​in the same order as above.

title: The Art of the Chinese Tea Ceremony: A Journey of Harmony and Tradition
description:
Tea is more than just a drink in China—it’s a cultural ritual that embodies harmony, respect, and mindfulness. The Chinese tea ceremony, known as "Gongfu Cha" (功夫茶), is a beautiful practice that transforms tea preparation into an art form.

Why the Chinese Tea Ceremony Matters
Unlike casual tea drinking, Gongfu Cha is about:
✔ Precision – Mastering water temperature, timing, and technique
✔ Appreciation – Savoring the tea’s aroma, flavor, and aftertaste
✔ Connection – Sharing tea as a gesture of hospitality and friendship

Essential Tools for Gongfu Cha
1. Teapot/Gaiwan – For brewing with control
2. Fairness Cup (Cha Hai) – Ensures even flavor distribution
3. Small Cups – To concentrate aroma and taste
4. Tea Tray – Catches spills during pouring
5. Tea Tools – Tweezers, scoop, and pick for handling leaves

Step-by-Step Tea Ceremony
1. Warm the Teaware – Rinse cups & pot with hot water.
2. Measure the Leaves – Use more leaves than Western brewing (5-8g per 100ml).
3. Rinse the Tea – A quick wash removes dust and awakens the leaves.
4. First Infusion – Steep for 5-30 seconds (varies by tea type).
5. Serve with Grace – Pour evenly into cups, ensuring each guest gets the same flavor.
6. Savor Slowly – Smell, sip, and enjoy the evolving taste over multiple brews.

Types of Tea Used in Ceremonies
✅Oolong (e.g., Tieguanyin, Da Hong Pao) – Complex, floral, and perfect for Gongfu Cha
✅Pu’er (Ripe or Raw) – Deep, earthy, and improves with age
✅Green Tea (e.g., Longjing) – Delicate, requiring lower temperatures

The Philosophy Behind the Ritual
The ceremony reflects Taoist and Confucian principles:
🌿 Balance (Yin & Yang) – Heat, time, and tea in harmony
🍵 Respect – Serving elders first as a sign of courtesy
☯ Mindfulness – Being present in each step

Experience It Yourself!
Want to try Gongfu Cha? Start with:
✅A simple Gaiwan set/Teapot
✅High-quality loose-leaf tea
✅Patience and curiosity

title: What Is the Chinese Tea Ceremony (Gongfu Cha)?
description:
Gongfu Cha is a method of brewing tea that originated in Fujian and Guangdong provinces during the Song and Ming dynasties. The phrase "gongfu" refers to effort, precision, and attention to detail—qualities that are essential to making good tea.

Unlike casual tea drinking, Gongfu Cha is ritualistic and uses small teaware, multiple infusions, and carefully timed steps to extract the full character of the tea leaves. It’s typically used for oolong, pu-erh, and fine black teas, which flourish under this method.#teapot #handmade #gongfutea #tealover #clayart #teatime #teaculture #collection #craftsmanship #traditional

Discover the ancient Chinese tea ceremony, Gong Fu Cha, filmed at the Buddhist Temple in Wuyi Mountains! 🍵 This exclusive film clip reveals the birthplace of this sacred meditation practice, where Buddhist monks have practiced mindful tea rituals since the Ming dynasty. ABOUT TEA: THE DRINK THAT CHANGED THE WORLD | 90-Minute Documentary

This breathtaking feature-length Documentary takes you on an epic journey through tea's incredible history – from its mystical Chinese origins in the legendary Wuyi Mountains to its mesmerizing global impact, immortalizing the celestial kingdom, toppling empires, and sparking revolutions that transformed cultures, history, and trade over four hundred years.

Shot in China's Forbidden Tea Regions of the Wuyi Mountains—the sacred birthplace of Oolong and Lapsang Souchong Black tea—and historic Boston Harbor, this cinematic masterpiece reveals how one simple beverage ignited the American Revolution, fueled the British Empire for over 300 years, and became humanity's most cherished drink after water.

Featuring exclusive, never-before-seen interviews with 11th and 12th-generation Oolong tea masters, you'll witness traditional Oolong tea-making techniques invented by the Buddhist Temple where Oolong tea was created during the Qing Dynasty in the mid-1600s in the Wuyi Mountains. You'll also hear exclusive interviews with a 24th-generation Black tea master, Chairman of the legendary Lapsang Souchong Company, recounting his family's invention of the world's first Black Tea. This historical tea captivated hearts in Europe, and brewed a profound impact, culminating in history's most audacious tea heist—forever transplanting tea from China to India.

title: Do Tai Chi to Improve Balance Every Day | Gentle Stretches
description:
Do Tai Chi to Improve Balance Every Day. These Gentle Stretches in 10 minutes can be done seated or standing as a standalone routine or as a warm-up before your workouts. The slow movements of Tai Chi engage your muscles and improve coordination, balance and flexibility, while also relieving stress and anxiety. 

Tai Chi, an ancient Chinese martial art that combines gentle physical movement, breath control, and meditation. Tai Chi is a weight-bearing exercise that engages various muscle groups which is essential for daily activities and overall mobility.

This stretching session is versatile. You can do these exercises every day – it can be used as a warm up or cooldown to any yes2next workout or a gentle workout by itself.

title: Tai Chi Walking to Improve Balance & Prevent Falls | Seniors 50+
description:
Tai Chi Walking to Improve Balance & Prevent Falls | Seniors 50+ is a gentle walking workout designed to help improve balance, coordination, stability, and confidence while moving. This Tai Chi-inspired walking workout combines mindful movement, forward and backward walking, weight shifting, and coordination training to help teach your brain and body to work together. The simple movements in gentle joint workout can help improve balance, strengthen the legs and hips, support better posture, and increase body awareness for everyday activities.

If you’re looking for balance exercises for seniors, fall prevention exercises, or a walking workout for seniors, this routine is an excellent place to start. The movements are low impact, joint-friendly, and easy to follow, making them suitable for beginners, older adults, and anyone looking to improve mobility and healthy aging.

Throughout the workout, you’ll practice gentle Tai Chi-inspired movements that help build coordination, stability, and confidence while walking. Regular practice may help you feel stronger, steadier on your feet, and more comfortable with everyday movement.

✅ Improve balance and stability
✅ Strengthen legs and hips
✅ Enhance coordination and body awareness
✅ Support mobility and healthy aging
✅ Increase confidence while walking
✅ Gentle, low-impact exercise for beginners

For safety, stay close to a wall, countertop, sturdy chair, or other support if needed, especially during the backward walking portions of the workout.

This workout is ideal for:
• Seniors over 50, 60, and 70
• Beginner exercisers
• Adults looking to improve balance
• Anyone interested in Tai Chi-inspired movement
• Those seeking gentle fall prevention exercises
• People looking for low-impact walking workouts

Practice regularly to help improve balance, mobility, coordination, and confidence in your daily life.

title: Tai Chi Walking for Balance: Step-by-Step Beginner Tutorial by Dr Paul Lam
description:
Learn *Tai Chi Walking with Dr Paul Lam,* creator of the Tai Chi for Health programs. This foundational movement from the *Tai Chi for Beginners* program teaches mindful weight transfer and balance through slow, deliberate stepping.

*What is Tai Chi Walking?*

Tai Chi Walking is walking with mindfulness of Tai Chi principles: mindful weight transfer, maintaining balance, upright posture, and awareness. Instead of using momentum, you shift your weight to one leg and establish good balance before moving the other foot. As Dr Lam explains: 

_"Tai Chi Walking is walking with mindfulness of Tai Chi principles: mindful weight transfer, maintaining balance, upright posture, and awareness."_

*What will I learn in this video?*

This video covers forward walking (Movement One: Stepping Forward). You'll learn the complete technique including proper knee alignment, the weight shift sequence, adding the turn, and how to practice both left and right sides in a flowing sequence. Backward walking is taught in the full Tai Chi for Beginners program.

0:00 Introduction and full demonstration
1:08 Movement One: Stepping Forward explained
2:48 Proper knee alignment
3:55 Building strength gradually
5:02 Adding the turn
6:30 Why balance matters (physical and mental)
8:01 Right side practice
9:11 Flowing sequence: left and right together
10:29 Practice with Dr Lam

*Who is this video for?*

Anyone wanting to improve balance and body awareness. No prior Tai Chi experience needed. This is Movement One: Stepping Forward from the Tai Chi for Beginners program.

*Is Tai Chi Walking good for balance?*

Yes. Research shows Tai Chi reduces recurring falls by nearly 70% and can reduce arthritis pain by 35%. While Tai Chi Walking on its own offers real benefits, complete Tai Chi sets provide deeper health benefits because they train the whole body and mind together. Think of this as a foundation for the full Tai Chi for Beginners program.

➡️ Go deeper with the Internal Aspects of Tai Chi Walking: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OqSXFZ6BxyE

➡️ Learn Tai Chi Walking Backwards: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=j6n-Lt8iCfg

➡️ *Read the complete guide to Tai Chi Walking* with detailed mechanics, benefits, and how to get started: https://taichiforhealthinstitute.org/tai-chi-walking?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=video&utm_campaign=tai-chi-walking-7KiS1icfK6E&utm_content=description

➡️ *Learn the full Tai Chi for Beginners Program* at https://www.onlinetaichilessons.com/tai-chi-for-beginners?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=video&utm_campaign=tai-chi-walking-7KiS1icfK6E&utm_content=description

➡️ *Learn Tai Chi for Arthritis and Fall Prevention* (CDC-recommended) at https://www.onlinetaichilessons.com/tai-chi-for-arthritis?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=video&utm_campaign=tai-chi-walking-7KiS1icfK6E&utm_content=description

*About Dr Paul Lam*
Dr Paul Lam is a retired family physician, tai chi expert, and founder of the Tai Chi for Health Institute. He created the Tai Chi for Health programs, practised by millions of people worldwide. His programs are supported by the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), and health departments and arthritis foundations around the world.

title: Tai Chi for Over 50 | Improve Balance, Strength & Flexibility
description:
Unlock the power of Tai Chi for everyone over 50 and discover how this ancient practice can transform your modern approach to healthy aging, including for seniors. In this session designed specifically for Tai Chi over 50, we focus on gentle exercises that prioritize balance, coordination, and joint health. Whether you are a total beginner or looking to refine your form, these slow, intentional movements provide a safe and effective way to build functional strength while reducing the risk of falls.

This moving meditation is more than just a workout; it is a tool for mental clarity and stress relief. By integrating deep breathing with fluid motion, you will learn to soothe the nervous system and cultivate mindful movement that carries over into your daily life. Join our community of active seniors as we practice this sustainable home workout to improve longevity and reclaim your vitality from the comfort of your own living room.Join BodyWisdom for this complete 15-minute Tai Chi routine, perfect for beginners and anyone over 50 looking to improve balance, stability, strength, and flexibility.

This routine focuses on gently mobilizing the major joints—from your ankles to your neck—to prepare your body for movement and promote long-term joint health. This is an excellent sequence to do before any Tai Chi practice or as a gentle morning routine.

title: Tai Chi for Beginners Video | Dr Paul Lam | Free Lesson and Introduction
description:
Learn Tai Chi from the beginning with *Dr Paul Lam* . This is the free first lesson from the *Tai Chi for Beginners* program, including introduction, how to use the program, staying safe, warm-up exercises, your first movements, and cool-down.

*What is Tai Chi for Beginners?*

Tai Chi for Beginners is Dr Paul Lam's program suitable for beginners, consisting of fluid, gentle and relaxed movements suitable for anyone and can be practised almost anywhere. It includes the Six Easy Steps, including four Qigong exercises, five foundational movements (including Tai Chi Walking), and the Tai Chi for Beginners set based on the 24 Forms, the world's most popular tai chi set. Starting with warm-up exercises, Dr Lam guides you steadily through each step as though you were in his class. This program improves muscular strength, flexibility, balance, and confidence.

*What will I learn in this free lesson?*

0:00 Introduction
0:39 How to use this Program
4:33 Introduction
4:37 Why Tai Chi for Beginners?
5:35 What is Tai Chi?
8:57 Welcome
10:59 Warm Up Exercises
22:47 Learn New Forms
36:47 Run Through Lesson One
38:19 Cool Down Exercises
39:42 Message

*Who is this program for?*

Tai Chi for Beginners is suitable for all ages and fitness levels. You can practise seated or standing, and adjust the depth of your movements to what feels comfortable.

*Is Tai Chi good for health?*

Yes. Medical studies have shown that tai chi improves muscular strength, flexibility, balance, and mental wellbeing. Dr Lam's Tai Chi for Health programs are supported by the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), and health departments and arthritis foundations around the world.

*Continue your Tai Chi journey*

*About Dr Paul Lam*
Dr Paul Lam is a retired family physician, tai chi expert, and founder of the Tai Chi for Health Institute. He created the Tai Chi for Health programs, practised by millions of people worldwide. His programs are supported by the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), and health departments and arthritis foundations around the world.

title: Taichi 4 basic moves
description:
Specific exercise improve symptoms, daily full-body exercises remove root causes.

https://taichizidong.com/products/daily-routine-1


Taichi 4 basic moves
1. 掤 peng(Warding off)
2. 挼 Lv(Rolling back)
3. 挤 Ji(Pressing)
4. 按 An(Pushing)


These are four of the eight most basic movements of Tai Chi, which express the four ways of using power. 
Peng is the outward spread and attack of power; 
Lv is the retraction and defense of force; 
Ji is the concentration of power in a point and burst; 
An is the absorption and conversion of force.

title: Seated Tai Chi For Seniors with David-Dorian Ross
description:
There are lots of reasons for Seniors to do Seated #TaiChi. My two favorites are: 1) you can literally do it anywhere that you can find a chair, and 2) now you never have an excuse for NOT getting in a Tai Chi session every day!  In this 10-minute seated Tai Chi for Seniors workout, I visit the local park - where (conveniently) there are a bunch of lovely benches to to some Tai Chi on. Notice that you can still do all the basic motions of standing Tai Chi. Your weight shifts from side to side; you twist the waist and hips - and of course you don't even need to adapt anything about the hands, arms and eyes.

Ready to continue your TaijiFit education? Go here: https://bit.ly/445lCQI

description:
Struggling with energy, cravings, or stress weight? 

You’re not alone.

Most people think weight loss is only about dieting and workouts. 

But stress (and the cortisol it creates) plays a huge role. When cortisol is high, your body holds onto weight, cravings spike, and energy crashes.

These Tai Chi & Qigong-inspired routines help:
✨ Reduce stress & lower cortisol
✨ Release tension stored in the body
✨ Improve energy for daily movement & workouts
✨ Support hormone balance naturally

It’s not a magic pill. But practiced consistently, this type of movement helps shift your body toward balance, health, and sustainable weight loss.

☯️ Who Am I:
“Student Always, Teacher Sometimes.” 
This mindset sets the tone for my lifelong journey in Chinese internal arts. I am a Tai Chi/Qigong practitioner and teacher with over 15 years of dedicated study, beginning in Southern California with Praying Mantis Kung Fu and Yang Style Tai Chi, and later continuing in Seattle under Andrew T. Dale, where I immersed myself in Chen Style Tai Chi, Bagua Zhang, and the internal principles that still guide my practice today. In 2007, I moved to Beijing, China, spending eight years training intensively with masters Zhang Wei Dong and Liu Zu Guang, refining my Tai Chi, Bagua Zhang, internal body mechanics, and traditional training methods while also studying Shuai Jiao and Da Bei Quan. During this time, I became the first foreigner awarded a Shuai Jiao Coach’s Certificate in China and co-founded the Guang Wu Shuai Jiao Association to help spread traditional Chinese martial arts internationally. My teaching emphasizes authentic Tai Chi and Qigong—rooted in structure, breath, intention, and internal alignment—to support sustainable practice, real skill development, and long-term vitality.

title: Tai Chi Walking Tutorial | How To Tai Chi Walk For Beginners (2025)
description:
Follow this Tai Chi Walking tutorial for beginners for balance, coordination and leg strength.

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Learn the art of Tai Chi Walking in this easy step-by-step tutorial designed for beginners. You’ll discover how to move with balance, coordination, and mindfulness while strengthening your body and calming your mind. Perfect for anyone interested in Tai Chi for wellness, stress relief, or improving overall mobility in 2025.

In this Tai Chi Walking lesson, you’ll first learn why it works — the science behind how slow, mindful stepping stimulates bone strength, balance, and coordination through gentle mechanical stress.

Then we’ll break it down into three clear levels:
• Level 1 – Basic Tai Chi Walking: Master the bow stance and cat stance with slow weight shifting to build grounding and stability.
• Level 2 – Walking with the Push: Add circular hand movements to engage the upper body, improve posture, and open the chest.
• Level 3 – Skip The Cat Stance: Combine the steps and movements into a continuous, meditative flow for balance, mindfulness, and full-body harmony.

By the end, you’ll feel lighter, more centered, and confident practicing Tai Chi Walking anywhere — indoors or outdoors — as a daily moving meditation to enhance balance, mobility, and emotional calm.

title: The Truth About Tai Chi Walking for Weight Loss — Science Exposes the Scam
description:
Learn Tai Chi Walking for FREE: https://www.annswansonwellness.com/walk
You've seen the ads: "Lose 25 pounds of belly fat in 7 minutes a day with Tai Chi walking!" But is this a Tai Chi scam — or is there real science behind Tai Chi Walking for weight loss?

I'm Ann Swanson — certified Tai Chi for Health instructor, Qigong teacher, and science author of books sold in 15+ languages. My original Tai Chi walking video, with 5 million+ views, helped spark this online trend. Now that the internet is flooded with misleading weight loss apps, fake AI "teachers," and before-and-after photos that defy physics, I feel responsible to set the record straight with science.

title: Tai Chi for Beginners: 10-Minute Flow to Release & Renew
description:
Before you looked at this video, you may have heard that Tai Chi is relaxing, that it helps you release tension. And you are right that it is all of that. To the extent that you considered this journey of release, it means that a part of you knows that underneath the tension you feel, under the burden you carry, the thoughts and feelings you had held, that you are more than any of that. And you are. 

Because like every baby, you are born pure, beautiful, with an infinite potential before you. And while life may have distracted you from any of this, and while a busy part of you may even have forgotten this, another part of you still knows this deeply and completely. 

So let the part of you that remembers your truth, guide you now, along this practice, to release, to renew, to rediscover your connection to your deepest truth. Because when the wisdom and strength you have built throughout your life, reconnects with the truth and meaning you have always held inside, who will you be, that is more? title: Tai Chi for Beginners: 5 Movements for a Full Mind-Body Reset
description:
👇Click the link to join our 7-Day Tai Chi & Qigong challenge👇
https://www.skool.com/rootedalchemy

📥 Business Inquiries
info@rootedalchemy.net

🌱 Today's Practice:
This Tai Chi flow guides you through five simple movements designed to build grounding, coordination, and calm awareness. Beginning in the relaxed Wuji stance, you connect breath and posture before moving through flowing arm lifts, gentle openings and closings, and circular motions that explore the balance of yin and yang—anchoring and rising, passive and active. The session ends by returning to stillness, helping you feel centered, balanced, and more connected to your body and space.

☯️ Who Am I:
“Student Always, Teacher Sometimes.” 
This mindset sets the tone for my lifelong journey in Chinese internal arts. I am a Tai Chi/Qigong practitioner and teacher with over 15 years of dedicated study, beginning in Southern California with Praying Mantis Kung Fu and Yang Style Tai Chi, and later continuing in Seattle under Andrew T. Dale, where I immersed myself in Chen Style Tai Chi, Bagua Zhang, and the internal principles that still guide my practice today. In 2007, I moved to Beijing, China, spending eight years training intensively with masters Zhang Wei Dong and Liu Zu Guang, refining my Tai Chi, Bagua Zhang, internal body mechanics, and traditional training methods while also studying Shuai Jiao and Da Bei Quan. During this time, I became the first foreigner awarded a Shuai Jiao Coach’s Certificate in China and co-founded the Guang Wu Shuai Jiao Association to help spread traditional Chinese martial arts internationally. My teaching emphasizes authentic Tai Chi and Qigong—rooted in structure, breath, intention, and internal alignment—to support sustainable practice, real skill development, and long-term vitality.
