title: Tai Chi Walking: from beginners to advanced (Tutorial)
description:
🌿 Continue Your Tai Chi Journey with Our Free Course 
🥋 Join Master Gu & Me at: https://FindBalance.Now

One of the simplest ways to improve balance, strengthen the legs, and bring more awareness into everyday movement is with Tai Chi Walking. 

In this beginner to advanced Tai Chi Walking tutorial, we'll explore how ordinary walking can become a moving meditation. By slowing down, connecting with the ground, and becoming more conscious of each step, we can develop greater balance, coordination, strength, and ease.

In this practice you'll learn:

✓ The foundations of Tai Chi Walking
✓ How to improve balance and stability
✓ Proper alignment for healthy movement
✓ Heel-to-toe and toe-to-heel walking
✓ Strength-building Tai Chi and Kung Fu variations
✓ Mindful movement and moving meditation principles
✓ Simple ways to bring Tai Chi into daily life

One of the beautiful things about Tai Chi Walking is that it can be practiced almost anywhere. Whether you're in your garden, walking through a park, or simply waiting for the kettle to boil, each step becomes an opportunity to cultivate presence, balance, and connection.

Remember, Tai Chi is not about perfection. It is about bringing awareness into the body, relaxing unnecessary tension, and learning to move with greater harmony and ease.

I hope you enjoy exploring this practice with me. Let me know in the comments—where do you most enjoy practicing Tai Chi Walking?

title: Wake Up with This Gentle Morning Tai Chi Routine
description:
👇Click the link to join our 7-Day Tai Chi & Qigong challenge👇
https://www.skool.com/rootedalchemy

📥 Business Inquiries
info@rootedalchemy.net

🌱 Today's Practice:
This session offers a gentle morning Tai Chi flow designed to help you wake up the body, improve circulation, and start the day with a clear, balanced mind. While Tai Chi can be practiced anytime, morning training provides a unique opportunity to reset posture, loosen the joints, and reconnect to your breath and center after sleep. The practice focuses on core Tai Chi principles such as yin-yang balance, open and close, and empty and full, guiding you through smooth, relaxed movements that build coordination and awareness from the ground up. Moving at your own pace, you’ll cultivate a sense of calm energy, stability, and full-body connection, setting a steady foundation for the rest of your day.

☯️ Who Am I:
“Student Always, Teacher Sometimes.” 

This mindset sets the tone for my lifelong journey in Chinese internal arts. I am a Tai Chi/Qigong practitioner and teacher with over 15 years of dedicated study, beginning in Southern California with Praying Mantis Kung Fu and Yang Style Tai Chi, and later continuing in Seattle under Andrew T. Dale, where I immersed myself in Chen Style Tai Chi, Bagua Zhang, and the internal principles that still guide my practice today. In 2007, I moved to Beijing, China, spending eight years training intensively with masters Zhang Wei Dong and Liu Zu Guang, refining my Tai Chi, Bagua Zhang, internal body mechanics, and traditional training methods while also studying Shuai Jiao and Da Bei Quan. During this time, I became the first foreigner awarded a Shuai Jiao Coach’s Certificate in China and co-founded the Guang Wu Shuai Jiao Association to help spread traditional Chinese martial arts internationally. My teaching emphasizes authentic Tai Chi and Qigong—rooted in structure, breath, intention, and internal alignment—to support sustainable practice, real skill development, and long-term vitality.

Above all, stay rooted and remain open🌱

title: Tai Chi Qigong 18 Form (Shi Ba Shi) 太極氣功十八式 with Vivien Chao
description:
To connect with Master Vivien Chao join her newsletter here: https://mailchi.mp/cdadce59e8e9/youtube

Free Week of Live Classes: https://www.balanceforlife.com/freeweek

This is a demo form. The full form can be found here: https://youtu.be/9gJmQ-MIOzQ

The Tai Chi Qigong 18 Form, also known as Shi Ba Shi, is a relatively recent development in the world of Qigong. It was created in 1979 by Professor Lin Housheng, a renowned expert in Qigong and former director of the Shanghai Qigong Research Institute.

Professor Lin designed the form to be accessible to people of all ages and fitness levels. It combines gentle, flowing movements with deep breathing techniques to promote physical and mental well-being. The form is based on traditional Tai Chi principles, but it is simpler and easier to learn than traditional Tai Chi forms.

The 18 movements of the Shi Ba Shi form are designed to stimulate the body's energy flow, improve circulation, and reduce stress. The form is also believed to boost the immune system, improve digestion, and promote better sleep.

Due to its simplicity and effectiveness, the Tai Chi Qigong 18 Form has gained popularity worldwide. It is now taught in many countries and is often recommended by healthcare professionals as a way to improve overall health and well-being.

Tai Chi Qigong 18 Form (Shi Ba Shi) 太極氣功十八式 Demonstration with Vivien Chao

The 18 forms of Tai Chi Qigong, or Shi Ba Shi, are a series of gentle, flowing movements designed to improve physical and mental health. Here are the 18 forms:

1. Commencing Form (起勢調息): This initial posture sets the stage for the practice, centering the mind and body. 0:00

2. Opening the Heart (開闊胸懷): This movement opens the chest and lungs, promoting deep breathing. 01:15

3. Painting a Rainbow Aura Around the Crown (揮舞彩虹): This movement stimulates the crown chakra, promoting spiritual awareness.  02:33

4. Parting the Clouds (輪臂分雲): This movement clears the mind and improves circulation to the arms and shoulders. 03:42

5. Repulse Monkey (定步倒卷肱): This movement strengthens the arms and shoulders and improves flexibility. 05:05

6. Rowing the Boat (湖心划船): This movement strengthens the core and improves balance. 06:06

7. Casting All Your Care Upon the Divine (肩前扥球): This movement releases stress and tension. 07:15

8. Gazing at the Moon (轉體望月): This movement calms the mind and improves digestion. 08:00

9. Pushing Palms (轉腰推掌): This movement strengthens the arms and shoulders and improves circulation.  08:50

10. Cloud Hands (馬步雲手): This movement improves balance and coordination. 10:02

11. Becoming one with the Sea and the Sky (撈海觀天): This movement expands the chest and lungs, promoting deep breathing. 10:53

12. Pushing the Waves (推波助浪): This movement strengthens the legs and improves circulation. 11:55

13. Flying Dove Spreads Its Wings (白鴿展翅): This movement opens the chest and lungs, promoting deep breathing. 12:50

14. Punching (伸臂沖拳): This movement strengthens the arms and shoulders. 13:50

15. Flying Wild Goose (大雁飛翔): This movement improves balance and coordination. 14:44

16. Turning the Wheel of Dharma (環轉飛輪): This movement stimulates the spine and improves flexibility. 15:51

17. Cross-Lateral Knee and Heel Raises (踏步拍球): This movement strengthens the legs and improves circulation. 17:26

18. Balancing and Storing the Chi (按掌平氣): This final posture grounds the practitioner and integrates the energy cultivated throughout the practice. 18:27

It's important to note that the specific movements and their names may vary slightly depending on different lineages and teachers. However, the overall principles and benefits of the practice remain the same.

title: Tai Chi Chuan para que serve #chi #saude #podcast #chinesa #medicinachinesa  @ctcl_centrotaoista ​
description:
A Medicina Tradicional Chinesa
é um sistema de saúde que  remonta há milhares de anos e que se baseia na ideia de que as doenças são causadas por um desequilibrio da força vital. Usa
uma abordagem holística para tratar o corpo, com práticas  complementares como a  acupuntura, fitoterapia, massagens, exercícios , dietoterapia, Tuí Na entre outras.
A Medicina Tradicional Chinesa
tem base na teoria Yin Yang , que descreve a interação entre opostos e a harmonia do universo.
O Chi, a energia vital que flui pelo corpo, mantendo a saúde e o equilíbrio.
E reconhece cinco elementos: 
Madeira, fogo, terra, metal e água que se relacionam com os órgãos do nosso corpo.
A medicina tradicional chinesa foi influenciada pelo Taoismo, uma filosofia e religião originada na China, que se baseia no conceito do TAO, ou seja, a ordem natural do universo com práticas que buscam equilibrar a energia vital.
Nosso convidado é o Professor 
Tsai Shien Jong, Criador  e Diretor do Centro Taoista de Cultivo da Longevidade onde atua como acupunturista e leciona Tai Chi Chuan, Qigong
e meditação.
Práticas que podem ser chamadas de treino de energia
e têm o poder de curar e trazer benefícios à saúde das pessoas por meio do equilíbrio energético.

title: Tai Chi Walking Session For Rapid Calorie Burning! Improve Circulation and Heart Health
description:
#taichi #taichiwalking #burnfat 
      Tai Chi walking is a low-impact exercise that offers significant health advantages for your body and mind.

 
Benefits:
🦵 Lower Body Mobility & Balance (Exercises 2, 3, 4, 16, 20)
   ✅  Unlocks tight hips: Movements like the Hip Opener-Closer and Wide Squat stretch the     adductors and deep hip rotators to improve stride.
    ✅Strengthens the ankle-foot complex: The Heel Lift Walk actively builds calf endurance and strengthens the Achilles tendon.
    ✅ Improves single-leg stability: The Knee to Elbow tracking trains your nervous system to stabilize your weight entirely on one leg.

💥 Core Power & Rotational Flexibility (Exercises 6, 7, 11, 21, 22, 23)
     ✅ Fires up the obliques: Left Right Twist and Swing Arms & Twist Hips activate the transverse abdominis and oblique muscles.
     ✅Decompresses the spine: The rotational momentum gently twists the vertebrae to relieve lower back stiffness.
      ✅Boosts functional power: Knee Strikes and Fist Pull Downs build explosive, grounded stability by connecting your core to your lower body.

👐 Upper Body De-Tensioning (Exercises 5, 8, 9, 10, 17, 18)
       ✅Releases neck and shoulder stress: Alternating Shoulder Rolls and Standing-Shoulder Taps break up tension in the trapezius muscles.
      ✅Improves shoulder joint health: Rolling Arms and Overhead Bounce move the rotator cuff through its full, circular range of motion.
     ✅Expands lung capacity: The pushing and pulling motions (First Pull & Palm Push) expand the rib cage to promote deeper diaphragmatic breathing.

🏃 Cardio-Agility & Coordination (Exercises 12, 13, 14, 15, 19, 24, 25)
      ✅Sharpens brain-body coordination: Alternating Kung Fu Punches and complex Side Steps build neural pathways for better spatial awareness.
     ✅Tones the legs: Agility drills like Side Step Squat Swings keep the quadriceps and glutes engaged through lateral movement.
      ✅Opens the chest: Step & Open Chest counteracts the hunched posture caused by looking at screens.

🌱 Today's Practice:
This outdoor Tai Chi and Qigong practice combines standing meditation, loosening drills, elemental Qigong, and Tai Chi-inspired movements into one relaxing flow. The session is designed to help you connect with your surroundings, improve posture, cultivate breath awareness, and develop a greater sense of grounding and relaxation. Through gentle movements, standing postures, and flowing exercises, you'll work on balance, coordination, the three harmonies, and the core Tai Chi principles of relaxation, awareness, and natural movement.

0:00 - 15:43 Elemental Qigong
15:43 - 18:54 Tai Chi "Cloud Hands"

☯️ Who Am I:
“Student Always, Teacher Sometimes.” 

This mindset sets the tone for my lifelong journey in Chinese internal arts. I am a Tai Chi/Qigong practitioner and teacher with over 15 years of dedicated study, beginning in Southern California with Praying Mantis Kung Fu and Yang Style Tai Chi, and later continuing in Seattle under Andrew T. Dale, where I immersed myself in Chen Style Tai Chi, Bagua Zhang, and the internal principles that still guide my practice today. In 2007, I moved to Beijing, China, spending eight years training intensively with masters Zhang Wei Dong and Liu Zu Guang, refining my Tai Chi, Bagua Zhang, internal body mechanics, and traditional training methods while also studying Shuai Jiao and Da Bei Quan. During this time, I became the first foreigner awarded a Shuai Jiao Coach’s Certificate in China and co-founded the Guang Wu Shuai Jiao Association to help spread traditional Chinese martial arts internationally. My teaching emphasizes authentic Tai Chi and Qigong—rooted in structure, breath, intention, and internal alignment—to support sustainable practice, real skill development, and long-term vitality.

Above all, stay rooted and remain open🌱

title: Tai Chi Balance in Walking
description: Tai Chi walking is all about maintaining your center. Don't let the movement of the leg pull your alignment off your center where you're falling into the step. We only pick up the foot when that leg is fully empty and when we first place the foot down on the ground, it is also still fully empty. Stay rooted so you stay centered and in control of your movements. Don't let your movements control your center.

title: Tai Chi Walking for Beginners: Rewriting Your Relationship to Gravity
description:
Walking is the most common movement in your life — yet almost no one has been taught how to relate to gravity correctly.

Tai Chi Walking is not choreography.
It is the reorganization of weight, balance, and pressure inside the body.

In this foundational lesson, you will learn:

• The real benefits of Tai Chi Walking (balance, joint health, calm, structural strength)
• What *Song* actually means — and how to experience it
• How to settle weight fully into one foot without collapsing
• How buoyancy rises naturally when weight is organized
• How the dantian directs the pressure field forward and backward
• How stepping begins without effort
• How to integrate this into daily life

When weight settles correctly, buoyancy answers.
When buoyancy rises, movement becomes light.

This is not about walking slower.
It is about walking correctly.

Tai Chi Walking is the simplest daily practice of:

Union — not fighting gravity, but organizing within it
Authenticity — movement initiated from your true center
Limitlessness — freedom from tension that restricts your range, balance, and power

Practice it in small spaces.
Practice it in your home.
Practice it in your life.

Organize your weight — and everything changes.

—

Phoenix Mountain Tai Chi
Internal strength. Structural clarity. Effortless power.

The complete course gives you the full step-by-step progression:

title: Most Women 45+ Quit Tai Chi at 3 Minutes… Don’t Make This Mistake
description:
This gentle Tai Chi flow is designed for women 45+ who want a slow, safe, and calming way to support daily movement at home. Every motion is low-impact, smooth, and easy to follow even if you are a complete beginner.
There is no jumping, no strain, and no fast cardio pressure. Just soft rhythmic movement combined with steady breathing to help your body relax into a more balanced daily rhythm.
This style of gentle flow may help support circulation, mobility, and a calmer nervous system state, making it easier to unwind tension from daily stress.

✅ Gentle Flow Benefits
✅ Low-impact movement safe for beginners 45+
✅ No jumping, no pressure on joints
✅ Supports calm breathing and relaxation
✅ Helps improve daily mobility and stiffness
✅ Encourages gentle full-body circulation
✅ Suitable for daily home practice
✅ Designed for stress-free consistency

⏱️ TIMELINE 
⏳ 0:00 – 0:10 INTRO

Standing Calm Breath Reset + Body Awareness

🧘 MAIN FLOW (EVERY 65 SECONDS = 1 MOVEMENT)
0:10 – 1:15 Gentle Rooting Breath Flow

Slow standing grounding, connecting feet to floor, calm inhale-exhale rhythm

1:15 – 2:20 Neck Release Circular Flow

Soft neck rotation, tension release, slow controlled range of motion

2:20 – 3:25 Shoulder Opening Expansion

Gentle shoulder lift and drop, widening chest space, relaxed posture reset

3:25 – 4:30 Slow Arm Wave Activation

Fluid arm movement like water flow, coordinated breathing and motion

4:30 – 5:35 Spine Lengthening Stretch Flow

Gentle vertical stretch, decompress spine, soft alignment reset

5:35 – 6:40 Weight Shift Left Flow

Slow Tai Chi weight transfer, stable lower body control, mindful balance

6:40 – 7:45 Weight Shift Right Flow

Mirror movement to opposite side, improving stability and coordination

7:45 – 8:50 Cloud Hands Slow Flow

Classic Tai Chi circular arm movement, smooth continuous transitions

8:50 – 9:55 Forward Step Gentle Flow

Slow step forward with coordinated arm movement, balance awareness

9:55 – 11:00 Back Step Recovery Flow

Controlled step back, grounding posture, calm reset breathing

11:00 – 12:05 Waist Rotation Soft Flow

Gentle torso twist, improves spinal mobility and digestion support

12:05 – 13:10 Arm Circle Energy Flow

Slow circular arm motion, shoulder joint lubrication, relaxed rhythm

13:10 – 14:15 Side Stretch Opening Flow

Gentle lateral stretch, rib expansion, breathing coordination

14:15 – 15:20 Knee Soft Bend Flow

Low impact knee flexion, joint-friendly movement, stability training

15:20 – 16:25 Balance Hold Flow

Stillness-based balance training, focus + posture alignment

16:25 – 17:30 Slow Walking Tai Chi Flow

Controlled step sequence, full-body coordination, gentle rhythm

17:30 – 18:35 Energy Sweep Down Flow

Hands guide downward motion, grounding and relaxation effect

18:35 – 19:40 Energy Sweep Up Flow

Hands rise slowly upward, expansion and breathing synchronization

19:40 – 20:45 Full Body Integration Flow

Combination of previous movements in smooth continuous flow

🌙 20:45 – 21:00 FINAL CALM

Standing Stillness + Deep Breathing Reset + Relaxed Finish

title: Before & After: Tai Chi - Chi Walking; walking softly
description:
Do you sound like an elephant trudging through your house? I've got some good news for you! Take it from someone who used to sound like a herd of elephants themselves: Tai Chi can make all the difference.  See my long video on how to do Tai Chi Walking: https://youtu.be/38tqFjB-o-g

Before discovering the joys of Tai Chi, I was heavy-footed despite my small 110lb frame. Boom, boom, boom - that's all my mother could hear as I stomped around the house. But after practicing Tai Chi, I now walk so softly that I could sneak up on even the stealthiest of ninjas.

Not only is walking softly easier on your body, joints, and bones, but it can also help extend the life of your joints. So, don't let your heavy footsteps weigh you down! Check out this short video that humorously illustrates the difference that Tai Chi can make in your life. Trust me, your neighbors (and your joints) will thank you!

title: Tai Chi Walking Tutorial: beginners to (extra) advanced
description:
🌿 Enjoyed this Tai Chi Walking practice?
🥋 Join our Free Taoist Wellness Course: https://FindBalance.Now

Tai Chi Walking is one of my favourite ways to develop balance, ease, and grace in the body.

In this practice, we'll start with the foundations and gradually explore more advanced variations, including turning, cloud walking, balance work, Tai Chi energy ball exercises, and even some Kung Fu-inspired movement.

Tai Chi Walking is much more than simply putting one foot in front of the other. It is a moving meditation. As you bring your awareness down into your feet and learn to move with softness and control, you begin to develop greater balance, coordination, relaxation, and presence.

In this video you'll learn:

✓ Tai Chi Walking for beginners
✓ How to improve balance and stability
✓ Heel-to-toe and toe-to-heel walking
✓ Cloud Walking techniques
✓ Tai Chi energy ball practice
✓ Advanced Tai Chi Walking variations
✓ Moving meditation principles

Remember, Tai Chi isn't about doing it perfectly. It's about meeting yourself where you are, softening tension, and enjoying the experience of moving your body.

Whether you're new to Tai Chi or looking to deepen your practice, I hope this tutorial helps you discover a little more balance, ease, and joy in your movement.

Let me know in the comments: which part of the practice was your favourite?

Este video de Tai Chi Chuan para iniciantes serve para qualquer pessoa que queira aprender sozinha em casa e sem complicação.
Descubra como resgatar a leveza e a energia do seu corpo em apenas 10 minutos por dia.

-------------------------------------

SOBRE O DESAFIO "LEVEZA E ENERGIA":

Este desafio foi criado pelo Fisioterapeuta Andre Leung para quem busca resultados rápidos, mas de forma suave e sem impacto.

São 7 práticas de 10 minutos baseadas nos fundamentos do Tai Chi Chuan e da Medicina Chinesa, criadas para:

✓ Soltar as tensões dos ombros, pescoço e costas
✓ "Destravar" o corpo e melhorar a flexibilidade
✓ Ativar sua energia e ter mais ânimo e disposição
✓ Acalmar a mente e aliviar o estresse do dia a dia
✓ Começar uma rotina de autocuidado fácil de seguir
Se você procura exercícios em casa para sair do sedentarismo, aliviar o estresse ou simplesmente ter mais vitalidade, este desafio é o primeiro passo perfeito.

-------------------------------------

QUEM É ANDRE LEUNG?

Andre Leung é Fisioterapeuta (CREFITO: 89250-F) , Mestre em Artes Marciais e especialista com Doutorado em Acupuntura pela WFCMS. 

Com mais de 20 anos de prática, sua missão é traduzir a sabedoria milenar da Medicina Chinesa em movimentos simples que qualquer pessoa pode fazer para ter mais saúde e bem-estar.

title: 15-Min Tai Chi After 40 🌿 Lose Belly Fat & Stretch Your Spine
description:
Looking for a gentle way to lose belly fat after 40 while improving flexibility and easing stiffness? This 15-minute Tai Chi workout is designed to support healthy movement, stretch the spine, and help your body feel lighter, stronger, and more relaxed — all without jumping or intense exercise.

In today’s session, you’ll follow slow, beginner-friendly Tai Chi movements that may help support:

🌿 Belly fat loss and healthy weight management
🌿 Better spine flexibility and posture
🌿 Reduced back stiffness and daily discomfort
🌿 Improved balance, mobility, and coordination
🌿 Gentle full body movement for adults over 40
🌿 Relaxation, circulation, and overall wellness

This low-impact Tai Chi routine is suitable for beginners and anyone wanting a calmer way to stay active at home. Move slowly, breathe naturally, and listen to your body throughout the workout.

If you enjoy this session, leave a comment and let me know where you’re practicing from. Thank you for moving with me today.

title: 8 MIN Full Body Taichi Weight Loss at Home • Gentle Cardio Lymphatic Drainage Exercises
description:
If you'd like to go deeper, join the Spring Thaw challenge,so your body is prepared for the upcoming Summer Pulse program we will begin all together on June 30 :)
Learn more at: https://www.alkimefit.com/start-spring-thaw

8-Minute Tai Chi Flow for Weight Loss & Lymphatic Drainage 
Weight balance begins with flow.

When we spend long periods sitting, feeling stressed, or moving very little, circulation slows, muscles become less active, and the lymphatic system (which depends on movement to circulate) can become sluggish. The body may respond with heaviness, fluid retention, fatigue, and difficulty maintaining a healthy weight.

This gentle Tai Chi-inspired routine is designed to awaken your body naturally through rhythmic, mindful movement.

In just 8 minutes, you'll support:

• lymphatic drainage and circulation
• gentle cardio without exhausting the body
• muscle activation and metabolic health
• stress reduction and nervous system balance
• improved mobility and energy flow
• a lighter, more energized feeling throughout the day

Weight loss is not only about burning calories.

It is also about creating a body that circulates well, moves well, and feels safe enough to release what it no longer needs to hold.

When movement is enjoyable and sustainable, consistency becomes effortless and that is where lasting transformation happens.

This is the philosophy behind the AlkimeFit™ Method: simple daily practices that support your nervous system, posture, lymphatic health, and metabolism through gentle yet effective movement.

Move with kindness.
Circulate with intention.
Transform from within.

—

title: 5 Tai Chi Movements To Feel Stress Free
description:
Stress doesn’t just live in the mind.
It settles in the body.

Tight shoulders.
A stiff spine.
Heavy hips.
Shallow breathing.

Tai Chi helps release that tension through slow, rhythmic movement.

Twisting motions loosen the spine.
Swinging the arms releases the shoulders.
Body waves reconnect breath and movement.
Simple steps bring circulation back to the legs.

Within a few minutes, the body starts to shift from tension to flow.

That’s why practices like Tai Chi have been used for centuries to calm the nervous system and restore balance.

You don’t need intensity to reduce stress.
You need intentional movement.

comment “Tai Chi” below, and I’ll send it to you.

title: Las 8 Fuerzas del Tai Chi Chuan
description:
#BafaWuBu #TecnicasTaichi #taichichuan 

Las 8 fuerzas del Tai Chi son los ocho métodos fundamentales de transferencia de fuerza del cuerpo, también llamados "Bafa" o "Ocho Puertas". Estas energías son la base de todas las técnicas del Taijiquan y se practican en conjunto para mejorar la salud y la habilidad marcial. 

Las 8 fuerzas (Bafa)
Peng (掤): Rechazar o expandir. Es una cualidad de expansión que desestabiliza al oponente, similar a la energía de un resorte. 
Lü (捋): Desviar, absorber o estirar. Se utiliza para redireccionar la fuerza del oponente adhiriéndose a él. 
Ji (擠): Presionar o estrujar. Se enfoca en aplicar presión sobre un punto específico, a menudo usando el dorso de la mano. 
An (按): Empujar. Es una energía que empuja, utilizando la estructura completa del cuerpo en lugar de solo los brazos. 
Cai (採): Tirar o arrancar. Es una versión descendente de "Lü", que redirige la energía del oponente hacia el suelo. 
Lie (挒): Separar o partir. Se basa en el uso de dos fuerzas en direcciones opuestas para separar el cuerpo del oponente. 
Zhou (肘): Codo. Se refiere al uso de golpes de codo como una técnica a corta distancia. 
Kao (靠): Hombro o apoyarse. Implica golpear con el hombro, utilizando el pecho y la espalda como parte de la técnica.

title: Tai Chi Melodies 🌸 Plum Blossom | Relaxing Traditional Chinese Music for Meditation & Flow
description:
Immerse yourself in the serenity of traditional Chinese music with "Plum Blossom," a gentle and flowing composition inspired by the delicate blooming of plum flowers in early spring.

In Chinese culture, the plum blossom is a powerful symbol of resilience, beauty, and renewal. Blooming in the cold of late winter, it represents inner strength, perseverance, and the quiet emergence of life even in the harshest conditions. It is deeply connected to harmony, balance, and spiritual awakening.

This music is carefully crafted to support Tai Chi, Qi Gong, meditation, yoga, and deep relaxation. With soft, flowing melodies and no percussion, it encourages mindful breathing, graceful movement, and inner calm.

Featuring traditional instruments such as guzheng, erhu, and bamboo flute (dizi), this soundscape invites you to slow down, reconnect, and let your energy flow naturally—like plum blossoms drifting in the spring breeze.

🌸 Perfect for:
• Tai Chi & Qi Gong practice
• Meditation & mindfulness
• Deep relaxation
• Gentle yoga
• Study & focus

Welcome to Vibrarte Music — your space to reconnect with calm, energy, and inner harmony.

title: TAI CHI Per Principianti - Lezione 1 di 3
description:
Il Tai Chi Chuan o Taijiquan è un’arte marziale cinese e una disciplina per il benessere di mente e corpo. In questo video vedrai come partire con il Tai Chi.
Accedi al Corso base di Tai Chi: 👉 https://www.marcogamuzza.it/tai-chi-start/

L’obiettivo del Tai Chi è quello di creare una connessione tra tutte le parti del corpo al fine di raggiungere un’armonia generale, un rilassamento fisico e una struttura piena e forte.

Le origini del Tai Chi risalgono intorno al 1324 d.C. ad opera del monaco taoista Zhang San Feng, studioso ed esperto praticante di medicina cinese, ma possiamo dire che in realtà in passato esistevano già un nucleo di tecniche che contribuiranno alla nascita del Tai Chi.

Infatti, già nel Yijing il libro dei mutamenti si fa riferimento al Tai Chi come all’unione dei due elementi Yin e Yang.

All’interno di questa serie di video troverai le prime nozioni di base sul Tai Chi Chuan, dai cenni storici, al saluto, fino ai movimenti fondamentali.

title: Tai Chi For Beginners: 7-Minute Routine For A New You
description:
When you consider your happiness and wellbeing, aside from all the reasons that have held back, and inside all that you have thought about what you want to accomplish, you can begin to realize, who are you, that is capable of more joy and more well-being?

And as you begin to feel more like your happiest and healthiest self, would you like that, for your life? Would it be worth 7 minutes of your day?

Because Tai Chi is completely transformative, and when you know the secret, totally easy. And what is the secret? 

The secret is to let go. Let go of your thoughts about doing it right or wrong. Let go of being perfect, of being critical, of self doubting. Let go and that means you can just float. Like a leaf in the wind, like a boat in a gentle stream, like a being of your highest   spirit, floating in a serene sea of blissful energy.

Let's learn how now.

title: Zhaobao Taijiquan: The Forgotten Chinese Fighting Style
description:
Discover the hidden world of Zhaobao Taijiquan, a rare and traditional style of Taijiquan that has retained its full martial essence. In this documentary, we follow Liu Yazi, a master of both Zhaobao and Chen Taijiquan, as he shares the philosophy, techniques, and history of this often overlooked martial art.

Shot in London, this film explores Liu Yazi’s journey as a practitioner and teacher, revealing the secret training methods and martial applications that make Zhaobao Taijiquan unique. Whether you are a martial artist, historian, or curious viewer, join us in uncovering a living tradition that connects the past to the present.

Master Liu's Zhaobao Taiji club in London: https://londontaiji.com/

_______________________________________________
✩ FAQ

▹Why is there no fighting? 
This is a performance of a taiji sword form in modern wushu. This is meant to be a form and not actual combat, although the movements all derive from real techniques. All Asian martial arts had a heavy emphasis on forms training, and it was the equivalent of shadowboxing or padwork in boxing. Traditionally, forms were really used to train and demonstrate techniques, and you would practice the form and actual fighting. 

For wushu's adaption to sport, there is a forms division (taolu), and a fighting division (sanda). Today there's no need to fight, so many people learn the basic applications, but focus only on taolu. Wushu taolu take a style's martial techniques and builds them into aesthetically pleasing routines that epitomize the ideals of a style's philosophy. These forms showcase techniques at the highest level, but are stylized and composed for performance in a sport setting. They are meant to be entertaining and pleasing to watch, just like a high-level gymnastics or ice skating routines. Wushu taolu is judged on proper technique, power, agility, technical difficulty, and martial intent.

If you're interested in actual Chinese martial arts in combat, take a look at Sanda (Chinese kickboxing): https://youtu.be/dwM0gEpD9QY

▹Why is her sword floppy?
Since this is adapted for sport and health, you want to change the weapons to be safer for practice and competition as a sport. Not only that, Taiji is regularly practiced by over a billion people, the last thing you need is these practitioners showing up to parks and gyms with real swords or anything close to it. Note, although these are made to be purposely floppy at the ends, they are counterbalanced at the hilt to be as closer to the balance weight as traditional Chinese swords. 

If you this seems to odd to you, think about some other sports that came from combat, such as fencing, archery, and javelin throwing . These sports use heavily modified weapons that are safer for sport practice as well. In fact, fencing swords doesn't even look like swords anymore, and are even more flexible than wushu swords.

title: TAI CHI Per Principianti - Lezione 1 di 3
description:
Il Tai Chi Chuan o Taijiquan è un’arte marziale cinese e una disciplina per il benessere di mente e corpo. In questo video vedrai come partire con il Tai Chi.
Accedi al Corso base di Tai Chi: 👉 https://www.marcogamuzza.it/tai-chi-start/

L’obiettivo del Tai Chi è quello di creare una connessione tra tutte le parti del corpo al fine di raggiungere un’armonia generale, un rilassamento fisico e una struttura piena e forte.

Le origini del Tai Chi risalgono intorno al 1324 d.C. ad opera del monaco taoista Zhang San Feng, studioso ed esperto praticante di medicina cinese, ma possiamo dire che in realtà in passato esistevano già un nucleo di tecniche che contribuiranno alla nascita del Tai Chi.

Infatti, già nel Yijing il libro dei mutamenti si fa riferimento al Tai Chi come all’unione dei due elementi Yin e Yang.

All’interno di questa serie di video troverai le prime nozioni di base sul Tai Chi Chuan, dai cenni storici, al saluto, fino ai movimenti fondamentali.

title: Tai Chi for Combat in Chenjiagou (Chen-Style Taiji Quan) | Kung Fu Wise
description:
Host Kleber Battaglia travels to Chenjiagou in Henan Province, the birthplace of Chen-style Taiji Quan. Training with Master Chen Ziqiang, he learns Taiji wrestling and how slow, circular forms connect to fast, practical applications. Out in the fields, he joins Master Chen in digging for yams, discovering how Chinese martial arts stances are rooted in daily life, before refueling on herbal chicken soup, yams, and other local delicacies. Back at the school, training intensifies with deep stances, Taiji ball exercises, and long-pole drills that test balance, coordination, and whole-body power. A quiet walk along the Yellow River leads to Taiji philosophy, as Master Chen explains Yin and Yang and the three “gates” of practice. After visiting a shrine to learn about the legacy of founder Chen Wangting, Kleber tests his skills in a wrestling match against Taiji students before being treated to a homemade bowl of egg-and-tomato noodles at Master Chen’s house. The journey ends at the confluence of two rivers, where Kleber learns the origin story of Taiji.

title: Solo necesitas aprender esto del Tai Chi (Péng explicado en 10 minutos)
description:
¿Te endureces ante la presión? ¿O te colapsas y pierdes tus límites? Existe una tercera vía, y viene del Taijiquan. Se llama Péng (掤), la "madre de todas las energías".

En este video descubrirás:
✅ Qué es exactamente Péng y por qué los maestros dicen que sin él no hay Tai Chi
✅ La metáfora del globo que cambiará cómo entiendes la resistencia
✅ Cómo sentir Péng en tu cuerpo (hombros hundidos, codos caídos, columna erguida)
✅ La aplicación práctica en tu vida diaria: ni atacar, ni huir. Mantener el centro.
✅ Un ejercicio simple para responder desde la expansión, no desde el miedo

Ya sea que practiques artes marciales, meditación, o simplemente quieras manejar mejor el estrés y las relaciones difíciles, este principio transformará tu forma de estar en el mundo.

🎯 Aplica Péng HOY: antes de reaccionar ante una presión, respira y pregúntate: ¿Cómo respondería desde mi plenitud, sin rigidez ni colapso?

---

📌 **Capítulos:**
0:00 - La pregunta que lo cambia todo
1:00 - ¿Qué es Péng? La metáfora del globo
3:00 - Cómo sentir Péng en tu cuerpo
5:00 - La tercera vía: ni lucha ni huida
7:30 - Ejercicio práctico para tu día a día
9:30 - Cierre e invitación

---

💬 **Déjame en los comentarios:** ¿En qué situación de tu vida necesitas aplicar más Péng? ¿Te endureces o te colapsas?

少林三十六擒拿手演示 :
金丝缠腕, 蛇形刁手,  老娘捂嘴,  罗汉扣腕
擒拿讲究借力制敌、控制关节、把握时机，
体现少林武术的智慧与技巧。

Today Coach Peng Siyuan demonstrates techniques from the Shaolin Thirty-Six Qinna Hands (少林三十六擒拿手), a traditional Chinese martial art system focused on control, leverage, joint manipulation, and tactical positioning.

Featured techniques include:
🔹 金丝缠腕 (Golden Silk Wrapping Wrist)
🔹 蛇形刁手 (Snake-Hand Seizing Technique)
🔹 老娘捂嘴 (Old Lady Covers the Mouth)
🔹 罗汉扣腕 (Arhat Wrist Lock)

These classical Shaolin methods emphasize timing, precision, structure, and control rather than brute force.


⚡ Traditional Kung Fu preserves centuries of martial knowledge through skill, discipline, and practice.

title: 每天坚持十分钟，活动下肢，带动循环，感受
description:
太極是一種古老的中國武術，它有時也被稱作「太極拳」或是「運動式冥想」。它由一系列不同姿勢、並以緩慢動作交會相連組成
據說，太極有利於心臟健康。在最近一項研究中，(（研究人員）在把太極和快走進行比較後發現，太極在降低血壓以及其他心臟病危險因素方面、比如血糖水平和膽固醇，效果明顯更好。
太極還可能對免疫系統有益。在加利福尼亞的一項研究中，研究人員讓一半參與者習練太極，另一半則參加健康教育課程。他們每個人都接種了帶狀皰疹疫苗。但9個星期以後，那組練習太極的學員免疫力是另一組學員的兩倍。

此外，太極組學員在身體機能、活力、精神健康以及減少身體疼痛方面也表現出更大的改善。

Struggling with energy, cravings, or stress weight? 

You’re not alone.

Most people think weight loss is only about dieting and workouts.

title: Tai Chi Chuan   24 forms
description:

#chinesemartialarts #taijiquan #wushu

24 Forms of Tai Chi Chuan, also known as the Yang Style Tai Chi, is a simplified version of the traditional Tai Chi forms. It is designed to be accessible to practitioners of all skill levels and emphasizes smooth, flowing movements.

 Overview of the 24 Forms
Here’s a brief overview of the 24 forms:

1. Beginning - Preparation and stance
2. Parting the Horse's Mane - A gentle opening movement
3. White Crane Spreads Its Wings - A graceful arm movement
4. Brush Knee and Push - A combination of brushing and pushing
5. Playing the Lute - A unique posture that resembles playing a musical instrument
6. Six Parting the Horse's Mane - Variations on the opening movement
7. Step Back and Repulse Monkey - A retreating movement
8. Wave Hands Like Clouds - Fluid side-to-side hand movements
9. Single Whip - A striking form with a sweeping motion
10. Lift Hands - Raising the arms in a balanced manner
11. Shoulder Stroke - A movement that emphasizes shoulder mobility
12. White Crane Spreads Its Wings (repeated) - Enhancing the earlier form
13. Cross Hands - A concluding gesture that symbolizes unity

Key Benefits of Practicing Tai Chi
Improves Balance: Enhances stability and coordination.
Increases Flexibility: Gentle movements promote joint health.
Reduces Stress: Mindful practice helps in stress relief.
Enhances Concentration: Focus on movements aids mental clarity.
Promotes Overall Health: Regular practice can lead to better physical fitness.

Wu Amin's Demonstration
Wu Amin's demonstrations of these forms likely highlight the elegance and precision involved in each movement. Watching a skilled practitioner can provide valuable insights into the flow and energy of Tai Chi.
Wu Amen, 6th duan of Chinese wushu, champion of taijiquan, jian, tuishou, technical consultant of Beijing culture publishing house.

☯️ Who Am I:
“Student Always, Teacher Sometimes.” 
This mindset sets the tone for my lifelong journey in Chinese internal arts. I am a Tai Chi/Qigong practitioner and teacher with over 15 years of dedicated study, beginning in Southern California with Praying Mantis Kung Fu and Yang Style Tai Chi, and later continuing in Seattle under Andrew T. Dale, where I immersed myself in Chen Style Tai Chi, Bagua Zhang, and the internal principles that still guide my practice today. In 2007, I moved to Beijing, China, spending eight years training intensively with masters Zhang Wei Dong and Liu Zu Guang, refining my Tai Chi, Bagua Zhang, internal body mechanics, and traditional training methods while also studying Shuai Jiao and Da Bei Quan. During this time, I became the first foreigner awarded a Shuai Jiao Coach’s Certificate in China and co-founded the Guang Wu Shuai Jiao Association to help spread traditional Chinese martial arts internationally. My teaching emphasizes authentic Tai Chi and Qigong—rooted in structure, breath, intention, and internal alignment—to support sustainable practice, real skill development, and long-term vitality.

title: Las 8 Fuerzas del Tai Chi Chuan
description:
#BafaWuBu #TecnicasTaichi #taichichuan 

Las 8 fuerzas del Tai Chi son los ocho métodos fundamentales de transferencia de fuerza del cuerpo, también llamados "Bafa" o "Ocho Puertas". Estas energías son la base de todas las técnicas del Taijiquan y se practican en conjunto para mejorar la salud y la habilidad marcial. 

Las 8 fuerzas (Bafa)
Peng (掤): Rechazar o expandir. Es una cualidad de expansión que desestabiliza al oponente, similar a la energía de un resorte. 
Lü (捋): Desviar, absorber o estirar. Se utiliza para redireccionar la fuerza del oponente adhiriéndose a él. 
Ji (擠): Presionar o estrujar. Se enfoca en aplicar presión sobre un punto específico, a menudo usando el dorso de la mano. 
An (按): Empujar. Es una energía que empuja, utilizando la estructura completa del cuerpo en lugar de solo los brazos. 
Cai (採): Tirar o arrancar. Es una versión descendente de "Lü", que redirige la energía del oponente hacia el suelo. 
Lie (挒): Separar o partir. Se basa en el uso de dos fuerzas en direcciones opuestas para separar el cuerpo del oponente. 
Zhou (肘): Codo. Se refiere al uso de golpes de codo como una técnica a corta distancia. 
Kao (靠): Hombro o apoyarse. Implica golpear con el hombro, utilizando el pecho y la espalda como parte de la técnica. 

www.facebook.com/ConcienciaBambu.Taichi
www.instagram.com/ConcienciaBambu.Taichi

title: Tai Chi Sword 16-Step | Taiji Jian | Follow Me Back View
description:
Tai Chi Sword 16-Step | Taiji Jian | Follow Me Back View

Experience the graceful flow of Tai Chi Sword 16-Step (Taiji Jian 十六式太极剑), a traditional Chinese martial art that blends balance, mindfulness, and inner strength. Follow me through this short sword form which emphasises smooth, continuous movement, uniting body and mind through breath and intention.

Practicing Tai Chi Sword helps improve coordination, posture, flexibility, and focus, while also reducing stress and enhancing overall vitality. It is both a martial practice and a moving meditation—building internal energy (Qi) while cultivating calmness and clarity.

Whether you’re new to Tai Chi (Taiji) or refining your form, this back view follow me demonstration offers a clear look at the beauty and benefits of Taiji Sword practice.

✨ CHAPTERS:  

00:00 – Taichi Sword 16式太極劍
00:04 – 1. Opening Stance 起式 
00:38 – 2. Point Sword With Feet together 並步點劍 
00:43 – 3. Step back to strike 撤步反擊 
00:52 – 4. Step forward to thrust 進步平刺 
01:00 – 5. Slice to the right 向右平帶 
01:04 – 6. Slice to the left 向左平帶 
01:10 – 7. Stand on one leg and thrust 獨立上刺 
01:16 – 8. Turn around to chop 轉身弓步劈劍 
01:22 – 9. Empty stance to draw back 虛步回抽 
01:29 – 10. Stand feet together to thrust 並步平刺 
01:34 – 11. Block to right bow stance 右弓步攔劍 
01:38 – 12. Block to the left bow stance 左弓步攔劍 
01:46 – 13. Step up with reverse thrust 進步反刺 
01:51 – 14. Step up hook and chop 上步掛劈 
02:04 – 15. Draw back with T Stance 丁步回抽 
02:07 – 16. Turn around to circular swipe 旋轉平抹 
02:16 – 17. Thrust with bow stance 弓步直刺 
00:00 – 18. Closing stance 收势

🌿 Symbolism & Benefits of the 16 Tai Chi Sword Movements

1. Opening Stance 起式
Symbolism: Beginning of intention, calming the mind, connecting to Qi.
Benefit: Establishes balance, focus, and body alignment.

2. Point Sword With Feet Together 並步點劍
Symbolism: Focused intention, directing energy through the blade.
Benefit: Enhances coordination of eyes, hands, and feet.

3. Step Back to Strike 撤步反擊
Symbolism: Yielding then returning force—defence becoming offence.
Benefit: Improves agility, balance, and awareness of timing.

4. Step Forward to Thrust 進步平刺
Symbolism: Direct, unwavering intent—precision and clarity.
Benefit: Builds leg strength and core stability.

5. Slice to the Right 向右平帶
Symbolism: Clearing obstacles, sweeping away resistance.
Benefit: Opens the shoulders and stretches the waist.

6. Slice to the Left 向左平帶
Symbolism: Balance and harmony, yin and yang.
Benefit: Improves lateral movement and flexibility.

7. Stand on One Leg and Thrust 獨立上刺
Symbolism: Stability amidst challenge.
Benefit: Strengthens balance, coordination, and leg power.

8. Turn Around to Chop 轉身弓步劈劍
Symbolism: Transformation and decisive action.
Benefit: Strengthens waist rotation and spinal mobility.

9. Empty Stance to Draw Back 虛步回抽
Symbolism*: Yielding and conserving energy.
Benefit: Trains weight shifting and leg control.

10. Stand Feet Together to Thrust 並步平刺
Symbolism: Unity of body and mind through a single strike.
Benefit: Improves posture, focus, and controlled movement.

11. Block to Right Bow Stance 右弓步攔劍
Symbolism: Strength with rooted defense.
Benefit: Strengthens legs and cultivates grounding.

12. Block to the Left Bow Stance 左弓步攔劍
Symbolism: Balance in duality—protecting both sides.
Benefit: Increases flexibility and body symmetry.

13. Step Up with Reverse Thrust 進步反刺
Symbolism: Turning challenges into opportunity.
Benefit*: Sharpens coordination and agility.

14. Step Up Hook and Chop 上步掛劈
Symbolism: Control followed by release of energy.
Benefit*: Strengthens shoulders, arms, and back.

15. Draw Back with T Stance 丁步回抽
Symbolism: Restraint and readiness.
Benefit: Develops leg strength, balance, and precision.

16. Turn Around to Circular Swipe 旋轉平抹
Symbolism: Flow of nature, circular motion of yin-yang.
Benefit: Improves waist flexibility, coordination, and fluidity.

17. Thrust with Bow Stance 弓步直刺
Symbolism: Focused intent, projecting Qi outward.
Benefit: Enhances stability, strength, and concentration.

18. Closing Stance 收势
Symbolism: Completion, return to stillness, integration of energy.
Benefit: Calms the body, regulates breathing, seals the practice.

title: Tai Chi Chuan   32 forms
description:
Support channel on Paypal: https://paypal.me/chinesemartialarts

#chinesemartialarts #taijiquan #wushu

Tai Chi Chuan is a beautiful and deeply meditative martial art, known for its graceful, flowing movements and emphasis on balance, relaxation, and mindfulness. The 32 forms you mentioned refer to a simplified set of Tai Chi movements that are often practiced to help beginners learn the basics. Here’s a breakdown of what you might find in Wu Amin's demonstration of these forms:

 Overview of Tai Chi Chuan - 32 Forms
Origin: Tai Chi Chuan has roots in ancient Chinese martial arts, evolving into a practice that promotes both physical and mental well-being.
Forms: The 32 forms are typically a simplified version of traditional Tai Chi, designed to make it accessible for practitioners of all levels.

Key Features of the 32 Forms
1. Flowing Movements: The forms are characterized by smooth transitions between postures.
2. Breathing Techniques: Emphasis on deep, mindful breathing that enhances relaxation.
3. Mindfulness: Practitioners focus on being present, cultivating awareness of their body and movements.
4. Self-Defense Application: While primarily practiced for health, the forms also have martial applications.

Sample Forms Included
Here are some commonly practiced forms in the 32 form set:
Commencing Form: The starting position, grounding the practitioner.
Grasp the Bird's Tail: A fundamental movement focusing on balance and coordination.
Wave Hands Like Clouds: Promotes fluidity and relaxation.
Single Whip: Integrates power with grace.
Closing Form: A calming sequence to conclude the practice.

Benefits of Practicing Tai Chi
Improves Balance: Essential for maintaining stability, especially as we age.
Enhances Flexibility: Gentle stretching movements promote overall flexibility.
Reduces Stress: The meditative aspect helps in reducing anxiety and stress.
Promotes Longevity: Regular practice is associated with improved health and longevity.
Wu Amen, 6th duan of Chinese wushu, champion of taijiquan, jian, tuishou, technical consultant of Beijing culture publishing house.

Follow along with Michelle in practicing our Yang Taijiquan sequence. Here is the front view for Part 1 of 3 at slow speed. 

Many people practice Taijiquan for its health benefits including increased energy, stress reduction, improved balance, and relief from ailments. However, Taijiquan was developed as an internal style of martial arts. As a soft style, Taijiquan emphasizes relaxed muscles to allow for better Qi flow. With a better Qi flow and a concentrated mind, practitioners learn to sense, neutralize, and redirect an opponent’s force.

title: Tai chi chuan Combat
description:
Tai chi combat sur http://www.imineo.com/sante-bien-etre/bien-etre/tai-chi-qi-gong/shaolin-vol8-tai-chi-tui-shou-video-15995.htm

Le Tui Shou : le Tai Chi appliqué au combat.

Dans cette vidéo, le moine Shaolin, Huang Aguilar, nous présente le Tui Shou, l'expression au combat des formes de Tai Chi. Le Tui Shou, "poussée des mains", est la partie la plus dynamique, mais aussi la plus compliquée de la pratique du Tai Chi Chuan.
Nous étudierons les séries d'exercices centrées sur le combat, la première forme bouddhiste de Tai Chi Chuan, les luxations de base, les luxations au combat et leurs contre-attaques, ainsi que les techniques de déplacement et de renversement. Une excellente occasion de découvrir la partie la moins connue et la moins pratiquée du Tai Chi.

title: Chen-style Taiji Single Sword.
description: Chen-style Tai Chi Single Sword DVD(language menu:Chinese/English/Cinese Simplified.subtitles menu:Chinese/English.Chinese Simplified):The single sword belongs to one of the Taiji short weapons. It has 49 forms, which mainly includes stab, chop, upper cut, parry, point, slice, upper block, sweep, cross cut, thrust and push. The player should move his body smoothly, steadily and quickly, applying his force hard or soft, closely following his opponent, dodging or tossing as the case may be. Chen-style Taiji Swordplay can be characterized as continuous and round movement of the sword with the whole body, quick change of attack and withdrawal, hard or soft application of forces. Exercise requirements: the player should move smoothly and roundly as cloud flying and water flowing, and apply his force suddenly as a lion shaking his hair. Be sure to keep your movements changing constantly.

title: Tai Chi Chin Na (YMAA Qin Na) Dr. Yang, Jwing-Ming - Taijiquan joint locks
description:
Streaming Video available: https://vimeo.com/ondemand/d0463taijichinna/411812278
DVD on Amazon: http://www.amazon.com/Taiji-Chin-Depth-Two-Disc-Edition/dp/B0024WVOX4
YMAA Publication Center: http://ymaa.com/publishing/dvd/internal/taiji-chin-na
Control Any Opponent with Simple, Powerful Joint Locks. Six-hour video lesson!
Chin Na (Qin Na) is the art of seizing and controlling an opponent. It is a fast, effective way to subdue an attacker using joint locks, cavity press, bone misplacement, muscle grabbing, and artery sealing. Taijiquan is a traditional martial art, and Chin Na is an important part of your complete Taiji (Tai Chi) training. Taiji Chin Na are smooth, flowing techniques that are simple to execute and difficult to escape.
Dr. Yang discusses, demonstrates, and teaches you his Chin Na techniques up-close and in detail. All these techniques are hidden inside the Yang style postures, but can be applied to all Taiji styles.
DISC ONE: Course One: 19 techniques from the Eight Moving Patterns, and Clockwise Coiling in Crossed Pushing Hands.

Course Two: 16 techniques from the Eight Moving Patterns including Rotating Neutralizing, and Counterclockwise Coiling in Crossed Pushing Hands.

Course Three: 21 techniques from the Eight Moving Patterns, and Parallel Pushing Hands.

DISC TWO: Course Four: 10 techniques specifically for countering an opponent controlling your elbow in Pushing Hands.
Also includes a Bonus Video (with over 200 chapters!) with Dr. Yang demonstrating 80 Taiji Chin Na techniques in slow motion and at full speed, categorized by the Eight Basic Taiji Patterns (Eight Doors) and chin na found in pushing hands. These techniques can be found in the popular book Taiji Chin Na (YMAA).
SPECIAL FEATURES: Five videos on Two DVDs • Over 6 hours • Hundreds of Scene Selections • Menus/Subtitles: English, Spanish

功法特點
動作簡單，容易掌握，舒適安全

太極氣功十八式第二套與第一套一樣，共有十八節，每節只有一個簡單的動作，比太極拳更為簡單、易學。但動作雖簡單，卻又包含著太極拳均勻、柔和、舒展、優美、意氣相隨、神體合一的特點，並體現出氣功的調身、調息、調心的特點，同時配合悠揚、舒情、輕鬆、優美的音樂伴奏，舒適安全，也不使人感到枯燥無味，容易被廣大群眾接受和掌握。

動作緩慢，均勻連貫。許多人由於身體的虛弱和疾病的影響，行動不便，不能像健康人或運動員那樣做快速而有力的動作。太極氣功十八式動作緩慢、均勻、連貫，患者力所能及，有利於調動患者的積極性，使其持之以恒地鍛鍊。意氣相隨，神體合一

太極氣功十八式是以意引氣，意氣相隨，上下協調。內外相合，"其根在腳，發於腿，主宰於腰，形與手指。"手動，腰動，腳動，眼神亦隨之動。手足開與合，上與下，心意也隨之開合與上下。神為主體，身為驅使，動作協調，舉動輕靈，神體合一。

動作舒展，幅度大
太極氣功十八式，每個動作都要求做得輕鬆、愉快、舒展、幅度大。例如鳳凰展翅，兩臂就注意伸展；懷中抱月，身體就應該注意伸展。由於動作舒展，幅度大，因此關節活動的範圍也就大，所牽垃活動的肌肉也多，這對身體功能性的鍛鍊有很大的幫助。能防治頸、肩、腰、腿痛等病，消除關節功能障礙，鬆懈關節周圍軟組織的粘連、僵硬、痙攣，改善病變部位的血液循環和氧化還原過程，增強肌肉力量。

靜裡有動，動中有靜
太極氣功十八式是動靜結合功，靜裡有動，動中有靜，整套動作緩慢、均勻、柔和、"以靜御動，雖動猶靜。肢體運動時，配合意念和呼吸，調動體內的 “內氣"運轉，使身體發熱出汗，唾液分泌增加，血管容積增大，皮膚電發生明顯變化，胃腸蠕動增強，橫隔肌升降幅度加大，肺活量增大。看起來是外動內靜，實際上還起到了內運動的作用，靜裡有動，動靜相兼。

適合於體弱及慢性病者鍛鍊
俗語說:"生命在於運動"。但有些慢性病者不能做劇烈活動怎麼辦 ?我們認為參加太極氣功十八式鍛鍊是比較適宜的。一般說來，健康的人如果天天躺在床上，不起來活動，一星期後就會感到頭昏眼花，手腳虛軟無力。這就証明運動對增進健康的重要性，青少年和體魄健康的同志，可通過打球、跑步、爬山、游泳等體育鍛鍊來增強體質，增進健康，而慢性病的患者怎麼辦 ?我們不能要求臥床病人做大量運動鍛鍊，也不能要求心臟病患者進行劇烈運動。太級氣功十八式動作緩慢、輕柔、舒適、特別適合體虛、體弱和慢性病者鍛鍊，以增強體質，恢復健康。

調動自身潛力，有病治病，無病強身
太極氣功十八式是通過自己練功來調整身體內部的功能，調動人體自身的潛力，發揮人體主觀能動性，依靠自己的力量來治病強身，因此，他與其他療法截然不同，如藥物治療(包括中藥和西藥)，針灸推拿等治療，起主導作用的是藥物的性能和醫生的技巧，病人僅處於配合或從屬的地位，只能起到協同作用。但太極氣功十八式則完全不同，它是由病人自己進行和控制的。只有練功者白已充份發揮主觀能動性，有信心、有耐心、有恒心地堅持鍛鍊，正確掌握練功方法和練功原則，注意練功注意事項，才能獲得療效。由於這種療法依靠自身鍛鍊，所以他不存在藥源性副作用

description:
Taichi Fighting Application - Snake Creeps Down 🐍

Here’s a fighting application for Xia Shi or Snake Creeps Down, the taichi move where you go into a low pi bu or crouching stance and stand up. The aim of the move is to basically get your hips under your attacker, uproot him into a modified fireman’s carry and throw him off your back. This is just one approach. There are many. 

I’m a small woman (just big in my mind) and you can uproot a larger opponent without problem with the proper entry.

title: ☁️ Wolkenhände einfach erklärt ☁️ | Tai Chi Übungen für Einsteiger #taichi #taijiquan
description:
In diesem Video zeige ich dir die Wolkenhände – eine der schönsten und beruhigendsten Tai-Chi-Übungen überhaupt. ☁️

Die Wolkenhände verbinden Armbewegung, Schritte und Koordination. Für Einsteiger können diese Bewegungsabläufe am Anfang herausfordernd sein – genau deshalb erkläre ich alles ganz leicht und verständlich, Schritt für Schritt.

Wir beginnen mit eckigen, klaren Bewegungen, damit du die Struktur gut nachvollziehen kannst. Mit der Zeit wird die Bewegung immer runder, weicher und fließender, bis die Wolkenhände ihre typische meditative Qualität entfalten. 🌿

Diese Übung hilft dir, Ruhe, Zentrierung und bessere Koordination zu entwickeln – und du kannst sie wunderbar zu Hause üben.

Viel Spaß bei unseren Wolkenhänden! 🙏✨

description:
In this beginner Tai Chi routine, you’ll learn foundational movements to help you build:

• Flow and coordination  
• Breath and body connection  
• Balance and posture  
• Internal energy awareness (Qi)  
• Calm, focus, and control  

This practice includes:
• Opening and Closing Form  
• Holding the Ball  
• Single-Hand Silk Reeling  

About Master Hao Zhang (张豪) ☯️

A world champion Tai Chi practitioner and teacher, Master Hao Zhang has trained at the highest level in China and internationally. With 40+ gold medals and experience coaching elite athletes, he now shares authentic Tai Chi training with students around the world.

Tai Chi is more than movement, it’s a lifelong practice.

Originating in ancient China and practiced in over 180 countries, Tai Chi is known to improve flexibility, reduce stress, enhance breathing, and support overall well-being. It is both a martial art and a form of moving meditation that cultivates balance between body and mind.

title: Master Tony Wong Advanced Chen Style Taijiquan training - Lao Jia Yilu Pao Chui
description:
Master Tony’s advanced class learning the second form of Chen Taiji — Laojia Paochui (Cannon Fist) — along with its martial applications. 

Building upon the foundational principles of Laojia Yilu, Paochui develops explosive power known as Fa Jing through fast, dynamic, and controlled movements. The training emphasizes Song (release and relaxation) and Chan Si Jin (silk-reeling energy), teaching the body to connect internal power with sudden explosive strikes, rapid footwork, and seamless martial application. Power and softness, speed and control, all integrated into one continuous flow.

Visit Master Tony’s 黃偉兒 website www.chenfamilytaiji.com for Qigong and Taijiquan classes and workshop information.

title: Yang shi tai chi quan. Complex of 8 forms. 8式太极拳
description:
Support channel on Paypal: https://paypal.me/chinesemartialarts

#chinesemartialarts #taijiquan #wushu

Qiu Hufang under the guidance of Li Deying demonstrates short complexes of 8 Yangshi Tai Chi Chuan forms, after which Li Deying gives a detailed analysis of the forms.
Li Deing is a professor of the Beijing Wushu Association, an international wushu referee and a four-time Chinese Taijiquan champion.

Yang Style Tai Chi Quan is one of the most popular forms of Tai Chi, known for its graceful movements and health benefits. The Complex of 8 Forms is a simplified version, making it accessible for beginners while still retaining the essence of Tai Chi practice.

Benefits of Practicing the 8 Forms

- Improves Flexibility: Enhances the range of motion in joints.
- Enhances Balance: Helps in developing better stability and coordination.
- Reduces Stress: Promotes relaxation and mental clarity.
- Strengthens Muscles: Engages various muscle groups gently.
- Increases Awareness: Encourages mindfulness and body awareness.

Tips for Practicing

1. Focus on Breathing: Coordinate movements with your breath.
2. Stay Relaxed: Keep your body relaxed to avoid tension.
3. Practice Regularly: Consistency is key to mastering the forms.
4. Seek Guidance: Consider joining a class or learning from an instructor for better understanding.

title: TAI CHI CHUAN - Caminhada do Tai Chi Chuan / Tai Chi Chuan para Iniciantes
description:
O Tai Chi Chuan possui prova científica e envolve três assuntos: Saúde Física, Saúde Mental e Arte Marcial para Defesa Pessoal, porque é um Estilo do Kung Fu.
O que importa no Tai Chi Chuan é a qualidade nos movimentos e a prática constante, para o praticante adquirir maiores benefícios.
O Tai Chi Chuan pode ser praticado por todas as pessoas, independente de raça, cultura ou religião de quem pratica.
Se você dominar o Tai Chi Chuan na teoria, mas não colocar em prática, todo o seu conhecimento será apenas teórico, estando preparado (a) para dar alguns conselhos para alguns praticantes, mas não estará adquirindo os benefícios que a prática do Tai Chi Chuan oferece.
Quanto aos nomes de movimentos que serão mencionados aqui, você vai perceber que em outros lugares, poderá encontrar alguns nomes diferentes, para os mesmos movimentos. O motivo é que cada nome foi escrito de forma poética na língua chinesa e não existe uma tradução exata para os outros idiomas, mas o movimento ou padrão é o mesmo.
Ao praticar o Tai Chi Chuan, você passa a conhecer melhor os limites do corpo e começa a sentir os efeitos.
O Tai Chi Chuan é um exercício corporal criado por Grandes Mestres, para que as pessoas possam adquirir o bem-estar e vida longa.
Os praticantes criam força com movimentos suaves e aos poucos aprendem novos padrões.

Francesco Cocco è allievo diretto del gran Maestro Shin Dae Woung.

Pratica il kung fu dall’età di quattordici anni, con una formazione che abbraccia diversi stili: Kung Fu, Tai Chi, Pa Kwa, Hi Sin Ghi, uso delle 18 armi. Assieme al gran Maestro Shin Dae Woung ha compiuto diversi viaggi di formazione in Corea del Sud e in Cina, praticando e affinando l’arte del kung fu e della meditazione. Presso il Tempio del Maestro Shin Dae Woung con sede a Rapallo, ha perfezionato le diverse discipline secondo il metodo tradizionale lavoro-allenamento, seguendo anche i corsi degli insegnanti qualificati Cinture Nere del Maestro Shin Dae Woung.

Ha conseguito il diploma di insegnante qualificato della disciplina Kung Fu tramite il corso istruttori tenuto direttamente dal gran Maestro Shin Dae Woung. Ha conquistato per due volte il titolo di Primo Classificato nei campionati italiani di forma della Scuola di Kung Fu e Tai Chi Dae Woung.

title: Chen Zhonghua "No Space" Chen Style Taijiquan Practical Method AZ Workshop 2026
description:
In this video, Master Chen Zhonghua explains the Practical Method concept of "No Space"—a fundamental principle in Chen Style Taijiquan. The idea is to eliminate unnecessary gaps within your own structure and between yourself and your opponent, maintaining continuous connection, control, and efficiency throughout every movement.

By removing space, the body functions as a unified whole, allowing force to be transmitted effectively without collapse or disconnection. This principle applies to push hands, applications, and form practice, helping practitioners develop stronger structure, better control of the center, and greater functional power.

Master Chen's teaching demonstrates how subtle adjustments in alignment and connection can dramatically change the effectiveness of movement, revealing the practical nature of Taijiquan beyond choreography and technique.

Join this channel to get access to live classes and member only content:
https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCXkn1aoWW_46_4UKuI54-dg/join

title: Yang  Style Taijiquan 108 form 1 of 3
description:
Yang Style Tai  Chi 108 form, Sifu Peter Tam Hoy at  Wudangshan "BEYOND THE FORM"  to purchase complete 2 DVD set, 2 Subtitled 108 forms & Fast (Fa-Jin) Set, click on  http://taijihawaii.com/pages/contact15.html
Beyond just learning an external form or forms, a student's emphasis "must" also focus on applying the "essential" internal principles of Taijiquan as specified by the Taiji classics, such as.....sinking , roundness , using the mind and not force, applying of the "Yi" (heart/mind), "Qi" (energy) & "Shen" (spirit) in one's form. 
Mindfulness to body alignment, raising the spirit, coordinating upper and lower parts of the body, etc. is demanded.
The achievement of "stillness in motion", "moving as one" distinguishing clearly between full & empty ", gathering & releasing, opening & closing and making the energy flow evenly thru all the meridian channels is always the challenge for everyone regardless of level..
This discipline of "internal work" with careful" intention" and "attention to detail" with consistent practice over an extended period is required if real benefit and progress is to be expected.
The rewards are well worth the effort.
Shirfu Peter Tam-Hoy
Tai Chi in Hawaii
Taijihawaii.com

title: Tai Chi Chuan. Complex of 42 forms Jian sword. 42式太极剑上
description:
Support channel on PayPal: https://paypal.me/chinesemartialarts

#chinesemartialarts #taijiquan #wushu

A complex of Taijiquan 42 forms with a Jian sword is demonstrated, with a detailed analysis. Performed by Chen Sitan, explained by Li Daeying.
Li Deing is a professor of the Beijing Wushu Association, an international wushu referee and a four-time Chinese champion in Taijiquan, the author of a book on Xin and Quan.
Chen Sitan is the winner of the men's champion title at the World Taijiquan Championship, the eleventh Asian champion in Taijiquan, the title of "King of Taiji", and is one of the top 100 fighters in Chinese martial arts.
The Tai Chi Chuan 42 Sword Form is indeed a magnificent blend of grace and precision! Here’s a bit more about it:
Overview of Tai Chi Chuan 42 Sword Form
- Combination of Arts: Merges the flowing movements of Tai Chi with the sharp techniques of the Jian sword.
- Purpose: Often performed in competitions and demonstrations, showcasing both skill and artistry.
Practice Tips
- Consistency: Regular practice will help in mastering the form.
- Focus on Breathing: Synchronizing your breath with movements enhances fluidity and control.
- Visualization: Picture the movements in your mind to better prepare for physical practice.

description:
The 8 stances that appear in the YMAA version of the Yang-style Taijiquan Long Form (108 Form):

Mabu (馬步) - Horse Stance
Deng Shen Bu (登山步) - Mountain Climbing Stance
Si Liu Bu (四六步) - Four Six Stance
Xu Bu (虛步) or Xuan Ji Bu (玄機步) - False Stance
Jin Ji Du Li (金雞獨立) - Golden Rooster Stance
Fu Hu Bu (伏虎步) - Tame the Tiger Stance
Qi Lin Bu (麒麟步) - Unicorn Stance (similar to transition)
Tun Bu (吞步) - Swallow Stance

title: Taijiquan Classics Unlocked   |   Chinese Swordsmanship's Hidden Principles Explained
description:
What do the Taijiquan Classics actually tell us about Chinese swordsmanship? Filmed at the House of China in San Diego, this deep Q&A with Scott M. Rodell unpacks the literary tradition of the Taijiquan Classics and applies it directly to Jianfa and Daofa — Chinese sword practice. Topics covered include the solitary predator mindset, why the body must move as one piece from the waist, the non-dualistic nature of Chinese swordsmanship, substantial and insubstantial (xū and kōng), and the essential principle that receiving is releasing and releasing is receiving. The duifang (对方) tells you what technique to use — but only if you're listening.
#Jianfa #Daofa #Taijiquan #ChineseSwordsmanship #TaijiquanClassics

description:

For the counterfeit to exist, then so must the real.

Adam Mizner training his inner door students with real Chi / Qi skills of Taijiquan!

This is what it is like to be fortunate enough to be an inner door student of a highly skilled Taijiquan teacher. Direct transmission of coveted Qi / Chi skills. In this case, Fa Fang / Fa Jin with Shen Yi Qi creating elastic strength, full body power and separating balance from power. a little too wowoo for a lot of folks who claim to know the internal arts, I guess. Well, skill speaks for itself.

More at Snapshots at:

https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL-vV-5dJicdIpYDRMoSwcPDWsDJNe0RGx

If you can't wrap your head around what's going on, that's because it is fake woowoo, so please move on.

If you still care to understand what is going on, there are few qualified teachers who are good at teaching this stuff. Please seek them out.

If you know exactly what is going on, congratulations! Your sincerity, hard work, perseverance and commitment has paid off. Would love to hear from you!

Some folks are known to hallucinate all kinds of conspiracy theories about what is going on with this type of technology. May God have mercy upon your souls.

Om Namah Shivaya!

title: 7º Seminário de Taijiquan | Sanshou com o Mestre Chen Ziqiang
description:
Neste vídeo, registramos um dos encontros do 7º Seminário de Taijiquan, dedicado ao Sǎnshǒu (散手) — espaço de investigação e prática da dimensão marcial do Taijiquan.

O treinamento de Sǎnshǒu é compreendido aqui como luta em sentido amplo, onde os princípios e fundamentos cultivados nas rotinas (taolu) se manifestam na relação com o outro. Estrutura, enraizamento, escuta, tempo, distância e intenção entram em diálogo vivo, permitindo experimentar o Taijiquan para além da forma, sem romper com sua tradição.

Mais do que aplicações isoladas, o Sǎnshǒu propõe a experimentação consciente do método, preservando o caráter investigativo e relacional do treinamento tradicional de combate.

A Escola Chenjiagou de Taijiquan Brasil é coordenada pelo Professor Gil Rodrigues, discípulo direto do Mestre Chen Ziqiang e representante da 21ª geração da família Chen, que segue comprometido com a preservação, o estudo e a transmissão responsável dessa linhagem no Brasil.

title: 晨起三个动作，锻炼全身。做完精神足，一天
description:
太極是一種古老的中國武術，它有時也被稱作「太極拳」或是「運動式冥想」。它由一系列不同姿勢、並以緩慢動作交會相連組成
據說，太極有利於心臟健康。在最近一項研究中，(（研究人員）在把太極和快走進行比較後發現，太極在降低血壓以及其他心臟病危險因素方面、比如血糖水平和膽固醇，效果明顯更好。
太極還可能對免疫系統有益。在加利福尼亞的一項研究中，研究人員讓一半參與者習練太極，另一半則參加健康教育課程。他們每個人都接種了帶狀皰疹疫苗。但9個星期以後，那組練習太極的學員免疫力是另一組學員的兩倍。

此外，太極組學員在身體機能、活力、精神健康以及減少身體疼痛方面也表現出更大的改善。

title: Stage de Tai Chi Chuan style Chen dans le Morbihan  #morbihan #taichichuan #taijiquan #ambon
description:
Plongez au cœur du stage d'octobre avec ce montage vidéo qui montre le groupe en pleine pratique du Tai Chi Chuan style Chen. 

Sous la guidance experte de Guillaume Large, enseignant 5ème Duan, les pratiquants explorent en harmonie les mouvements et postures de cet art martial interne. Une belle démonstration de partage, de concentration et d'énergie collective.

Guillaume Large, disciple direct du maître Chen Ziqiang, enseigne dans différentes communes sur Bordeaux et les alentours.

Nous avons eu la chance de le recevoir les 5 et 6 octobre 2024 à Ambon dans le Morbihan, près de Muzillac.

Ce stage a été organisé par l'association Eiqilibre, qui propose des cours de Tai Chi Chuan axés sur la recherche d'équilibre, d'énergie (Qi) et de sérénité. Rejoignez-nous pour découvrir cet art martial ancestral, accessible à tous, quel que soit votre niveau.

title: Tai chi chuan - Aula 3 | Movimento de ataque
description:
Tai Chi Chuan para iniciantes Aula 3 foca no movimento de ataque frontal com a palma, um exercício essencial de rotação de cintura e fluidez para quem busca os benefícios marciais e terapêuticos.

📌 Corpo leve e com energia em 7 dias?
➡️Comece seu Desafio aqui: https://andreleung.com.br/desafio7dias/

Neste vídeo, você aprenderá o Tai Chi Chuan para iniciantes através da técnica da Palma de Ataque. Se você busca melhorar sua coordenação motora, fortalecer as pernas através da base Mabu (Base do Cavalo) ou ganhar mobilidade no tronco, esta Aula 3 detalha como projetar a energia para frente de forma relaxada e contínua. Como fisioterapeuta, preparei este tutorial para que você entenda que o ataque no Tai Chi não depende de força bruta, mas sim do uso correto da cintura e da intenção mental (Qi), promovendo o equilíbrio físico e a saúde.

O QUE VOCÊ VAI APRENDER NESTA AULA 3:

👣 Base Mabu Avançada: Como montar a base do cavalo com passos coordenados (45º) para garantir que seu tronco fique sempre de frente para o objetivo.

✋ Técnica da Palma (Ataque): O segredo de "puxar o elástico" — como relaxar o punho e empurrar sem colocar tensão excessiva nos ombros ou braços.

🔄 O Poder da Cintura: Entenda como a rotação do tronco é o motor que impulsiona o braço, evitando movimentos rígidos e melhorando a circulação energética.

🧘 Foco e Mira: Aprenda a alinhar seu ataque na altura do coração/ombro, utilizando o espelho como ferramenta para ajustar a geometria do seu corpo.

🧠 Paciência no Movimento Lento: Como a execução devagar recruta mais musculatura e treina a concentração, transformando o exercício em uma meditação ativa.

POR QUE ESTE MOVIMENTO É FUNDAMENTAL?
Muitos acreditam que o Tai Chi para iniciantes é apenas defensivo, mas entender o movimento de ataque é crucial para dominar a alternância entre ceder e empurrar. Este exercício fortalece as coxas, queima calorias pela resistência da base e retifica a postura ao exigir a contração constante do abdômen. É uma prática completa de vitalidade, acessível mesmo para quem está começando agora.

QUER DESTRAVAR SEU CORPO E TER VITALIDADE TODOS OS DIAS?

Conheça o "Desafio 7 Dias"!
Meu programa de entrada com o passo a passo ideal para iniciantes que buscam resultados rápidos e um corpo mais solto com apenas 10 minutos de prática diária.

title: Tai Chi- Odnajdź równowagę, rozmowa z  mistrzem Markiem Balińskim (Akademia Chen Taijiquan)
description:
Tai chi does not mean oriental wisdom or something exotic. It is the wisdom of your own senses, your own mind and body together as one process.

Chungliang Al Huang


Taijiquan to najbardziej rozpoznawalna i powszechnie uprawiana chińska sztuka walki (wushu/gongfu) zaliczana do tzw. stylów wewnętrznych (neijia) i integracji ciała i umysłu; to obszerny i kompleksowy systemem, który jak żaden inny, umożliwia ludziom w każdym wieku na wszechstronny rozwój. Posiada walory zdrowotne, rehabilitacyjne, oraz poprawiające kondycję fizyczną i psychiczną. 

Twórca taijiquan – Chen Wangting (XVII w.), będąc wysokiej rangi wojskowym, połączył rodzinną sztukę walki i samoobrony, doświadczenie wojenne, wiedzę klasyków z zakresu sztuk wojennych, ze zdrowotnymi ćwiczeniami dao yin oraz koncepcją meridianów medycyny chińskiej. Cały system oparł filozofii równowagi i mądrościach zawartych w księdze przemian Yijing.

Po pojawieniu się broni palnej znaczenie bojowe chińskich sztuk walki, w tym taijiquan, straciło na znaczeniu. Zyskały natomiast, pozostałe jego adpekty: sportowy, zdrowotny i filozoficzno-duchowy.

title: Wooden movable-type printing of China
description:
UNESCO: List of Intangible Cultural Heritage in Need of Urgent Safeguarding - 2010
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/USL/00322
Description: One of the world's oldest printing techniques, wooden movable-type printing is maintained in Rui'an County, Zhejiang Province, where it is used in compiling and printing clan genealogies. Men are trained to draw and engrave Chinese characters, which are then set into a type-page and printed. This requires abundant historical knowledge and mastery of ancient Chinese grammar. Women then undertake the work of paper cutting and binding, until the printed genealogies are finished. The movable characters can be used time and again after the type-page is dismantled. Throughout the year, craftspeople carry sets of wooden characters and printing equipment to ancestral halls in local communities. There, they compile and print the clan genealogy by hand. A ceremony marks the completion of the genealogy, and the printers place it into a locked box to be preserved. The techniques of wooden movable-type printing are transmitted through families by rote and word of mouth. However, the intensive training required, the low income generated, popularization of computer printing technology and diminishing enthusiasm for compiling genealogies have all contributed to a rapid decrease in the number of craftspeople. At present, only eleven people over 50 years of age remain who have mastered the whole set of techniques. If not safeguarded, this traditional practice will soon disappear.
Country(ies): China
© 2009 Culture Ministry

title: Movable-Type Printing | The Timeless Innovation of Ancient China
description:
✨ Movable-type printing, one of China's Four Great Inventions, revolutionized traditional printing methods and opened new avenues for knowledge dissemination. Invented by Bi Sheng during the 11th century in the Northern Song Dynasty, this groundbreaking technique laid the foundation for modern printing and left an indelible mark on human civilization.

Unique Features of Movable-Type Printing

🌟 Diverse Materials
Early types were made of ceramic, later evolving into wood and metal, catering to various printing needs.

🌟 Modular Design
Each character was carved on individual blocks, allowing flexibility in arrangement and reuse, enhancing efficiency and cost-effectiveness.

🌟 Efficiency
Compared to block printing, movable type dramatically increased production speed and reduced costs, fostering widespread access to literature.

🌟 Versatile Applications
From classic texts to business documents, movable type provided a practical solution for mass text reproduction.

Cultural and Historical Significance
🌟 Knowledge Accessibility
By making books cheaper and faster to produce, movable-type printing enabled broader access to knowledge and culture.

🌟 Global Influence
This invention served as a vital medium for spreading ideas, technologies, and cultural values worldwide.

🌟 A Foundational Contribution
Movable-type printing provided the technical groundwork for modern printing, continuing to inspire innovation.

Modern-Day Relevance
Despite the rise of advanced printing technologies, movable-type printing remains a cherished intangible cultural heritage, celebrated for its:

✨ Cultural Showcase: As an educational tool, it offers hands-on experiences in traditional craftsmanship.
✨ Skill Preservation: Teaching and demonstration ensure that this ancient art is passed down to future generations.
✨ Artistic Value: Many artists incorporate movable type into contemporary designs, breathing new life into this timeless craft.

A Legacy of Innovation
Movable-type printing is not just a technique but a legacy of creativity, intellectual pursuit, and cultural exchange. It continues to inspire us to value knowledge, cherish tradition, and innovate for the future.

title: Living Heritage: Woodblock Printing
description:
Delicate strokes, carved onto wood, with ultimate precision. This is #woodblockprinting, an ancient #printing technique that propelled human civilization.

As Buddhism prevailed in China during the Tang Dynasty (618-907), there was a strong need to produce a large amount of Buddhist scriptures, and copying by hand could not meet the rising demand. Ancient Chinese craftsmen thus came up with a novel way to mass produce printed works. Hence came the prototype of woodblock printing.

Traditional woodblock printing can be divided into four major steps: writing, engraving, printing, and binding. With each step then sub-divided into several procedures, it takes roughly 30 steps to produce a woodblock print.

Carving lies at the center of woodblock printing as this arduous step can make or break the final print. Characters and images are carved to produce raised areas or lines that will eventually apply ink to paper. It calls for a pair of skilled hands.

A five-meter-scroll of the Buddhist scripture Diamond Sutra, printed in 868, is the "earliest dated printed book" in the words of the British Library where it is stored. It is just one example of ancient works of art that not only tell the wisdoms of our predecessors, but also is a testimony to the pursuit of beauty by Chinese craftsmen throughout centuries.

The invention of writing gave life to great thoughts, but it is the invention of printing that made knowledge a shareable fruit for all humankind. #Chineseculture

title: Did Ancient China Invent Printing 700 Years Before Europe? (The Shocking Truth)
description:
Ever wondered where the printing press really came from? Most people think of Gutenberg, but the true story goes much, much deeper, back to ancient China! In this short, we uncover the incredible history of printing, starting with woodblock printing in the Tang Dynasty around 700 AD. This groundbreaking invention allowed for mass production of books centuries before Europe.

Discover how China was even using bronze movable type by the 12th century. This video explores the often-overlooked contributions of Chinese ingenuity that reshaped how we learn and connect.
