title: The Hard Part of Inventing The Printing Press
description:
The invention of the printing press in the 15th century revolutionized the world by enabling mass production of written material. Its impact on communication, education, and the dissemination of knowledge was profound. However, the development of this groundbreaking technology was not without its challenges.

One of the major hurdles was the creation of movable type, a system where individual characters could be arranged and rearranged to form words and sentences. Metal typefaces had to be crafted with precision and uniformity, allowing for efficient printing. This required the mastery of metalworking techniques and the creation of durable alloys that could withstand the pressure of the printing process.

In addition to the technological aspects, chemical advancements were essential. The invention of oil-based inks with the right viscosity and drying properties was crucial. Printers had to experiment with various pigments, binders, and drying agents to achieve optimal results.  Another hurdle was developing the. right metal to be used for the typeblocks. The metal needed to be soft enough to be easily melted and molted, but durable enough to withstand the pressures of the printing process.

Overcoming these challenges required the ingenuity and collaboration of inventors, craftsmen, chemists, and scholars.

title: Woodblock & Movable Type: How China Invented the Printing Revolution
description:
🖨️ Printing in Ancient China — From Woodblock to Movable Type
The written word scaled through innovation — how Chinese printing changed human history.

📝 Blog-Style Overview
Long before Gutenberg revolutionized printing in Europe, China had already pioneered printing technology — first with woodblock printing, and later with movable type. These early innovations made the mass production of texts possible and dramatically increased the spread of knowledge, culture, and governance in East Asia.

Both woodblock printing (7th century CE) and movable type printing (11th century CE) laid the foundation for modern publishing and global literacy.

🪵 Woodblock Printing (Tang Dynasty, 7th Century)
In woodblock printing:

Entire pages were carved into wooden blocks (mirror-image)

Ink was applied, and the block was pressed onto paper, silk, or fabric

It was used for books, religious texts, and government documents

🔖 Oldest known example:
The Diamond Sutra, printed in 868 CE, is the world’s oldest surviving printed book with a date.

Advantages:

Durable and reusable blocks

Efficient for mass-copying texts
Limitations:

Labor-intensive to carve each page

Time-consuming to revise or correct errors

🧱 Movable Type Printing (Song Dynasty, c. 1040 CE)
Invented by Bi Sheng, movable type printing used individual characters (initially clay, later metal or wood) that could be rearranged to print multiple pages.

Advantages:

Faster typesetting for lengthy works

Reusable characters

Easier error correction
Challenges:

Thousands of Chinese characters made organizing and using type difficult

Less efficient in China than in alphabet-based systems like Latin

Still, movable type printing flourished in Korea (where metal movable type was perfected) and had a significant influence on later global developments in print technology.

🌍 Global Impact
Laid groundwork for the mass production of books

Enabled wider literacy, education, and bureaucratic administration in East Asia

Spread to the Islamic world and eventually influenced European printing

Became a key precursor to the Gutenberg press (1440s)

title: The History of Printing : The world's first metal movable type and Renaissance
description:
A short history of Printing from woodblock printing to 3D with the first metal movable type printing in Korea. The history of printing is a fascinating journey that has undergone significant technological advancements over the centuries. Here are the main events of the printing history.

1. Woodblock Printing (200 AD): Originating in China, woodblock printing involved carving characters or images onto wooden blocks, applying ink, and transferring them onto paper or fabric.
2. Moveable Type (1040 AD): Invented by Bi Sheng in China, moveable type allowed individual characters to be rearranged and reused, facilitating the printing process.
3. Korean Metal Movable Type (13th Century): Korea made significant contributions with the invention of metal movable type. Choe Yun-ui created durable characters from metal, enhancing the efficiency and longevity of moveable type printing.
4. Gutenberg Printing Press (1440): Johannes Gutenberg introduced the mechanical movable-type printing press in Europe. This innovation marked the beginning of the mass production of books, significantly reducing the cost of producing written material.
5. Incunabula (15th Century): Refers to books printed before 1501, a period that witnessed the early stages of printing technology.
6. Copperplate Engraving (Late 15th Century): A technique involving engraved copperplates to print illustrations alongside text, enhancing the visual appeal of printed materials.
7. Industrial Revolution (18th-19th Centuries): Technological advancements, such as steam-powered printing presses and improvements in paper manufacturing, led to increased printing efficiency and lowered costs.
8. Lithography (1796): Alois Senefelder invented lithography, a method of printing on a flat surface using a chemical process. This technique allowed for high-quality image reproduction.
9. Offset Printing (1875): Developed by Robert Barclay, offset printing separated the inked image from the printing surface, leading to more efficient and high-speed printing.
10. Linotype Machine (1886): Invented by Ottmar Mergenthaler, the Linotype machine automated the typesetting process by casting entire lines of type in a single piece.
11. Phototypesetting (20th Century): Replaced traditional metal type with photographic techniques, improving the speed and flexibility of typesetting.
12. Digital Printing (Late 20th Century): The advent of computers and digital technology revolutionized printing, allowing for direct reproduction from digital files without the need for physical typesetting.
13. 3D Printing (1980s Onward): A revolutionary technology that enables the creation of three-dimensional objects layer by layer, transforming various industries, including manufacturing and healthcare.
14. Inkjet and Laser Printing (Late 20th Century): Popular methods for home and office printing, offering high-quality and fast printing capabilities.
15. E-books and Online Publishing (21st Century): The rise of digital platforms has shifted the focus from traditional print to electronic formats, impacting the publishing industry.

title: 活字印刷術 (Movable type)
description:
Movable type（是印刷和排版的系統和技術） 世界上第一個用於紙質書籍的活字印刷技術是由瓷器材料製成的，由中國發明家畢昇於公元 1040 年左右在中國北宋發明。最早印有錢幣識別碼的金屬活字印刷紙幣是宋代1161年製造的。1193年，宋代一本書記載了銅活字的使用方法。現存最古老的金屬活字印刷書籍《直指》於 1377 年在朝鮮印刷高麗王朝。⁣
⁣
Movable type is the system and technology of printing and typography 。The world's first movable type printing technology for paper books was made of porcelain materials and was invented around AD 1040 in China during the Northern Song dynasty by the inventor Bi Sheng 。The earliest printed paper money with movable metal type to print the identifying code of the money was made in 1161 during the Song dynasty. In 1193, a book in the Song dynasty documented how to use the copper movable type.The oldest extant book printed with movable metal type, Jikji, was printed in Korea in 1377 during the Goryeo dynasty.⁣

title: Movable type printing - Song dynasty, inventor Bi Sheng
description:
Movable-type printing
Block printing had existed in China since at least the Tang dynasty, but the system of printing was made more convenient, popular and accessible under the Song. The early process involved a primitive system where words or shapes were carved onto wooden blocks, while ink was applied on the surface. The printing was fixed and a whole new board had to be made for different designs.

In 1040 AD, during the Song dynasty, inventor Bi Sheng came up with the ‘movable-type printing’ system. This ingenious development involved the use of single tiles made of clay for common characters which were placed in order within an iron frame. Once the characters were set close together the result was one solid block of type. Over the years the use of clay to create the tiles was changed to wood and later metal.

title: The first printing machine that changed the world is being rebuilt! 🖨️😮
description: You might think this person is making a cake, but they are actually recreating the world’s first movable type printing press, developed by Bi Sheng in the 11th century. The process begins with clay characters, crafted as delicately as fine coins, which are then fired to harden. Bi Sheng used a metal plate coated with pine resin, wax, and paper ash to print. The characters were arranged on this plate within an iron frame to form a sturdy printing block. After being gently heated, the block was leveled with a wooden tool and prepared for printing. With reusable characters, this method revolutionized printing, making it faster and more economical.

title: China traditional printing_movable type printing technology
description:
Movable Type Printing

　　Block Printing was a costly and time-consuming process, for each carved block could only be used for a specific page of a particular book, besides, a single mistake in carving could ruin the whole block. However movable type changed all of that.

　　In the Song Dynasty （960-1279）, a man named Bi Sheng carved individual characters on identical pieces of fine clay. Each piece of movable type had on it one Chinese character which was carved in relief on a small block of moistened clay. After the block had been hardened by fire, the type became hard and durable and could be used wherever required. The pieces of movable type could be glued to an iron plate and easily detached from the plate. Each piece of character could be assembled to print a page and then broken up and redistributed as needed. When the printing was finished, the pieces were put away for future use.

　　By the year1000, paged books in the modern style had replaced scrolls. Two color printing （black and red） was seen as early as 1340.

　　This technology then spread to Korea, Japan, Vietnam and Europe. Later, German Johann Gutenberg invented movable type made of metal in the 1440s. Movable Type Printing developed very fast. Based on clay type, type made of wood, lead, tin and copper gradually appeared.

title: China's Creations Captivate! Pioneering #paper , Groundbreaking #gunpowder , & Powerful #printing g
description:
China has a rich history of innovation and has contributed significantly to the development of human civilization through its groundbreaking inventions. Some of the most notable Chinese inventions include paper, the compass, gunpowder, and printing.

Paper: Invented around 105 AD by a Chinese official named Cai Lun, paper was a revolutionary development that transformed communication and record-keeping. Prior to the invention of paper, people used various materials such as bamboo, silk, and papyrus for writing. Paper, made from mulberry bark and other plant fibers, was easier to produce, more portable, and more affordable than previous materials. The technique of papermaking spread from China to the Arab world and eventually to Europe, where it played a crucial role in the proliferation of knowledge through books and written records.

The compass: The compass was invented in China during the Han Dynasty (202 BC - 220 AD) as a divination tool. Initially, it was a lodestone (a naturally magnetized iron ore) that aligned itself with the Earth's magnetic field, pointing to the magnetic north and south. Later, the Chinese developed the magnetic needle compass, which provided more accurate navigation. This invention was essential for advancements in seafaring and exploration, enabling navigators to determine their direction even on cloudy days or at night. The compass eventually spread to the Arab world and Europe, revolutionizing navigation and facilitating global trade and exploration.

Gunpowder: Gunpowder was discovered in China during the Tang Dynasty (618 - 907 AD) while alchemists were searching for an elixir of immortality. It is a mixture of saltpeter, sulfur, and charcoal. Initially used for fireworks, the Chinese soon recognized its potential for military applications, creating weapons like rockets, cannons, and guns. The knowledge of gunpowder spread to the Arab world and Europe, changing the nature of warfare and leading to the development of modern weaponry.

Printing: The invention of printing in China can be traced back to the Tang Dynasty (618 - 907 AD). The earliest known form of printing is woodblock printing, where individual characters were carved onto wooden blocks and then inked and pressed onto paper or cloth. This method allowed for the mass production of texts and played a significant role in spreading knowledge and culture. Later, during the Song Dynasty (960 - 1279 AD), Bi Sheng invented the first known movable type printing, which used individual clay or ceramic type pieces that could be rearranged for different texts. This innovation allowed for even greater efficiency in printing and contributed to the global spread of knowledge, ideas, and culture.

These inventions from China have had a profound and lasting impact on human civilization, shaping the course of history and laying the foundation for modern advancements in communication, navigation, warfare, and knowledge dissemination.

title: First Metal Movable Type
description:
First Metal Movable Type
----------------------------------
The transition from wood type to metal type occurred in 1234 during the Goryeo Dynasty of Korea and is credited to Choe Yun-ui. A set of ritual books, Sangjeong Gogeum Yemun was printed with the movable metal type in 1234. The oldest extant movable metal print book is the Jikji, printed in Korea in 1377. The techniques for bronze casting, used at the time for making coins (as well as bells and statues) were adapted to making metal type. The following description of the Korean font casting process was recorded by the Joseon dynasty scholar Seong Hyeon (성현, 成俔, 1439--1504): At first, one cuts letters in beech wood. One fills a trough level with fine sandy [clay] of the reed-growing seashore. Wood-cut letters are pressed into the sand, then the impressions become negative and form letters [molds]. At this step, placing one trough together with another, one pours the molten bronze down into an opening. The fluid flows in, filling these negative molds, one by one becoming type. Lastly, one scrapes and files off the irregularities, and piles them up to be arranged.
A potential solution to the linguistic and cultural bottleneck that held back movable type in Korea for 200 years appeared in the early 15th century—a generation before Gutenberg would begin working on his own movable-type invention in Europe—when King Sejong the Great devised a simplified alphabet of 24 characters (hangul) for use by the common people, which could have made the typecasting and compositing process more feasible. Adoption of the new alphabet was stifled by the Korea's cultural elite, who were "...appalled at the idea of losing Chinese, the badge of their elitism." Proliferation of movable type was also obstructed by a "Confucian prohibition on the commercialization of printing" restricted the distribution of books produced using the new method to the government. The technique was restricted to use by the royal foundry for official state publications only, where the focus was on reprinting Chinese classics lost in 1126 when Korea's libraries and palaces had perished in a conflict between dynasties.

For more information, you can visit:

- http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metal_movable_type#Metal_movable_type
- http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jikji

title: From Mud to Magic: See Ancient Printing in Action!
description:
Before information was instant, it was intimate—shaped by the patient hand of a master craftsman who gave physical form to our thoughts.

This video is a tribute to the forgotten artisans behind one of history's most vital inventions: Chinese movable type printing.

We explore the unseen labor and profound discipline required to turn raw wood into immortal words, celebrating a legacy built not on speed, but on soul.

📜 Step-by-Step: The Artisan's Touch, Revived

The Carver's Hand
A block of wood, a sharp blade, and absolute focus. Each character is a sculpture, carved in reverse with a meditative precision that leaves no room for error.

The Scholar's Eye
Thousands of characters, each a universe of meaning, are sorted by hand into a complex system. This requires the memory of a scholar and the organizational mind of a librarian.

The Setter's Rhythm
Like a musician finding notes, the typesetter plucks characters from the tray, arranging them into lines of text. It's a dance of muscle memory and deep concentration.

The Printer's Judgment
Applying the ink is an act of balance. Too much, and the words will blur; too little, and they will fade. The printer’s eye judges the perfect, even coat.

The Moment of Pressure
Paper meets wood in an act of trust. A firm, even pressure is applied, transferring the ink and the artisan’s effort from the physical block to the waiting page.

The Legacy Unveiled
The paper is peeled back. In that quiet moment, the artisan’s spirit is immortalized on the page, ready to speak across a thousand years.

🖋️ This isn’t just about a machine; it’s about human mastery.
It’s a testament to the idea that the most profound technologies are often powered by the quiet dedication of a single person.

🎥 This film is a proud entry in the #100YoungIntangibleHeritageProject (#百young非遗计划), celebrating the hands that built our literary heritage.

📌 Movable type didn’t just print books; it enshrined the skill of the craftsman in every page. It reminds us that knowledge has a human touch.

👉 Subscribe to honor the masters of the past and the timeless skills they perfected.

Witness the meticulous *printing process* of ancient blocks, starting with raw clay. The *carving* and shaping, followed by a lengthy hardening period, showcases *ancient technology* at its finest. See how these *ceramics* are used to create beautiful prints, reminiscent of the *renaissance* era.

title: China engraved block printing technique
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00229
Description: The traditional China engraved block printing technique requires the collaboration of half a dozen craftspeople possessed of printing expertise, dexterity and team spirit. The blocks themselves, made from the fine-grained wood of pear or jujube trees, are cut to a thickness of two centimetres and polished with sandpaper to prepare them for engraving. Drafts of the desired images are brushed onto extremely thin paper and scrutinized for errors before they are transferred onto blocks. The inked designs provide a guide for the artisan who cuts the picture or design into the wood, producing raised characters that will eventually apply ink to paper. First, though, the blocks are tested with red and then blue ink and corrections are made to the carving. Finally, when the block is ready to be used, it is covered with ink and pressed by hand onto paper to print the final image. Block engraving may be used to print books in a variety of traditional styles, to create modern books with conventional binding, or to reproduce ancient Chinese books. A number of printing workshops continue this handicraft today thanks to the knowledge and skills of the expert artisans.

title: The Origin of the Movable Metal Typeㅣ Part1-Gutenberg: Inventor or Innovator?
description:
The Origin of the Movable Metal Type
Part1-Gutenberg: Inventor or Innovator?


Director: Nam Yun-seong

Production: MBC Chungbuk, Korea  
Production date: December 2003 

What is Jikji?

Jikji, the oldest existing book printed with metal printing types, was published in Cheongju city in 1377 which was 78 years earlier than German inventor Gutenberg’s printing. Jikji, a worldwide cultural heritage, is currently the France National Library’s collection.


JThe content of Jikji was first written by Goryeo Dynasty's renowned Buddhist's monk Baekunhwasang and his principles "Seokchak" and "Daljam" transcribed it into metal print in 1377. 'Jikji' is not available in the republic of Korea , but the National Library in France retains the 2nd volume of the original version.


**The value of Jikji**
Dating 78 years back from Gutenberg's '42-line bible', the western hemisphere's proud tradition, Jikji is the manifestation of the advanced printing technology and flourishing publishing culture in late Goryeo Dynasty.


**‘Jikji’,’UNESCO’s Memory of the World**
UNOSCO's a UN organization whose main objective is to contribute to peace and security in the world by promoting collaboration among nations through education, science, culture and communication.UNESCO instituted "Memory of the World Program" to protect and preserve "written legacies." In September 4, 2001, Jikji is listed on Memory of the World program.

title: How the Gutenberg Bible Was Made
description:
#gutenberg  #gutenbergpress #gutenbergbible #print  #printmaking  #printdesign  #printisnotdead  #printing  #historyofprinting #educationaltechnology  #educational  

Widely considered the most important invention between 1000-2000 AD, Johann Gutenberg’s printing press quite literally revolutionized the world.

👀 But why would a printing press be so important???

Well, between 1400-1450 roughly 20,000-50,000 books were produced across all of Europe.

Gutenberg finished his press c1450.

Then from 1450-1500 there were (wait for it…) over 8 MILLION books produced. Some estimates even suggest up to 20 million copies depending whether you count pamphlets and smaller works. 🤯

Thanks to this hot new toy on the market, information was able to spread like wildfire. Begetting massive historical events like the Renaissance, the Reformation, and the Scientific Revolution.

👏 A ginormous thank you to our dear friends at @printmuseum for letting us come and film with them. They are an excellent museum with wonderful people and highly worth visiting!

If you are interested in learning more about how Gutenberg invented the type for this machine. We will have a video on that shortly 👀

title: Ancient Lithography
description: Ancient lithography or printing - Printing in East Asia originated in China, evolving from ink rubbings made on paper or cloth from texts on stone tablets, used during the sixth century.A type of printing called mechanical woodblock printing on paper started in China during the 7th century in the Tang dynasty.The use of woodblock printing spread throughout East Asia. As recorded in 1088 by Shen Kuo in his Dream Pool Essays, the Chinese artisan Bi Sheng invented an early form of movable type using clay and wood pieces arranged and organized for written Chinese characters. The use of metal movable type was known in Korea by the 13th century during the Goryeo period,with the world's oldest surviving printed book using moveable metal type being from 1377 in Korea.

title: Archaeologists unearth 1,600 movable types from early Joseon Dynasty

공평동서 조선금속활자 1천600점…가장 오래된 한글활자도 나와

Local archaeologists have discovered 16-hundred movable metal printing types that date back 600 years.
The Cultural Heritage Administration says the collection, found buried in Seoul's Jongno-gu District, is the largest collection ever to be found in South Korea.
The movable types date back to the early Joseon Dynasty.
Archaeologists also unearthed other artifacts, including an astronomical clock and copper pieces from an automatic water clock.

title: From Mud to Magic: See Ancient Printing in Action!
description:
Before information was instant, it was intimate—shaped by the patient hand of a master craftsman who gave physical form to our thoughts.

This video is a tribute to the forgotten artisans behind one of history's most vital inventions: Chinese movable type printing.

We explore the unseen labor and profound discipline required to turn raw wood into immortal words, celebrating a legacy built not on speed, but on soul.

📜 Step-by-Step: The Artisan's Touch, Revived

The Carver's Hand
A block of wood, a sharp blade, and absolute focus. Each character is a sculpture, carved in reverse with a meditative precision that leaves no room for error.

The Scholar's Eye
Thousands of characters, each a universe of meaning, are sorted by hand into a complex system. This requires the memory of a scholar and the organizational mind of a librarian.

The Setter's Rhythm
Like a musician finding notes, the typesetter plucks characters from the tray, arranging them into lines of text. It's a dance of muscle memory and deep concentration.

The Printer's Judgment
Applying the ink is an act of balance. Too much, and the words will blur; too little, and they will fade. The printer’s eye judges the perfect, even coat.

The Moment of Pressure
Paper meets wood in an act of trust. A firm, even pressure is applied, transferring the ink and the artisan’s effort from the physical block to the waiting page.

The Legacy Unveiled
The paper is peeled back. In that quiet moment, the artisan’s spirit is immortalized on the page, ready to speak across a thousand years.

🖋️ This isn’t just about a machine; it’s about human mastery.
It’s a testament to the idea that the most profound technologies are often powered by the quiet dedication of a single person.

🎥 This film is a proud entry in the #100YoungIntangibleHeritageProject (#百young非遗计划), celebrating the hands that built our literary heritage.

📌 Movable type didn’t just print books; it enshrined the skill of the craftsman in every page. It reminds us that knowledge has a human touch.

👉 Subscribe to honor the masters of the past and the timeless skills they perfected.

Witness the meticulous **printing process** of ancient blocks, starting with raw clay. The **carving** and shaping, followed by a lengthy hardening period, showcases **ancient technology** at its finest. See how these **ceramics** are used to create beautiful prints, reminiscent of the **renaissance** era.

title: Panizzi Lecture 1: Woodblock Publishing in China’s First Age of Print
description:
In this first of three Panizzi lectures, Professor Cynthia Brokaw explores why woodblock printing or xylography, remained, throughout China’s pre-modern history, the major print technology.

The lecture will consider what advantages xylography enjoyed over movable type as a technology for reproduction of Chinese texts. It will also discuss what woodblock-printed texts looked like, and survey the development of printing from its origins in the seventh to eighth centuries through the 14th century, China’s first age of print.

Cynthia Brokaw is Professor of History and East Asian Studies and Chen Family Professor of Chinese Studies at Brown University, where she teaches early modern Chinese history. Her research focuses on the social history of publishing and book culture in China, with particular attention to the relationship between the growth of commercial publishing and the development of a large commoner reading public in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

title: Turning Mud Into A Printing Press | 2000 Year Old Chinese Technique
description:
Did you know that China invented printing over 1000 years before the printing press existed in Europe, and they did it using nothing but clay, ink and bare hands? 🖨️ Watch this incredible ancient craftsmanship!

title: I tried movable type printing today. 1,000 years old. Still done by hand.
description:
The master sets each character one by one. Carved wood, ink, pressure. You pick your own words, arrange them yourself, roll the ink, press the paper. My first pull? Smudged. Second pull? Uneven. Third pull? Still not perfect.
But the master smiled and said: "That's the point."
No factory in the world can replicate this. Your ink density, your pressure, your heartbeat in that moment. It's imperfect. It's yours. It's one of a kind.
I took it home. It's going on my wall. Not because it's perfect ,because nobody else has this exact one.
This is what we mean by real heritage. Not watching. Making.

title: 1455: Gutenberg Printing Press
description:
The Printing Press that Revolutionized the World: Gutenberg and His Movable Type
Though printing was in use long before goldsmith Johann Gutenberg's time—especially with the Chinese—Gutenberg made it available to the public through his movable type press. Although the press has no surviving blueprints, it is known that his machine was similar to the olive and wine presses—a wooden device based on a moving screw at the center, through which the operator could apply pressure to the object being manufactured. Gutenberg and his assistants would lay out the movable type, letter by letter, until it formed the page they desired to print. This type was housed in a wooden box, and was moistened with an oil-based ink. When the paper was laid on top of the type, and pressed with the wooden screw device, one page was printed. Although it still took a very long time to print each page, the process was faster than the hand-lettered books of the day, and still preserved the beautiful handwork of the scribes. Gutenberg's invention of the printing press also helped to spread the fires of the Protestant Reformation, as Bibles, books, and other written works were spread to the public and other printing firms began to be established. The Bible—the first item printed on his press—was the 42-line Latin Bible, a beautiful copy of the Scriptures and a monument to both printing and the Word of God.

title: How Moveable metal type printing allowed knowledge to be shared by the many - Exhibition footages
description:
JIKJI, THE GOLDEN SEED is an exhibition celebrating the world’s first book printed with metal moveable type – or Jikji – and the impact that the printed word has had on the world ever since. It was created in the Korean city of Cheongju in 1377, 78 years before Gutenberg’s famous bible was printed in Germany.

With exhibits ranging from 600-year old cultural artefacts to a 360 degree virtual reality film, a controversial “portrait” created on glass from human cells and a ceramic work comprised of thousands of pieces of the word “Seed” written in the Hangul (Korean) alphabet, Jikji is interpreted not just as a type, but as innovation, and the show demonstrates how important such technological shifts are to human development.

Curator Stephanie Seungmin Kim has brought together 39 contemporary artists, architects and designers from Canada, China, Germany, Japan, Israel, Italy, Korea, South Africa, Turkey, the UK and the USA, with many producing new work for the exhibition, all involved in engaging critically with the idea of the invention. The historic cultural works come from the Gutenberg Museum in Mainz and the Old Printing Museum in Cheongju.

“Moveable metal type printing allowed knowledge to be shared by the many,” explains Stephanie Seungmin Kim. “In the West it played a crucial role in the Renaissance and the Enlightenment and has allowed the dissemination of scientific knowledge. The “Golden Seed” of the exhibition title refers to the potential for knowledge to spread, it is the seed from which the modern world grows.”

title: MIND-BLOWING Printing Press |Reviving MovableType by Stone Press
description:
Long before keyboards and screens… ideas were carved in wood.

This video brings to life one of the greatest inventions in human history:
Traditional Chinese movable type printing.

Dating back nearly a thousand years, this technique allowed thoughts, stories, and knowledge to transcend time—one block at a time.

Today, we revisit this ancient method to celebrate the beauty of language, labor, and legacy.

📚 Step-by-Step: The Movable Type Process, Reimagined
Carving the Characters
Each Chinese character is hand-carved into individual clay or wooden blocks. The carver’s blade must be precise—mistakes cannot be erased.

Sorting the Type
Like composing music, each character is sorted into trays, waiting to be arranged into sentences, poems, prayers, or books.

Typesetting by Hand
Typesetters align the characters, one by one, into printing frames—laying the foundation for each page with delicate fingers and perfect memory.

Inking the Surface
Ink is gently brushed onto the raised surfaces. The process is slow, deliberate—every drop must land exactly right.

Pressing the Page
Handmade paper is laid carefully over the type, then pressed or rubbed with a soft tool to imprint the characters.

Revealing the Print
The sheet is lifted—revealing black characters dancing across a blank page. Ancient thoughts reborn in real time.

🖋️ This is more than printing.
It’s a conversation across centuries, a craft where language is sculpted, not typed, and every word carries the weight of tradition.

🎥 This project is part of the #100YoungIntangibleHeritageProject (#百young非遗计划)—preserving disappearing arts that once changed the world.

📌 From Confucian texts to poetry, movable type brought knowledge to the masses. Today, it brings us closer to our roots.

title: printing press inventions | printing press characteristics
description:
The printing press is a mechanical device that applies pressure to an inked surface (usually type or a plate) resting upon a print medium (such as paper or cloth), transferring ink from the surface to the medium and producing multiple copies of text or images. Its invention revolutionized the distribution of information, leading to widespread literacy and the dissemination of ideas.

Invention of the Printing Press

The invention of the printing press is attributed to Johannes Gutenberg around 1440 in Mainz, Germany. However, some earlier forms of printing, like woodblock printing, were already in use in China and Korea.

1. Early Printing in East Asia:

Woodblock Printing: This method, which originated in China around the 7th century, involved carving entire pages of text into wooden blocks, inking them, and pressing paper onto them.

Movable Type: Around 1040, Bi Sheng in China developed a form of movable type using ceramic materials, though it didn’t achieve widespread use.



2. Gutenberg's Printing Press (1440s):

Gutenberg’s press was based on the concept of movable type, but it was more efficient. He developed metal movable type, which could be rearranged and reused, and a more sophisticated pressing mechanism modeled after wine or olive presses.

The key innovation was metal alloy type (a mixture of lead, tin, and antimony), which could be cast in large numbers and used repeatedly, leading to faster production.




The first major book printed using Gutenberg's press was the Gutenberg Bible in 1455, which is widely considered a masterpiece of typography and a landmark in printing history.

Characteristics of the Printing Press

1. Movable Type:

The most important feature of the printing press was its use of movable type. Individual letters (or characters) were cast in metal and could be arranged into words, sentences, and pages. After printing, they could be disassembled and reused.



2. Ink:

Gutenberg developed a durable oil-based ink that adhered well to the metal type, significantly improving print clarity and longevity.



3. Press Mechanism:

The mechanical press was based on existing screw press designs used in agriculture (for pressing grapes or olives) but adapted to ensure even pressure over a page, allowing for high-quality printing.



4. Efficiency:

The movable type system, combined with the mechanical press, allowed for rapid production of many identical copies of a document, drastically reducing the time and labor needed compared to earlier methods like hand-copying or woodblock printing.



5. Paper:

The use of paper rather than parchment or vellum was a crucial aspect of the press’s success. Paper was cheaper and more abundant in Europe by the 15th century, making mass production of books possible.



6. Scalability:

Unlike earlier forms of printing, where each page or image had to be carved or written by hand, the printing press allowed for multiple copies of the same text to be created quickly and cheaply. This had profound effects on literacy, education, and the dissemination of ideas.



7. Standardization:

The printing press helped in the standardization of texts. Manuscripts copied by hand were often riddled with errors, but with printed books, consistent versions of works could be distributed.




Impact of the Printing Press

1. Spread of Knowledge:

The printing press is often credited with accelerating the spread of knowledge, leading to the Renaissance, the Reformation, and the Scientific Revolution. Ideas could be distributed more easily and to a wider audience than ever before.



2. Literacy:

With books becoming cheaper and more accessible, literacy rates began to rise. People could access religious texts, scientific treatises, literature, and educational materials.



3. Religious Reformation:

The printing press played a crucial role in the Protestant Reformation, allowing reformers like Martin Luther to distribute their ideas quickly and widely.



4. Political and Social Change:

It gave rise to the mass media, helped form public opinion, and provided a platform for political and social discourse.




The printing press was a cornerstone technology that laid the groundwork for modern communication and publishing. It significantly influenced the course of history by enabling the mass production of written material.

title: History of  First Printing
description:
Before the printing press, books were rare treasures, painstakingly copied by hand! 📜 But did you know that innovative printing methods existed long before Gutenberg? In the 9th century, China introduced woodblock printing, while the 11th-century invention of movable type by Bi Sheng paved the way for faster production. Korea refined this further in the 13th century with metal type! But it was Gutenberg in the 15th century who revolutionized printing in Europe, making books accessible and affordable, starting with the Bible. This sparked the Renaissance, the Reformation, and scientific advancements! Discover how these groundbreaking techniques transformed knowledge sharing and shaped our modern world! 

This video features AI-generated images to enhance the storytelling.

Like, share, and join us on this incredible journey!

title: 温州游记（3）瑞安活着的发明 木活字印刷 Wenzhou Trip : In Rui’an I Found a Living Invention—Wood Movable-Type Printing
description:
这是《温州游记》第三集。
在很多人眼里，创新是科技公司的事，但在温州，我们看到了另一种答案。

这一集，我们来到浙江瑞安，走进中国现存最完整的木活字印刷体系。
从毕昇发明活字印刷，到王祯将它变成一整套高效运转的系统，这项技术不仅改变了中国，也深刻影响了世界。

更令人震撼的是，这不是一项被写进教科书的“过去式”。
在温州，木活字印刷至今仍被用于修族谱、续家史，作为一门“活着的技术”持续运转。

亲手排字、上墨、拓印的那一刻，你会发现——
真正的创新，从来不是噱头，而是把一件事做到极致，并且持续百年。

This is Episode 3 of our Wenzhou travel series.
When people talk about innovation, they often think of modern tech companies—but Wenzhou tells a very different story.

In this episode, we visit Rui’an, home to one of the world’s most complete surviving systems of wood movable-type printing.
From Bi Sheng’s original invention to Wang Zhen’s breakthrough that turned it into an efficient production system, this technology reshaped China—and influenced the world.

What makes it truly special is that this is not a relic locked in a museum.
In Wenzhou, wood movable-type printing is still used today to preserve family genealogies, making it a living technology, not just history.

By setting the type, inking the blocks, and printing by hand, we finally understand:
real innovation isn’t about novelty—it’s about mastery, continuity, and ideas that last for centuries.

title: #cncmachine #cncrouter #chinacnc #cncengravingmachine #viralvideo
description:
Definition of Technology
In modern times, the term technology is sometimes used to describe relatively recently invented devices, such as computers, cellular phones, and cars. Historically, however, technology has been defined much more broadly and includes more primitive machines, devices, and tools, such as hunting spears, the wheel, and the printing press. Technology is the application of systematic knowledge toward the development of a device, machine, or method of making or doing things.
Examples of Early Technology Spreading
Once technologies are used successfully, there is a tendency to spread the new method, device, or tool. With some technologies, it is difficult to pinpoint the details of technology adaptation because there are often many iterations or versions of a technology. The spread is also dependent on the characteristics of the technology itself, like how expensive and accessible it is.

The technology of writing, for instance, started in ancient Mesopotamia when accountants used the method to track the flow of goods. As the practice was established, more and more people started using writing to communicate. Soon after this development, as an independent event, people in Egypt started writing in hieroglyphics. These writing methods spread throughout their geographical regions over time. In ancient times, however, not every citizen wrote. Writing was time-consuming and was generally reserved for the upper portion of society.


Egyptian hieroglyphics are an early writing technology.

hieroglyphics are early writing technology

The spread of more recent technologies can be tracked by data showing when 50 percent of the population owned or used the technology. In the United States in the 1940s, 50 percent of the population had a refrigerator and a telephone, and in the 1990s 50 percent of the population had a dishwasher and a computer. Some of these technologies spread more quickly than others. The time that it takes for a technology to spread can depend on the price of the item, the value it creates for the consumer, and how accessible the item is, among other factors.

The globalization of the earth has increased the speed of the spread of many modern technologies. Globalization generally links countries so that knowledge is more easily spread among various regions. Additionally, it is thought that globalization increases competition for technological developments among nations.

Examples of Revised Technology
Over time, many technologies are changed and revised. Additionally, new technologies are built on the foundation of older technologies. Humans use existing building blocks to assemble new technologies. One example is the printing press. Gutenberg, who created technology for printing with movable type in the 15th century, was not the first to use moveable pieces in the printing process. In 11th century China, separate porcelain pieces were used for printing. Additionally, in 13th century Korea, individual metal characters were part of the printing process. Gutenburg was able to use previously developed technologies, like the Roman alphabet, metal-smelting, the wooden press, ink, and paper to create a new technology. Following Gutenberg's printing press, paper printing became an important way of disseminating information. Eventually, technologies such as the mass printing of books, magazines, and newspapers followed.

title: From Clay to Classic Poetry: The Ancient Craft of Making Ceramic Movable Type #shorts
description:
Witness the rebirth of an ancient printing technology in this mesmerizing journey from raw earth to a finished book. This video documents the entire painstaking process of creating ceramic movable type by hand, a craft requiring immense patience and skill.

Follow the craftsman as he:

Prepares and kneads raw clay using traditional methods.

Meticulously forms over 2,400 individual characters in a wooden mold.

Fires the clay types in a handmade outdoor kiln.

Treats the ceramics with a unique recipe to ensure a flawless print.

Sorts thousands of characters and sets them in hot wax.

Applies ink and prints the ancient verses of the "Shījīng" (The Classic of Poetry).

Binds the finished pages into a beautiful, handcrafted book.

If you are fascinated by traditional craftsmanship, ancient history, and satisfying processes, you won't want to miss this incredible transformation.

title: don't check this sounds ☠️ #trending #geography
description:
Long before keyboards and screens… ideas were carved in wood.

This video brings to life one of the greatest inventions in human history:
Traditional Chinese movable type printing.

Dating back nearly a thousand years, this technique allowed thoughts, stories, and knowledge to transcend time—one block at a time.

Today, we revisit this ancient method to celebrate the beauty of language, labor, and legacy.

📚 Step-by-Step: The Movable Type Process, Reimagined
Carving the Characters
Each Chinese character is hand-carved into individual clay or wooden blocks. The carver’s blade must be precise—mistakes cannot be erased.

Sorting the Type
Like composing music, each character is sorted into trays, waiting to be arranged into sentences, poems, prayers, or books.

Typesetting by Hand
Typesetters align the characters, one by one, into printing frames—laying the foundation for each page with delicate fingers and perfect memory.

Inking the Surface
Ink is gently brushed onto the raised surfaces. The process is slow, deliberate—every drop must land exactly right.

Pressing the Page
Handmade paper is laid carefully over the type, then pressed or rubbed with a soft tool to imprint the characters.

Revealing the Print
The sheet is lifted—revealing black characters dancing across a blank page. Ancient thoughts reborn in real time.

🖋️ This is more than printing.
It’s a conversation across centuries, a craft where language is sculpted, not typed, and every word carries the weight of tradition.

🎥 This project is part of the #100YoungIntangibleHeritageProject (#百young非遗计划)—preserving disappearing arts that once changed the world.

📌 From Confucian texts to poetry, movable type brought knowledge to the masses. Today, it brings us closer to our roots.

title: DERGE WOODBLOCK PRINTING TECHNIQUE
description:
Do you know plateau 'movable type printing'?
The Tibetan block printing technology of Dege Printing House is known as the "living fossil of Chinese movable board printing", and the wisdom of thousands of years depends on a knife and a moment to "print" out!
In the seventh year of Yongzheng in the Qing Dynasty (1729), the Tibetan engraving printing technique of Dege Printing House was famous for the construction of the Dege Printing House by Qujia Dengba Zeren, the 12th chiefess of Dege. Among them, woodcut engraving technique is famous for its exquisite selection of materials, rigorous procedures, exquisite design and exquisite carving, and is known as the peak of Tibetan engraving technique. Each block is the "source code" of Tibetan culture, and Buddhist scriptures, medicine, history, and art are all carved into the wood.
The Dege Printing House is the "great encyclopedia" of Tibetan culture, with 320,000 blocks of engravings carrying the essence of Tibetan culture. Craftsmen use knife as pen and wood as paper to engrave faith and culture into time, and every knife is a adherence to tradition. Dege woodblock engraving technology not only promotes the development of woodblock printing technology in the snowy plateau, but also makes great contributions to the inheritance of Chinese excellent traditional culture.
It is not only the carrier of words, but also the treasure of art, delicate lines, exquisite patterns, every knife mark is full of vitality.

title: 活字譜師 ｜他修煉近50年，刻的木活字比機器刻的還好看！Genealogy-printing master｜Chinese Characters Movable Woodblock printing
description:
活字譜師 ｜活字印刷術｜了不起的匠人｜傳統文化 中國｜China culture｜China documentary 2021

他是木活字印刷技術的國家級傳承人，從19歲開始練習“破木造字”，至今已做了近50年的譜師。他不僅記錄下了一個個家族的血脈故事，更傳承了中國大地上已瀕危的古老技藝。
He is the national inheritor of wood printing technology and has been a genealogist for nearly 50 years since he started practicing "wood breaking" at the age of 19. He has not only recorded the story of a family lineage, but also passed down an ancient technique that has become endangered in China.

“家譜”是一個距離現代生活越來越遠的詞，但仍然有人在堅守著“譜師”的職業。撿字塊、排印版、修家譜，這是王超輝大半輩子的生活狀態，也是他們王家祖祖輩輩的生活。在電子排版和鉛字印刷的時代，他卻仍然執著於木刻刀和撿字訣，親手在書頁裡留下墨香。
"Genealogy" is a word that is getting farther and farther away from modern life, but there are still people who are sticking to the profession of "genealogist". Picking up word blocks, typesetting printing plates and repairing genealogies, this has been the life of Wang Chaohui for most of his life, and also the life of the ancestors of the Wang family for generations. In the era of electronic typesetting and lead printing, he still sticks to his wood carving knife and word picking skills, leaving the fragrance of ink in the pages with his own hands.

The pithy formula echoed in the small village in south China, as well as the square characters which can be seen everywhere by the rice fields, has attracted the attention of more and more visitors both at home and abroad. Wang Chaohui is a“genealogy-printing master” in Rui'an, Zhejiang province, whose genealogy-printing handicraft handed down from generation to generation in his family. He has enabled us to witness how the“movable type," one of the Four Great Inventions of Ancient China, has been preserved, making it the "living fossil" that China has left to the world culture.

title: Confucius' Philosophy, Movable Type, Chinese Writing | Beijing 2008 Olympics Opening Ceremony
description:
Confucius ( 551 – 479 BC) was a Chinese teacher, editor, politician, and philosopher of the Spring and Autumn period of Chinese history. The philosophy of Confucius emphasized personal and governmental morality, correctness of social relationships, justice and sincerity. His followers competed successfully with many other schools during the Hundred Schools of Thought era only to be suppressed in favor of the Legalists during the Qin Dynasty. Following the victory of Han over Chu after the collapse of Qin, Confucius's thoughts received official sanction and were further developed into a system known as Confucianism. Confucius is traditionally credited with having authored or edited many of the Chinese classic texts including all of the Five Classics, but modern scholars are cautious of attributing specific assertions to Confucius himself. Aphorisms concerning his teachings were compiled in the Analects, but only many years after his death.


Block Printing was a costly and time-consuming process, for each carved block could only be used for a specific page of a particular book, besides, a single mistake in carving could ruin the whole block. However movable type changed all of that. In the Song Dynasty (960-1279), a man named Bi Sheng carved individual characters on identical pieces of fine clay. Each piece of movable type had on it one Chinese character which was carved in relief on a small block of moistened clay. After the block had been hardened by fire, the type became hard and durable and could be used wherever required. The pieces of movable type could be glued to an iron plate and easily detached from the plate. Each piece of character could be assembled to print a page and then broken up and redistributed as needed. When the printing was finished, the pieces were put away for future use. By the year1000, paged books in the modern style had replaced scrolls. Two color printing (black and red) was seen as early as 1340.
This technology then spread to Korea, Japan, Vietnam and Europe. Later, German Johann Gutenberg invented movable type made of metal in the 1440s. Movable Type Printing developed very fast. Based on clay type, type made of wood, lead, tin and copper gradually appeared.


Chinese characters are logograms used in the writing of Chinese and some other Asian languages. In Standard Chinese, and sometimes also in English, they are called Hanzi. They have been adapted to write a number of other languages including: Japanese, where they are known as kanji, Korean, where they are known as hanja, and Vietnamese in a system known as chữ Nôm. Collectively, they are known as CJK characters. In English, they are sometimes called Han characters. Chinese characters constitute the oldest continuously used system of writing in the world. By virtue of their widespread current use in East Asia, and historic use throughout the Sinosphere, Chinese characters are among the most widely adopted writing systems in the world.

The  Chinese character appeared three times in the performance is 和 which means "harmony".

description:
Definition of Technology
In modern times, the term technology is sometimes used to describe relatively recently invented devices, such as computers, cellular phones, and cars. Historically, however, technology has been defined much more broadly and includes more primitive machines, devices, and tools, such as hunting spears, the wheel, and the printing press. Technology is the application of systematic knowledge toward the development of a device, machine, or method of making or doing things.
Examples of Early Technology Spreading
Once technologies are used successfully, there is a tendency to spread the new method, device, or tool. With some technologies, it is difficult to pinpoint the details of technology adaptation because there are often many iterations or versions of a technology. The spread is also dependent on the characteristics of the technology itself, like how expensive and accessible it is.

The technology of writing, for instance, started in ancient Mesopotamia when accountants used the method to track the flow of goods. As the practice was established, more and more people started using writing to communicate. Soon after this development, as an independent event, people in Egypt started writing in hieroglyphics. These writing methods spread throughout their geographical regions over time. In ancient times, however, not every citizen wrote. Writing was time-consuming and was generally reserved for the upper portion of society.


Egyptian hieroglyphics are an early writing technology.

hieroglyphics are early writing technology

The spread of more recent technologies can be tracked by data showing when 50 percent of the population owned or used the technology. In the United States in the 1940s, 50 percent of the population had a refrigerator and a telephone, and in the 1990s 50 percent of the population had a dishwasher and a computer. Some of these technologies spread more quickly than others. The time that it takes for a technology to spread can depend on the price of the item, the value it creates for the consumer, and how accessible the item is, among other factors.

The globalization of the earth has increased the speed of the spread of many modern technologies. Globalization generally links countries so that knowledge is more easily spread among various regions. Additionally, it is thought that globalization increases competition for technological developments among nations.

Examples of Revised Technology
Over time, many technologies are changed and revised. Additionally, new technologies are built on the foundation of older technologies. Humans use existing building blocks to assemble new technologies. One example is the printing press. Gutenberg, who created technology for printing with movable type in the 15th century, was not the first to use moveable pieces in the printing process. In 11th century China, separate porcelain pieces were used for printing. Additionally, in 13th century Korea, individual metal characters were part of the printing process. Gutenburg was able to use previously developed technologies, like the Roman alphabet, metal-smelting, the wooden press, ink, and paper to create a new technology. Following Gutenberg's printing press, paper printing became an important way of disseminating information. Eventually, technologies such as the mass printing of books, magazines, and newspapers followed.

title: From Mud to Magic: See Ancient Printing in Action!
description:
Before information was instant, it was intimate—shaped by the patient hand of a master craftsman who gave physical form to our thoughts.

This video is a tribute to the forgotten artisans behind one of history's most vital inventions: Chinese movable type printing.

We explore the unseen labor and profound discipline required to turn raw wood into immortal words, celebrating a legacy built not on speed, but on soul.

📜 Step-by-Step: The Artisan's Touch, Revived

The Carver's Hand
A block of wood, a sharp blade, and absolute focus. Each character is a sculpture, carved in reverse with a meditative precision that leaves no room for error.

The Scholar's Eye
Thousands of characters, each a universe of meaning, are sorted by hand into a complex system. This requires the memory of a scholar and the organizational mind of a librarian.

The Setter's Rhythm
Like a musician finding notes, the typesetter plucks characters from the tray, arranging them into lines of text. It's a dance of muscle memory and deep concentration.

The Printer's Judgment
Applying the ink is an act of balance. Too much, and the words will blur; too little, and they will fade. The printer’s eye judges the perfect, even coat.

The Moment of Pressure
Paper meets wood in an act of trust. A firm, even pressure is applied, transferring the ink and the artisan’s effort from the physical block to the waiting page.

The Legacy Unveiled
The paper is peeled back. In that quiet moment, the artisan’s spirit is immortalized on the page, ready to speak across a thousand years.

🖋️ This isn’t just about a machine; it’s about human mastery.
It’s a testament to the idea that the most profound technologies are often powered by the quiet dedication of a single person.

🎥 This film is a proud entry in the #100YoungIntangibleHeritageProject (#百young非遗计划), celebrating the hands that built our literary heritage.

📌 Movable type didn’t just print books; it enshrined the skill of the craftsman in every page. It reminds us that knowledge has a human touch.

👉 Subscribe to honor the masters of the past and the timeless skills they perfected.

Witness the meticulous **printing process** of ancient blocks, starting with raw clay. The **carving** and shaping, followed by a lengthy hardening period, showcases **ancient technology** at its finest. See how these **ceramics** are used to create beautiful prints, reminiscent of the **renaissance** era.

title: Before Gutenberg: The Wild Origins of Printing!
description:
Long before Gutenberg’s famous printing press changed the world, humans had already discovered the power of printing.
From ancient Mesopotamian seals and mysterious clay tablets to Chinese woodblocks and medieval European engravings — the road to the printing revolution was long and full of forgotten inventions.

In this episode, we’ll uncover how early printing techniques evolved thousands of years before movable type — how people carved entire pages into wood, pressed ink onto paper by hand, and created the first mass-produced images and texts.

✨ Discover the incredible creativity of ancient printers, and how their work laid the foundations for one of humanity’s greatest innovations — the printed word.

If you love history, archives, old books, and stories of human ingenuity, this video will take you on a fascinating journey through time.

title: China invented paper, gunpowder, the compass, and printing.
description:
China (China) is widely recognized as one of the great ancient centers of innovation, having developed several transformative inventions that reshaped human history: paper, gunpowder, the compass, and printing.

Paper

Paper was invented during the Han Dynasty, traditionally credited to Cai Lun in around 105 CE. Before paper, people in China wrote on materials like bamboo strips, silk, or wooden tablets, which were heavy and expensive. Paper revolutionized communication by being lightweight, cheap, and easier to produce, eventually spreading across the world and enabling mass literacy, record-keeping, and education.

Gunpowder

Gunpowder, one of the most influential military inventions in history, was discovered during the Tang Dynasty (around the 9th century). It was originally developed by alchemists searching for immortality elixirs. The mixture of saltpeter, sulfur, and charcoal led to explosive reactions. Over time, it transformed warfare globally, leading to fireworks, cannons, and firearms.

Compass

The earliest form of the compass appeared during the Han Dynasty and was initially used for divination before being adapted for navigation during the Song Dynasty. The magnetic compass allowed sailors to determine direction even when the sun or stars were not visible, dramatically improving sea travel and trade across long distances.

Printing

Printing technology began with woodblock printing during the Tang Dynasty and later evolved into movable type during the Song Dynasty, invented by Bi Sheng. This innovation made it possible to reproduce texts quickly and in large quantities, spreading knowledge, religion, literature, and science more efficiently than ever before.

Together, these four inventions are often referred to as the Four Great Inventions of ancient China, and they had a profound impact not only on Chinese civilization but on the entire world, shaping global communication, warfare, navigation, and knowledge sharing.

title: #hax #cncmachine
description:
Definition of Technology
In modern times, the term technology is sometimes used to describe relatively recently invented devices, such as computers, cellular phones, and cars. Historically, however, technology has been defined much more broadly and includes more primitive machines, devices, and tools, such as hunting spears, the wheel, and the printing press. Technology is the application of systematic knowledge toward the development of a device, machine, or method of making or doing things.
Examples of Early Technology Spreading
Once technologies are used successfully, there is a tendency to spread the new method, device, or tool. With some technologies, it is difficult to pinpoint the details of technology adaptation because there are often many iterations or versions of a technology. The spread is also dependent on the characteristics of the technology itself, like how expensive and accessible it is.

The technology of writing, for instance, started in ancient Mesopotamia when accountants used the method to track the flow of goods. As the practice was established, more and more people started using writing to communicate. Soon after this development, as an independent event, people in Egypt started writing in hieroglyphics. These writing methods spread throughout their geographical regions over time. In ancient times, however, not every citizen wrote. Writing was time-consuming and was generally reserved for the upper portion of society.


Egyptian hieroglyphics are an early writing technology.

hieroglyphics are early writing technology

The spread of more recent technologies can be tracked by data showing when 50 percent of the population owned or used the technology. In the United States in the 1940s, 50 percent of the population had a refrigerator and a telephone, and in the 1990s 50 percent of the population had a dishwasher and a computer. Some of these technologies spread more quickly than others. The time that it takes for a technology to spread can depend on the price of the item, the value it creates for the consumer, and how accessible the item is, among other factors.

The globalization of the earth has increased the speed of the spread of many modern technologies. Globalization generally links countries so that knowledge is more easily spread among various regions. Additionally, it is thought that globalization increases competition for technological developments among nations.

Examples of Revised Technology
Over time, many technologies are changed and revised. Additionally, new technologies are built on the foundation of older technologies. Humans use existing building blocks to assemble new technologies. One example is the printing press. Gutenberg, who created technology for printing with movable type in the 15th century, was not the first to use moveable pieces in the printing process. In 11th century China, separate porcelain pieces were used for printing. Additionally, in 13th century Korea, individual metal characters were part of the printing process. Gutenburg was able to use previously developed technologies, like the Roman alphabet, metal-smelting, the wooden press, ink, and paper to create a new technology. Following Gutenberg's printing press, paper printing became an important way of disseminating information. Eventually, technologies such as the mass printing of books, magazines, and newspapers followed.

Skipping ahead a few hundred years, the artist Xu Bing created Book from the Sky as a monumental print. title: Taiwan's last Chinese character letterpress. Preserving the dying art of Chinese mold making
description:
Chang, Chieh-Kuan, 'Traditional Chinese characters named Rixing Type Foundry in Taipei, Taiwan.  We produce and provide words for letterpress printing. Letterpress printing means that we can print in the form of a letterpress on the old style print layout, where each word is composed of a unit during the typeset. It's already been 52 years since I started working in this field in 1967. Rixing Type Foundry was established in 1969, and it moved around 4 or 5 times and finally ended up here in 1979. There were almost 30 employees, but there were eventually only 2 or 3 left. We do however have some new, young people who are beginning to learn this skill. Therefore, we currently have 6 or 7 people on staff. Its existence has a very special cultural value, so we hope to preserve it. Traditional Chinese characters are an ancient language of this world, comprised of ideograms. An ideogram means each word has a very complete meaning, and therefore its beauty is incomparable. We hope that more and more people will begin to understand the beauty and cultural value of traditional Chinese characters,  and embrace the beauty of Chinese culture based on our love for them."

title: Ancient tech, The 1000 year old keyboard
description:
Forget plastic and pixels—the original "keyboard" was made of clay! 🧱✨



Before the digital age, the Song Dynasty in China revolutionized the world with Movable Type Printing. This ancient invention allowed knowledge to spread faster than ever before by turning mud into individual characters that could be rearranged to print any book imaginable.
In this video, we go from kneading the raw sediment-rich clay to the final ink press. It’s a 1,000-year-old process that eventually changed how the entire world communicates!
Key Highlights:

🏺 Kneading clay to remove air bubbles (durability is key!)

🔪 Carving individual characters for "movable" printing

🔥 Firing the "type" in a kiln to make them stone-hard

🖨️ The final ink-to-paper process

title: He Turned Mud Into a Printing Press — And It Actually Works
description:
Before Gutenberg ever touched a press, a Chinese craftsman named Bi Sheng had already cracked the code — using nothing but mud, fire, and pine resin. This video shows the complete, uncut process of making and using clay movable type, an invention over 1,000 years old that quietly changed the course of human history.
It starts with raw clay — dry, chalky chunks broken straight from the earth. He beats them into a thick brown slurry using a bamboo pole, then strains the entire mixture through hanging cloth suspended on a bamboo tripod, filtering out every grain of grit until only fine liquid clay remains. That liquid sits covered and undisturbed for a full week. Gravity does the work — clay sinks, water rises. The clear water gets skimmed off. The dense mud at the bottom gets scooped into cloth bags, hung to drip dry, then sealed inside ceramic jars to age. This aging process — called 陈化 — is what transforms raw clay into something workable, plastic, and alive.
Once ready, the clay gets wedged hard on a wooden table to knock out every air bubble. Rolled into uniform spheres. Each sphere gets pressed firmly into a hand-carved wooden master block, picking up a single reversed Chinese character — raised, sharp, and ready. Those raw type pieces go into a kiln and fire until they become hard ceramic. Each block then gets sealed with a liquid made from white root (白芨) and millet to fill the pores, then sanded smooth by hand until the character face is clean and precise.
Hundreds of finished blocks get sorted by stroke count into wooden grid trays for easy retrieval. When it's time to print, the artisan selects characters one by one, packs them into an iron frame, brushes the entire surface with ink, lays a sheet of white paper across the top, presses it flat with steady hands — and peels it back to reveal sharp, readable columns of printed Chinese text. Perfect. Clean. Repeatable.
And when the job is done, the frame gets gently heated. The pine resin adhesive softens. Every block loosens and drops free, gets scooped back into the tray, and waits for the next page. No waste. No loss. The same blocks, used again and again — exactly as they were over a thousand years ago.
This is not a reconstruction. This is not CGI. This is a living craft, demonstrated by hand, showing every step of a process that made mass communication possible centuries before the Western world caught up.

Once technologies are used successfully, there is a tendency to spread the new method, device, or tool. With some technologies, it is difficult to pinpoint the details of technology adaptation because there are often many iterations or versions of a technology. The spread is also dependent on the characteristics of the technology itself, like how expensive and accessible it is.

The technology of writing, for instance, started in ancient Mesopotamia when accountants used the method to track the flow of goods. As the practice was established, more and more people started using writing to communicate. Soon after this development, as an independent event, people in Egypt started writing in hieroglyphics. These writing methods spread throughout their geographical regions over time. In ancient times, however, not every citizen wrote. Writing was time-consuming and was generally reserved for the upper portion of society.


Egyptian hieroglyphics are an early writing technology.

hieroglyphics are early writing technology

The spread of more recent technologies can be tracked by data showing when 50 percent of the population owned or used the technology. In the United States in the 1940s, 50 percent of the population had a refrigerator and a telephone, and in the 1990s 50 percent of the population had a dishwasher and a computer. Some of these technologies spread more quickly than others. The time that it takes for a technology to spread can depend on the price of the item, the value it creates for the consumer, and how accessible the item is, among other factors.

The globalization of the earth has increased the speed of the spread of many modern technologies. Globalization generally links countries so that knowledge is more easily spread among various regions. Additionally, it is thought that globalization increases competition for technological developments among nations.

Examples of Revised Technology
Over time, many technologies are changed and revised. Additionally, new technologies are built on the foundation of older technologies. Humans use existing building blocks to assemble new technologies. One example is the printing press. Gutenberg, who created technology for printing with movable type in the 15th century, was not the first to use moveable pieces in the printing process. In 11th century China, separate porcelain pieces were used for printing. Additionally, in 13th century Korea, individual metal characters were part of the printing process. Gutenburg was able to use previously developed technologies, like the Roman alphabet, metal-smelting, the wooden press, ink, and paper to create a new technology. Following Gutenberg's printing press, paper printing became an important way of disseminating information. Eventually, technologies such as the mass printing of books, magazines, and newspapers followed.

title: Books #subscribe #short #shortsfeed #shortvideo
description:
A book can be broadly defined as a written document of at least 49 text pages that communicates thoughts, ideas, or information. Throughout the ages, books have changed dramatically, assuming a number of different forms. To a great extent, the evolution of the book has followed the expansion of communication forms and methods and the ever-increasing demand for information.

The first known forms of written documentation were the clay tablet of Mesopotamia and the papyrus roll of Egypt. Examples of both date back as early as 3000 b.c. Independent of these developments were Chinese books, made of wood or bamboo strips bound together with cords. These books dated back to 1300 b.c.

Modern book production came about as a result of the invention of printing press. Although the invention of printing most likely occurred earlier in China as well, the introduction of movable type and the printing press to Europe is credited to Johann Gutenberg of Germany. Gutenberg, in collaboration with his partners Johann Fust and Peter Schoffer, printed a Latin Bible using a hand printing press with movable lead type by about 1456. Each individual letter of early hand-set type was designed in a style closely resembling script or handlettering. Thus, the first books printed in Europe appeared much like books produced by scribes. Books printed in the fifteenth century are now called incunabula, a word derived from the Latin word for cradle. In 1640, Stephen Day printed the first book in North America, in Cambridge, Massachusetts.

Because the printing press and moveable type mechanized the book production process, books became available in greater numbers. By the nineteenth century, however, the demand for books could not be met quickly enough by the process of hand printing. Printers developed larger presses to accommodate larger sheets of paper and/or the newly invented continuous rolls of paper. These improvements allowed printers to produce books at a much faster rate. During the decades of the mid-1800s, further progress was made, including the invention of the papermaking machine (1820s), binding machinery (1860), and the cylinder press (1840s); later, the linotype (invented in 1884), cast type by line rather than by individual letter.

Book production in America and throughout the industrialized world has flourished and expanded during the twentieth century. Important advances in printing, such as the introduction of the offset printing press and computerized typesetting, have made mass production more economical. The development of the paperback book, which was introduced in the 1940s to provide a less expensive alternative to the traditional hardback book, has also made books more accessible to the public. While the invention of other forms of media, such as radio and television, has had an adverse impact on reading in general, books remain the primary source of knowledge throughout most of the world.

title: The Fascinating Evolution of Printing Technology
description:
Printing is a process for reproducing text and images, typically with ink on paper using a printing press. It is a crucial part of publishing and transaction printing, serving as a large-scale means of communication and dissemination of information. The history of printing dates back to ancient times, with significant developments occurring across various cultures. Modern printing technologies have evolved significantly, enabling high-speed and high-quality production. Here’s an overview of printing, including its history, types, and significance.

History of Printing
Woodblock Printing: Originating in China around the 8th century, this is one of the oldest printing techniques, where images and text were carved onto a wooden block, inked, and then pressed onto paper.
Movable Type: Also invented in China by Bi Sheng in the 11th century, movable type involved individual characters made from wood or metal that could be rearranged and reused. This innovation significantly improved printing efficiency and flexibility.
Gutenberg Press: In the 15th century, Johannes Gutenberg developed the first mechanical printing press in Europe, using movable metal type. This revolutionized the production of books, making them more accessible and affordable, and is often credited with contributing to the spread of the Renaissance, Reformation, and the Scientific Revolution.
Types of Printing Processes
Offset Lithography: This is the most common printing technique for high-volume commercial printing. The image to be printed gets transferred from a plate to a rubber blanket and then to the printing surface.
Flexography: Used for packaging, labels, and newspapers, flexography employs a flexible relief plate. It is well-suited for printing on non-flat surfaces like plastic, metallic films, and cellophane.
Digital Printing: Including inkjet and laser printing, digital printing directly transfers digital images to the printing surface. It offers high-quality prints with quicker turnaround times and is ideal for short-run printing.
Screen Printing: Used for textiles, ceramics, and posters, this method involves creating a stencil (or "screen") and using it to apply layers of ink on the printing surface, one color at a time.
Gravure Printing: Known for its high quality, gravure is used for large volume works like magazines, mail order catalogs, packaging, and wallpaper. It involves engraving the image onto a cylinder.
Significance of Printing
Knowledge Dissemination: Printing has been instrumental in the spread of knowledge, ideas, and education. It has democratized information, making it accessible to a broader audience.
Cultural Impact: The ability to reproduce texts and images has had a profound impact on culture, literacy, and education, fostering a more informed and educated society.
Economic Impact: Printing has created industries around publishing, journalism, and advertising, significantly contributing to the global economy.
Technological Advancements: The evolution of printing technology has facilitated advancements in various fields, including graphic design, communications, and marketing.
The Future of Printing
With the advent of digital media, the role of traditional printing is evolving. However, print media continues to hold value for its tangibility, permanence, and aesthetic qualities. The future of printing includes advancements in digital printing technologies, environmentally friendly practices, and integration with digital and augmented reality technologies, ensuring its relevance in a rapidly changing digital world.

In conclusion, printing remains a fundamental technology that has shaped and continues to influence the dissemination of information, cultural development, and economic growth worldwide. Its evolution reflects the ongoing human quest for knowledge and connection, highlighting its enduring significance in society.
