title: World’s First Printing Press | How Gutenberg Press Worked
description:
The Gutenberg Press, invented in the 15th century by Johannes Gutenberg, changed the world of communication forever. It was the first mechanical printing press that used movable metal letters to print books and documents quickly. Unlike handwritten manuscripts, this machine pressed inked letters onto paper with uniform accuracy, making knowledge available to common people. The Gutenberg Bible was its first major print, marking the beginning of mass printing. This revolutionary invention sparked the spread of education, science, and ideas across Europe. Discover how this press worked and why it remains a milestone in history.

title: #cncmachine #wirecut
description:
Definition of Technology
In modern times, the term technology is sometimes used to describe relatively recently invented devices, such as computers, cellular phones, and cars. Historically, however, technology has been defined much more broadly and includes more primitive machines, devices, and tools, such as hunting spears, the wheel, and the printing press. Technology is the application of systematic knowledge toward the development of a device, machine, or method of making or doing things.
Examples of Early Technology Spreading
Once technologies are used successfully, there is a tendency to spread the new method, device, or tool. With some technologies, it is difficult to pinpoint the details of technology adaptation because there are often many iterations or versions of a technology. The spread is also dependent on the characteristics of the technology itself, like how expensive and accessible it is.

The technology of writing, for instance, started in ancient Mesopotamia when accountants used the method to track the flow of goods. As the practice was established, more and more people started using writing to communicate. Soon after this development, as an independent event, people in Egypt started writing in hieroglyphics. These writing methods spread throughout their geographical regions over time. In ancient times, however, not every citizen wrote. Writing was time-consuming and was generally reserved for the upper portion of society.


Egyptian hieroglyphics are an early writing technology.

hieroglyphics are early writing technology

The spread of more recent technologies can be tracked by data showing when 50 percent of the population owned or used the technology. In the United States in the 1940s, 50 percent of the population had a refrigerator and a telephone, and in the 1990s 50 percent of the population had a dishwasher and a computer. Some of these technologies spread more quickly than others. The time that it takes for a technology to spread can depend on the price of the item, the value it creates for the consumer, and how accessible the item is, among other factors.

The globalization of the earth has increased the speed of the spread of many modern technologies. Globalization generally links countries so that knowledge is more easily spread among various regions. Additionally, it is thought that globalization increases competition for technological developments among nations.

Examples of Revised Technology
Over time, many technologies are changed and revised. Additionally, new technologies are built on the foundation of older technologies. Humans use existing building blocks to assemble new technologies. One example is the printing press. Gutenberg, who created technology for printing with movable type in the 15th century, was not the first to use moveable pieces in the printing process. In 11th century China, separate porcelain pieces were used for printing. Additionally, in 13th century Korea, individual metal characters were part of the printing process. Gutenburg was able to use previously developed technologies, like the Roman alphabet, metal-smelting, the wooden press, ink, and paper to create a new technology. Following Gutenberg's printing press, paper printing became an important way of disseminating information. Eventually, technologies such as the mass printing of books, magazines, and newspapers followed.

description:
What if the world’s first information revolution did not begin in Silicon Valley
but in Song Dynasty China over a thousand years ago

MOVEABLE TYPE is a powerful English language hip hop anthem inspired by the invention of movable type printing created by Bi Sheng during China’s Northern Song Dynasty

Bi Sheng lived from 990 to 1051
He was born in Qishui County Qizhou Huainan Circuit which is now Yingshan County Hubei Province
Some historical accounts also associate him with Hangzhou Zhejiang

Bi Sheng was a craftsman woodblock engraver and inventor
He created the world’s first movable type printing system allowing knowledge to be replicated reorganized and shared at scale for the first time in human history

This invention did not just print books
It printed civilization forward

Musical Concept

This song is built on a rare and intentional sound architecture

Oriental industrial hip hop BPM 94

The rhythm moves like typesetting blocks locking into a frame
Dry precise non swing drums strike like a printing press in motion

Deep controlled 808 bass with short attack stamp like and never EDM
Taiko drums are used only as structural impact appearing at key moments and the final chorus
Pipa appears as fast percussive fragments like individual movable type characters

The sound palette is built from wood metal stone heat and pressure

Cyber textures appear only as subtle glitch scans and digital afterimages
There is no dominant synth lead because the system speaks not the machine

The female lead vocal is calm commanding and iconic
Spoken rap delivery with strong pauses no speed flex and no softness

At the core of the track is a pure instrumental confrontation
Taiko versus pipa with no vocals
Letting the mechanics of civilization speak for themselves

The final chorus expands into full stereo width releasing controlled overwhelming pressure
Ancient yet futuristic never cute always inevitable

Why Movable Type Matters to Me

I believe culture is not decoration
It is infrastructure

Movable type was not just a tool
It was the moment humanity learned that ideas could be modular transferable and free

That is why I created this song
Not as nostalgia
But as a reminder that every modern screen every feed and every message traces back to this system

About My Music Label SOONG 宋潮

My music label is called SOONG 宋潮

宋 is my surname
It is one of the most common Chinese family names
It originated from the ancient State of Song during the Zhou Dynasty

Although the surname Song appears across different historical periods including the Song Dynasty
It was never exclusive to royalty
It is a surname of the people broad cultural and enduring

潮 means trend current and movement

SOONG 宋潮 represents something I deeply believe in
When ancient culture becomes modern momentum

I want every song I release to carry depth history and future vision
Music that stays meaningful over time

If you are listening to MOVEABLE TYPE
You are not just hearing a song

You are standing at the moment
where civilization learned how to multiply itself

==================================================

如果世界上第一場資訊革命
不是從矽谷開始
而是誕生於一千多年前的中國宋朝

活字印刷 MOVEABLE TYPE 是一首英文演唱的嘻哈作品
靈感來自北宋發明家畢昇 990 至 1051 所創造的活字版印刷術

畢昇為淮南路蘄州蘄水縣人
今湖北省英山縣
亦有史料記載其與浙江杭州有關

他是一位刻版工匠與發明家
創造了世界上第一套可重組可複製可大規模傳播的印刷系統

活字印刷不只是印書
它改寫了人類文明的運作方式

=================================================

音樂風格說明

這首歌我採用高度概念化的聲音結構

比較特別的是採用 東方工業嘻哈 BPM 94

節奏如同活字排版時一塊塊字模嵌入框架
每一次擊打都像印刷機落下的瞬間

低頻 808 短促而剋制
太鼓僅在關鍵段落與最終副歌出現
琵琶被切成節奏碎片如同單一活字模組

女聲主唱冷靜而有權威感
語句短停頓強如同宣告一個系統的誕生

為什麼我覺得宋代活字印刷很酷

因為那是人類第一次意識到
思想可以被模組化複製重新排列與傳播

這不只是技術
而是自由的起點

宋是我的姓氏
源自周朝宋國
是中國常見的姓氏之一
屬於文化與人民而非皇族

讓文化成為趨勢

title: Evolution of the Printing Press
description:
Gutenberg printing press,oil-based ink,mass production,metal type,standardization,wooden screw press,iron printing press,Charles Stanhope,Columbian press,Albion press,cast iron frame,Friedrich Koenig,steam-powered press,double-cylinder press,mechanization,Richard March Hoe,rotary press,web-fed press,continuous paper rolls,offset printing,rubber blanket,lithography,sheet-fed press,automation,digital printing,inkjet printing,laser printingThe printing press has undergone significant evolution since its invention in the 15th century, with each major development contributing to faster, more efficient, and higher-quality printing. Below is an evaluation of the printing press from its inception, with a focus on the key changes made over the centuries:

1. The Invention of the Printing Press (1440s)

Inventor: Johannes Gutenberg, a German blacksmith and goldsmith, is credited with inventing the first mechanical printing press around 1440.

Technology: Gutenberg's press was based on a modified wine or olive press. It used movable type, a revolutionary invention that allowed individual letters (type) to be arranged and reused.

Ink: Oil-based ink was another innovation by Gutenberg, which adhered better to metal type and paper compared to the water-based inks used in earlier Chinese and Korean printing.

Paper and Printing Plate: The press used paper, vellum, or parchment, with metal movable type made of lead, tin, and antimony.


Impact:

Mass Production: Gutenberg’s press allowed for the mass production of books, making them more affordable and accessible.

Standardization: The printing press led to more uniform and standardized texts compared to hand-copied manuscripts, revolutionizing communication, education, and religion (e.g., the spread of the Bible during the Protestant Reformation).


2. The Wooden and Metal Screw Presses (1500s - 1700s)

Development: Gutenberg's press remained largely unchanged for about 300 years, with small improvements made to the mechanics of the press, such as stronger wooden frames and more efficient screw mechanisms.

Materials: By the 17th century, some presses started incorporating metal parts, making them sturdier and more durable.

Changes in Type: By the 18th century, better quality typefaces and typesetting techniques were developed, improving the clarity and aesthetic of printed materials.


Impact:

Increased Productivity: Minor improvements in the materials and design of the press, such as the use of iron for screws, improved the press's durability and allowed for more frequent use.

Global Spread: These presses spread throughout Europe and the world, furthering the dissemination of information, which played a critical role in scientific advancements, political discourse, and the Enlightenment.


3. The Iron Printing Press (1800s)

Stanhope Press (1800): Charles Stanhope, an Englishman, created the first all-metal press, made from cast iron. This increased the strength of the press and allowed for larger print areas.

Efficiency: Stanhope's press required less force to operate, increasing output. It could print about 250 sheets per hour, compared to around 200 from the wooden press.

Columbian Press (1813): Invented by George Clymer, the Columbian press featured a more sophisticated lever mechanism, further reducing manual effort.

Albion Press (1820): Another notable iron press, it was simpler and lighter than the Columbian press, making it popular in commercial printing.


Impact:

Stronger and Faster: The use of iron allowed for greater precision in printing, leading to sharper and more consistent prints. The increased speed of printing contributed to the rise of newspapers and other periodicals.

Durability: Iron presses were more durable, requiring less maintenance and offering longer lifespans than their wooden predecessors.


4. The Steam-Powered Press (1810s - 1830s)

Friedrich Koenig’s Steam Press (1812): One of the most significant advances came with the invention of the steam-powered printing press by Friedrich Koenig. His press could print over 1,000 sheets per hour, a dramatic increase in productivity.

Double-Cylinder Steam Press (1814): Koenig and his partner Andreas Bauer improved their design with a double-cylinder press used by The Times of London, which could print 1,100 sheets per hour on both sides of the paper.

Changes in Operation: The mechanization of the press replaced the need for human muscle power with steam engines, drastically improving speed and efficiency.


Mass Printing: The steam press made mass production of newspapers, books, and pamphlets economically viable. This led to the rise of mass media and the newspaper industry

As the practice was established, more and more people started using writing to communicate. Soon after this development, as an independent event, people in Egypt started writing in hieroglyphics. These writing methods spread throughout their geographical regions over time. In ancient times, however, not every citizen wrote. Writing was time-consuming and was generally reserved for the upper portion of society.


Egyptian hieroglyphics are an early writing technology.

hieroglyphics are early writing technology

The spread of more recent technologies can be tracked by data showing when 50 percent of the population owned or used the technology. In the United States in the 1940s, 50 percent of the population had a refrigerator and a telephone, and in the 1990s 50 percent of the population had a dishwasher and a computer. Some of these technologies spread more quickly than others. The time that it takes for a technology to spread can depend on the price of the item, the value it creates for the consumer, and how accessible the item is, among other factors.

The globalization of the earth has increased the speed of the spread of many modern technologies. Globalization generally links countries so that knowledge is more easily spread among various regions. Additionally, it is thought that globalization increases competition for technological developments among nations.

Examples of Revised Technology
Over time, many technologies are changed and revised. Additionally, new technologies are built on the foundation of older technologies. Humans use existing building blocks to assemble new technologies. One example is the printing press. Gutenberg, who created technology for printing with movable type in the 15th century, was not the first to use moveable pieces in the printing process. In 11th century China, separate porcelain pieces were used for printing. Additionally, in 13th century Korea, individual metal characters were part of the printing process. Gutenburg was able to use previously developed technologies, like the Roman alphabet, metal-smelting, the wooden press, ink, and paper to create a new technology. Following Gutenberg's printing press, paper printing became an important way of disseminating information. Eventually, technologies such as the mass printing of books, magazines, and newspapers followed.

title: History of Printing Press | Media ki baat ShreyaVani ke sath | ShreyaVani | YouTube Radio
description:
#shreyavani #history #printing #printingpress #youtuberadio #media #historyofmedia #Media_ki_baat #ugcnet #journalism #journalismandmasscommunication

Books, newspapers, magazines, or any form of printed material are not surprising in our lives today. They have become such an integral part of our lives that it seems like they have always existed. However, years ago, printing was not as easy as it is today, where we can print any document from a printer in just a few seconds.

Namaskar, Ye hai Media ki baat ShreyaVani ke saath.

The 15th century was a time when Latin was the dominant language of communication, primarily used by religious institutions. However, since the invention of printing by Johannes Gutenberg, the reach of language and communication expanded, leading to the beginning of the Information Age. 
Canadian philosopher Marshall McLuhan once said that the invention of printing by Gutenberg was nothing short of a miracle. The Renaissance period in Europe, between the 13th and 16th centuries, also saw the significant role of printing presses.

Paper was crucial for printing, and its invention dates back to around 100 BC in China. In 105 AD, Ts'ai Lun started the paper-making business in China. The woodblock printing technique began around 600 AD, where letters, images, or any design were carved onto a wooden block. Then, in 1041 AD, movable clay type printing was introduced by Bi Sheng in China. It involved creating characters on blocks, but these blocks were made of clay, which was mixed with glue and fired in a kiln for strength.
Similarly, Wang Zhen initiated movable wooden type printing in 1298. However, the most significant breakthrough came with Johannes Gutenberg's invention of hot movable typefaces in 1434, which revolutionized the printing industry. Then, in 1440 AD, the first printing press for metal type was established in Germany.

Finally, the iconic printing movement from 1451 to 1455 began when the Bible was printed. After that, various printing techniques evolved over time, such as lithography in 1796, rotary press in 1846, offset printing, screen printing in 1910, inkjet 
printing in 1951, and digital printing from 1991. Digital printing marked a significant advancement, allowing printing of any digital design or image on various materials, not just paper.

Printing has come a long way from a small wooden block to digital printing, covering nearly 1500 years of history. If we divide printing into categories, there are four main types: hot type, cold type, offset printing, and screen printing.

The first printing in India took place in 1556 in Goa, initiated by the Portuguese. The first printing press was established at the Jesuit St. Paul's College, Old Goa.
The advent of printing presses led to the proliferation of newspapers worldwide. We will discuss how newspapers contributed to the field of information and their role in India's independence in the next part of the media discussion.

Thank you for listening to this program. We will meet again with new information.

title: Johannes Gutenberg The Man Who Invented Printing and Changed the World Ep. (20)
description:
In this compelling and insightful episode of "The Most Influential People in History" series, we delve into the life and revolutionary achievements of Johannes Gutenberg, the German blacksmith and inventor whose innovation didn't just create a machine—it ignited an information revolution that forever altered the course of human civilization. Before his breakthrough in the mid-15th century, books were meticulously copied by hand, a slow and expensive process that restricted knowledge primarily to the clergy and the wealthy elite.
This program explores how Gutenberg developed a complete system for printing with movable type. His genius lay in combining several elements into an efficient, economically viable process: an innovative method for casting durable, reusable metal typefaces, a specialized oil-based ink suitable for printing, and an adaptation of a wooden wine press for printing. While the Chinese had developed movable type centuries earlier, Gutenberg’s system was the first to achieve practical and mass-market success in Europe.
His masterpiece, the 42-line "Gutenberg Bible," was the first widely printed book in the West. The rapid spread of his printing press technology across the continent made books affordable and accessible to a broader audience, drastically increasing literacy rates and directly fueling the Renaissance, the Protestant Reformation, and the Age of Enlightenment. Despite dying in relative poverty and debt, Gutenberg’s legacy is priceless. He paved the way for the boundless flow of ideas and information, cementing his status as truly one of the most influential figures in human history.

title: THE INVENTION OF PAPER - CAI LUN ( CHINA 105 CE)
description:
The invention of paper is a significant milestone in the history of human communication and information preservation. Paper as we know it today has a long and complex history that spans centuries and multiple cultures. Here is a brief overview of the invention and development of paper:

Ancient China (c. 2nd century BCE): The earliest precursor to modern paper was invented in ancient China during the Han Dynasty (206 BCE – 220 CE). Traditional accounts credit a eunuch named Cai Lun with the invention of paper around 105 CE. This early form of paper was made from mulberry bark, old rags, and other plant fibers. The process involved pulping the fibers, pressing them into sheets, and then allowing them to dry.

Spread to Other Cultures: Papermaking technology gradually spread from China to other parts of the world. It reached Central Asia, the Middle East, and eventually Europe through trade routes and the exchange of knowledge.

Islamic World (8th century CE): The Islamic world played a crucial role in advancing papermaking technology. Arab scholars improved upon Chinese methods and established paper mills in places like Baghdad. They introduced new materials for papermaking, such as cotton and linen fibers.

Arrival in Europe (11th century CE): Papermaking technology arrived in Europe through trade with the Islamic world. The first paper mill in Europe was established in Spain in the 11th century. Paper gradually replaced parchment and vellum (animal skins) as the primary medium for writing and recording information.

Gutenberg's Printing Press (15th century CE): The invention of the printing press by Johannes Gutenberg around 1440 greatly increased the demand for paper. His movable type printing press revolutionized the dissemination of information and contributed to the spread of literacy.

Papermaking Improvements: Over the centuries, papermaking techniques continued to evolve. Water-powered mills and mechanical processes were developed to increase the efficiency of paper production. Different fibers, including wood pulp, were used to make paper.

Industrial Revolution (18th century CE): The Industrial Revolution in the 18th century brought significant advancements to papermaking. Machines like the Fourdrinier machine made continuous paper production possible, and the use of wood pulp became more widespread.

Modern Paper Production: In the 19th and 20th centuries, paper production became highly industrialized, leading to the creation of a wide variety of paper types for various purposes, from newspapers to books to packaging materials.

Today, paper remains an essential medium for communication, documentation, art, and packaging, despite the digital age. The invention of paper in ancient China was a pivotal moment in the history of human civilization, as it enabled the widespread dissemination of knowledge and the recording of history.

title: They Stole the Secret of Paper from China — And Changed Civilization
description:
📬 Subscribe for weekly hidden history drops → https://hidden-history.com

Before paper, a single book cost as much as a house. 

A government report weighed as much as a small child. 

And an entire civilization's knowledge could vanish in a single fire.

This is the hidden history of paper — the invention you use every day and never think about. 

From an accidental discovery in ancient China to a forgotten battle in 751 AD that changed the intellectual destiny of the Western world, the story of paper is more dramatic than you probably ever realized.

In this episode: the eunuch who standardized paper making — and was destroyed by the very court documents he made possible. The Battle of Talas, where Chinese prisoners of war built the first paper mills outside China and ignited the Islamic Golden Age. And the German goldsmith named Gutenberg who combined paper with movable type — and accidentally sparked the Protestant Reformation and the Scientific Revolution.

It wasn't just an invention. It was civilization itself.

0:00 — A World Without Paper: The Thought Experiment
1:23 — Before Paper
2:56 — Cai Lun and the Standardization of Paper
5:10 — The Dark Turn: Cai Lun's Fall
6:36 — The Battle of Talas: How Paper Escaped China
7:41 — The Islamic Golden Age and the House of Wisdom
9:00 — Paper Reaches Europe & Gutenberg's Revolution
10:54 — Why Paper Still Runs the World

description:
Definition of Technology
In modern times, the term technology is sometimes used to describe relatively recently invented devices, such as computers, cellular phones, and cars. Historically, however, technology has been defined much more broadly and includes more primitive machines, devices, and tools, such as hunting spears, the wheel, and the printing press. Technology is the application of systematic knowledge toward the development of a device, machine, or method of making or doing things.
Examples of Early Technology Spreading
Once technologies are used successfully, there is a tendency to spread the new method, device, or tool. With some technologies, it is difficult to pinpoint the details of technology adaptation because there are often many iterations or versions of a technology. The spread is also dependent on the characteristics of the technology itself, like how expensive and accessible it is.

The technology of writing, for instance, started in ancient Mesopotamia when accountants used the method to track the flow of goods. As the practice was established, more and more people started using writing to communicate. Soon after this development, as an independent event, people in Egypt started writing in hieroglyphics. These writing methods spread throughout their geographical regions over time. In ancient times, however, not every citizen wrote. Writing was time-consuming and was generally reserved for the upper portion of society.


Egyptian hieroglyphics are an early writing technology.

hieroglyphics are early writing technology

The spread of more recent technologies can be tracked by data showing when 50 percent of the population owned or used the technology. In the United States in the 1940s, 50 percent of the population had a refrigerator and a telephone, and in the 1990s 50 percent of the population had a dishwasher and a computer. Some of these technologies spread more quickly than others. The time that it takes for a technology to spread can depend on the price of the item, the value it creates for the consumer, and how accessible the item is, among other factors.

The globalization of the earth has increased the speed of the spread of many modern technologies. Globalization generally links countries so that knowledge is more easily spread among various regions. Additionally, it is thought that globalization increases competition for technological developments among nations.

Examples of Revised Technology
Over time, many technologies are changed and revised. Additionally, new technologies are built on the foundation of older technologies. Humans use existing building blocks to assemble new technologies. One example is the printing press. Gutenberg, who created technology for printing with movable type in the 15th century, was not the first to use moveable pieces in the printing process. In 11th century China, separate porcelain pieces were used for printing. Additionally, in 13th century Korea, individual metal characters were part of the printing process. Gutenburg was able to use previously developed technologies, like the Roman alphabet, metal-smelting, the wooden press, ink, and paper to create a new technology. Following Gutenberg's printing press, paper printing became an important way of disseminating information. Eventually, technologies such as the mass printing of books, magazines, and newspapers followed.

title: General Knowledge Quiz Episode #277 #trivia #quiz
description:
Test your knowledge with 3 fun and challenging questions!  These questions will put your brain to the test and may teach you something new. 

Check my channel for similar quizzes and for longer quizzes.

#quiz #generalknowledge #trivia

+++++++++++
Transcript:

In which country was the game of chess invented?
1) Persia
2) China
3) India
The correct answer is 3) India
Chess originated in India around the 6th century AD as a game called Chaturanga.
It spread to Persia, where it became Shatranj, and then to Europe via the Arab world.
Modern chess rules were largely standardized in Europe during the 15th century.

Which country invented the printing press?
1) China
2) Germany
3) South Korea
The correct answer is 2) Germany
In 1440s Germany, Johannes Gutenberg changed the world by inventing the movable-type printing press. His first major project, the Gutenberg Bible, pioneered a new era of mass production. This single invention accelerated the spread of knowledge and fueled the flames of the Renaissance

Which country invented Paper?
1) China
2) India
3) Egypt
The correct answer is 1) China
Before the invention of paper, writing was a heavy task involving bamboo, silk, and clay tablets. That all changed in 105 A.D. when Cai Lun, a Chinese court official, revolutionized history by creating the very first sheets of paper

First movable type: Invented by Bi Sheng in China (~1040–1048 AD) using ceramic (later wood/metal), but limited adoption due to thousands of Chinese characters.
Earlier woodblock printing: From China's 7th–9th centuries.
Practical mechanized press with metal type: Johannes Gutenberg (~1440, Germany), enabling mass production; famous for the Gutenberg Bible (1455).

title: How Ancient China Printed Entire Books Before Machines Ever Existed
description:
This video reveals the ingenious traditional Chinese craft of making ceramic movable type.
Raw clay is carefully refined into a pure paste, shaped into individual characters, and fired at over 900°C. After strengthening with natural Bletilla striata and millet, the ceramic pieces are arranged in a frame using pine resin adhesive.
Ink is applied, paper is laid down, and pressure transfers the text with perfect clarity. The characters are then easily released for reuse.
The printed pages are folded, trimmed, and bound using the ancient four-eye stitch, creating complete books ready to carry knowledge across generations.
⚠️ Disclaimer:
This content is created for educational and informational purposes only. It explains traditional Chinese ceramic movable type and printing techniques.

title: Print culture and Modern world| Grade 10 | SNS Institutions
description:
How did a simple wooden press change the course of human history? In this video, we explore the fascinating journey of Print Culture, from its origins in East Asia to the Gutenberg revolution in Europe and its powerful impact on the Indian nationalist movement.
This session is designed for students preparing for the 2026 Board Exams and history buffs alike. We break down complex historical shifts into easy-to-understand segments, focusing on how the "written word" sparked revolutions, spread literacy, and challenged authority across the globe.
What You Will Learn:
The Origins of Print: Evolution from hand-copying to woodblock printing in China and Japan.
The Gutenberg Revolution: How the mechanical printing press shifted Europe from a hearing public to a reading public.
Religion & Reform: The role of print in the Protestant Reformation and the spread of new ideas.
The Reading Mania: How libraries and cheap books transformed the lives of workers, women, and children.
Print in India: The growth of the vernacular press, social reform movements, and the struggle against British censorship.

title: Printed Words
description:
Printed Words : Allowing WORDS to spread across the world further than ever before.

Pressed to impress:
        Europe got the printed word in about 1450. Germany inventor johannes gutenberg developed letters made of METAL
that could be arranged and rearranged inside a wooden grid. These were covered in ink and pressed hard onto paper,printing a whole page each time.     

Chinese blocks :
        Avid readers in CHINA were enjoying the printed word and image long before anyone else. Chinese printers used
etched wood and clay blocks from about 600 CE to print images and text onto paper or cloth.

Printing Power :
        By 1500, printing presses were in operation in most major cities of europe. The availability of printed books made it easier for everyone to learn to read, and for ideas to spread FAR and WIDE, leading to many scientific discoveries
in the years ahead.

title: Making treading tools #cncmachine #chinacnc
description:
Definition of Technology
In modern times, the term technology is sometimes used to describe relatively recently invented devices, such as computers, cellular phones, and cars. Historically, however, technology has been defined much more broadly and includes more primitive machines, devices, and tools, such as hunting spears, the wheel, and the printing press. Technology is the application of systematic knowledge toward the development of a device, machine, or method of making or doing things.
Examples of Early Technology Spreading
Once technologies are used successfully, there is a tendency to spread the new method, device, or tool. With some technologies, it is difficult to pinpoint the details of technology adaptation because there are often many iterations or versions of a technology. The spread is also dependent on the characteristics of the technology itself, like how expensive and accessible it is.

The technology of writing, for instance, started in ancient Mesopotamia when accountants used the method to track the flow of goods. As the practice was established, more and more people started using writing to communicate. Soon after this development, as an independent event, people in Egypt started writing in hieroglyphics. These writing methods spread throughout their geographical regions over time. In ancient times, however, not every citizen wrote. Writing was time-consuming and was generally reserved for the upper portion of society.


Egyptian hieroglyphics are an early writing technology.

hieroglyphics are early writing technology

The spread of more recent technologies can be tracked by data showing when 50 percent of the population owned or used the technology. In the United States in the 1940s, 50 percent of the population had a refrigerator and a telephone, and in the 1990s 50 percent of the population had a dishwasher and a computer. Some of these technologies spread more quickly than others. The time that it takes for a technology to spread can depend on the price of the item, the value it creates for the consumer, and how accessible the item is, among other factors.

The globalization of the earth has increased the speed of the spread of many modern technologies. Globalization generally links countries so that knowledge is more easily spread among various regions. Additionally, it is thought that globalization increases competition for technological developments among nations.

Examples of Revised Technology
Over time, many technologies are changed and revised. Additionally, new technologies are built on the foundation of older technologies. Humans use existing building blocks to assemble new technologies. One example is the printing press. Gutenberg, who created technology for printing with movable type in the 15th century, was not the first to use moveable pieces in the printing process. In 11th century China, separate porcelain pieces were used for printing. Additionally, in 13th century Korea, individual metal characters were part of the printing process. Gutenburg was able to use previously developed technologies, like the Roman alphabet, metal-smelting, the wooden press, ink, and paper to create a new technology. Following Gutenberg's printing press, paper printing became an important way of disseminating information. Eventually, technologies such as the mass printing of books, magazines, and newspapers followed.

title: Xu Bing | A Book from the Sky
description:
Skipping ahead a few hundred years, the artist Xu Bing created Book from the Sky as a monumental print. It is probably among the most ambitious, labor-intensive, and useless books ever to be printed in China or anywhere else. He created 4,000 unique characters on wood blocks to print this massive "book" but while those characters look like Chinese writing, they are actually completely meaningless.

title: Laser GRBL Software: One of The Best For Image Laser Engraving
description:
From Gutenberg to 3D printing: 
A Pressing History 

By Rhodri Marsden

This printing history timeline takes you on a journey from Gutenberg’s revolutionary printing press to the 3D printers of today.

The blurb that accompanies any new printer tends to loudly boast the pages per minute (ppm) or the number of pages of text that it can pump out in one minute.

The kind of speeds we see from modern printers such as HP’s PageWide models would have caused Johannes Gutenberg to faint; in 1454, when his groundbreaking printing press was being used to print copies of the Bible, he was producing pages at the rate of six per day – a rather lowly PPM of 0.004.

It is a measure of progress that we can print pages in our homes about 13,000 times faster than Gutenberg could, but print has developed in many ways since his assistants were mixing soot, turpentine and walnut oil into printing ink.

In truth, Gutenberg was not the first to use a movable type system; experiments in China, India and Korea preceded his efforts by up to several hundred years.

But his system, acknowledged as one of the most significant inventions in world history, prompted a complete change in culture; ideas could be disseminated more easily, knowledge could be pooled, and it ultimately led to a scientific revolution.

In many countries it was regarded as a threat to the established order (printing in France without royal permission was made punishable by death in 1563) and this suspicion may have contributed to a slow pace of change in the world of printing itself.

For example, the problem of printing shades between black and white was not efficiently solved until mezzotint was invented – about 200 years after Gutenberg’s first experiments – by roughening the surface of the metal plate by using a “rocker” with tiny teeth. 

Printing would undergo more radical changes at the dawn of the 19th century.

Charles Stanhope’s press, invented in 1800, was the first to be made entirely of metal, and it was significantly faster and more durable than its wooden counterparts.

Boosting speed was also the aim of the rotary press (1843), which used impressions on a cylinder to print continuously on long rolls of paper. But automation was always going to be the key to increasing that ppm.

In 1834 a New Yorker, Darius Wells, invented a means of mass producing type, but the process of arranging that type still had to be done manually until the 1880s, when two different methods of automatic typesetting were invented: Monotype and Linotype.

Both were operated by a keyboard, but while Monotype machines typeset one letter at a time Linotype produced (as its name suggests) a whole line, or “slug”, in one go.

Linotype was favoured by the newspaper industry, the more easily corrected Monotype system by book printers. But printing developments were not all about speed; lithographic printing (invented by Alois Senefelder in 1796), chromolithography (1837) and offset lithography (1875) would use chemical processes to dramatically improve the clarity and sharpness of the print.

The age of the PC would change printing for ever, but pioneering work on the inkjet printers that many of us use today was being done as long ago as the early 1950s.

Similarly, the laser printer was born in a Xerox lab in California way back in 1969, but it was not until 1984 that both these technologies were harnessed for the mass market with HP’s Thinkjet and Laserjet printers.

Today, there is a huge focus on 3D printing and how the ability to print out objects will change our lives, but quiet improvements are still being made in traditional printing – such as PageWide technology, where the paper moves over a stationary print head.

All 1,282 pages of Gutenberg’s Bible can be delivered to a print tray in under 25 minutes. Back in the 1450s such a thing would have seemed like witchcraft. 😇😆🙏😂

title: Print comes to Europe | Part 2_Print Culture and the Modern World_CBSE_NCERT_Class 10
description:
#PrintComestoEurope
For centuries, silk and spices from China flowed into Europe through
the silk route. In the eleventh century, Chinese paper reached Europe
via the same route. Paper made possible the production of
manuscripts, carefully written by scribes. Then, in 1295, Marco Polo,
a great explorer, returned to Italy after many years of exploration in
China. As you read above, China already had the technology of
woodblock printing. Marco Polo brought this knowledge back with
him. Now Italians began producing books with woodblocks, and
soon the technology spread to other parts of Europe. Luxury
editions were still handwritten on very expensive vellum, meant for
aristocratic circles and rich monastic libraries which scoffed at printed
books as cheap vulgarities. Merchants and students in the university
towns bought the cheaper printed copies.
As the demand for books increased, booksellers all over Europe
began exporting books to many different countries. Book fairs were
held at different places. Production of handwritten manuscripts was
also organised in new ways to meet the expanded demand. Scribes
or skilled handwriters were no longer solely employed by wealthy
or influential patrons but increasingly by booksellers as well. More
than 50 scribes often worked for one bookseller.
But the production of handwritten manuscripts could not satisfy
the ever-increasing demand for books. Copying was an expensive,
laborious and time-consuming business. Manuscripts were fragile,
awkward to handle, and could not be carried around or read easily.
Their circulation therefore remained limited. With the growing
demand for books, woodblock printing gradually became more
and more popular. By the early fifteenth century, woodblocks were
being widely used in Europe to print textiles, playing cards, and
religious pictures with simple, brief texts.
There was clearly a great need for even quicker and cheaper
reproduction of texts. This could only be with the invention of a
new print technology. The breakthrough occurred at Strasbourg,
Germany, where Johann Gutenberg developed the first-known
printing press in the 1430s.
Imagine that you are Marco Polo. Write a letter
from China to describe the world of print which
you have seen there.
Activity
New words
Vellum – A parchment made from the skin
of animals
The Jikji of Korea is among the world’s oldest
existing books printed with movable metal type.
It contains the essential features of Zen
Buddhism. About 150 monks of India, China and
Korea are mentioned in the book. It was printed
in late 14th century. While the first volume of
the book is unavailable, the second one is available
in the National Library of France. This work marked
an important technical change in the print
culture. That is why it was inscribed on the
UNESCO Memory of the World Register in 2001.

Platen – In letterpress printing, platen is a board which is
pressed onto the back of the paper to get the impression from
the type. At one time it used to be a wooden board; later it
was made of steel
2.1 Gutenberg and the Printing Press
Gutenberg was the son of a merchant and grew up on a large
agricultural estate. From his childhood he had seen wine and olive
presses. Subsequently, he learnt the art of polishing stones, became a
master goldsmith, and also acquired the expertise to create lead
moulds used for making trinkets. Drawing on this knowledge,
Gutenberg adapted existing technology to design his innovation.
The olive press provided the model for the printing press, and moulds
were used for casting the metal types for the letters of the alphabet.
By 1448, Gutenberg perfected the system. The first book he printed
was the Bible. About 180 copies were printed and it took three
years to produce them. By the standards of the time this was fast
production.
The new technology did not entirely displace the existing art of
producing books by hand.
In fact, printed books at first closely resembled the written
manuscripts in appearance and layout. The metal letters imitated the
ornamental handwritten styles. Borders were illuminated by hand
with foliage and other patterns, and illustrations were painted. In the
books printed for the rich, space for decoration was kept blank on
the printed page. Each purchaser could choose the design and decide
on the painting school that would do the illustrations.
In the hundred years between 1450 and 1550, printing presses were
set up in most countries of Europe. Printers from Germany travelled
to other countries, seeking work and helping start new presses. As
the number of printing presses grew, book production boomed.
The second half of the fifteenth century saw 20 million copies of
printed books flooding the markets in Europe. The number went
up in the sixteenth century to about 200 million copies.

title: From Blade to Algorithm: Chinese Characters as AI's Money-Saving Craft
description:
What do ancient bone carving and modern AI have in common?
In this special episode from Baigongtu LAB (Hundred Crafts Lab), we trace 3,000 years of Chinese writing as the world's longest-running information processing craft.

From knife marks on oracle bones → molten bronze inscriptions → brush strokes on silk → wooden printing blocks → metal movable type → keyboard code → neural network parameters...

Chinese characters evolved not just as writing, but as technology - each era optimizing how humans compress and transmit meaning.

Here's the twist: Modern AI discovered Chinese characters are surprisingly cost-efficient. Same idea, fewer characters, lower computing cost. What took ancient craftsmen generations to perfect, AI calls "token efficiency."

description:
Definition of Technology
In modern times, the term technology is sometimes used to describe relatively recently invented devices, such as computers, cellular phones, and cars. Historically, however, technology has been defined much more broadly and includes more primitive machines, devices, and tools, such as hunting spears, the wheel, and the printing press. Technology is the application of systematic knowledge toward the development of a device, machine, or method of making or doing things.
Examples of Early Technology Spreading
Once technologies are used successfully, there is a tendency to spread the new method, device, or tool. With some technologies, it is difficult to pinpoint the details of technology adaptation because there are often many iterations or versions of a technology. The spread is also dependent on the characteristics of the technology itself, like how expensive and accessible it is.

The technology of writing, for instance, started in ancient Mesopotamia when accountants used the method to track the flow of goods. As the practice was established, more and more people started using writing to communicate. Soon after this development, as an independent event, people in Egypt started writing in hieroglyphics. These writing methods spread throughout their geographical regions over time. In ancient times, however, not every citizen wrote. Writing was time-consuming and was generally reserved for the upper portion of society.


Egyptian hieroglyphics are an early writing technology.

hieroglyphics are early writing technology

The spread of more recent technologies can be tracked by data showing when 50 percent of the population owned or used the technology. In the United States in the 1940s, 50 percent of the population had a refrigerator and a telephone, and in the 1990s 50 percent of the population had a dishwasher and a computer. Some of these technologies spread more quickly than others. The time that it takes for a technology to spread can depend on the price of the item, the value it creates for the consumer, and how accessible the item is, among other factors.

The globalization of the earth has increased the speed of the spread of many modern technologies. Globalization generally links countries so that knowledge is more easily spread among various regions. Additionally, it is thought that globalization increases competition for technological developments among nations.

Examples of Revised Technology
Over time, many technologies are changed and revised. Additionally, new technologies are built on the foundation of older technologies. Humans use existing building blocks to assemble new technologies. One example is the printing press. Gutenberg, who created technology for printing with movable type in the 15th century, was not the first to use moveable pieces in the printing process. In 11th century China, separate porcelain pieces were used for printing. Additionally, in 13th century Korea, individual metal characters were part of the printing process. Gutenburg was able to use previously developed technologies, like the Roman alphabet, metal-smelting, the wooden press, ink, and paper to create a new technology. Following Gutenberg's printing press, paper printing became an important way of disseminating information. Eventually, technologies such as the mass printing of books, magazines, and newspapers followed.

title: 1450'den 600 Yıl Geriye: Matbaanın Kaynağı #tarih #bilim
description:
Matbaayı Gerçekten Kim Buldu?

Çoğu kişi matbaayı Johannes Gutenberg’in bulduğunu düşünür. Okullarda da genellikle böyle öğretilir. Ancak bu bilgi aslında tam olarak doğru değildir.

Gerçekte matbaa fikri Gutenberg’den yüzyıllar önce Çin’de ortaya çıkmıştır. Çin’de yaklaşık 600–700 yıllarında tahta bloklar kullanılarak kitap basılmaya başlanmıştır. Bu yöntemde yazılar bir tahta kalıba oyulur, mürekkep sürülür ve kağıda basılırdı. Hatta dünyanın bilinen en eski basılı kitabı olan Diamond Sutra, Çin’de 868 yılında bu yöntemle basılmıştır.  

Daha sonra 11. yüzyılda Çinli mucit Bi Sheng, harflerin tek tek yerleştirilebildiği hareketli harf sistemi geliştirdi. Bu sistem bugünkü matbaanın temel fikrünü oluşturuyordu.  

Peki Gutenberg ne yaptı?

Alman mucit Johannes Gutenberg, yaklaşık 1450 yılında Avrupa’da metal hareketli harfli modern matbaayı geliştirdi. Onun yaptığı yenilik, harfleri metalden üretmek ve bunları hızlı şekilde çoğaltabilen bir baskı makinesi kullanmaktı. Bu sayede kitaplar çok daha hızlı ve ucuz basılabildi.  

Sonuç olarak:
 • Matbaanın ilk fikirleri Çin’de ortaya çıktı.
 • Gutenberg ise modern ve hızlı çalışan matbaayı geliştirdi.

Bu yüzden aslında doğru ifade şudur:
Matbaayı Gutenberg icat etmedi, ama modern matbaayı geliştiren kişi Gutenberg’dir.

title: This NEW 2026 Chinese Off-Road 4x4 Pickup RV Costs Only $54K! | Livezone Freedom Poer A
description:
In this video, we take a closer look at the new Livezone Freedom Poer A — a compact Chinese 4x4 pickup-based motorhome built on the GWM Poer / Great Wall Cannon platform.

At first glance, it looks like a small pickup camper, but the specs are surprisingly serious: a 2.4L turbo engine, 9-speed automatic transmission, intelligent all-wheel drive, two differential locks, a 48V electrical system, a large battery, solar panels, a diesel heater, fresh water tank, gray water tank, and a practical interior layout for 2–3 people.

Inside, this RV has an over-cab bed, compact bathroom, L-shaped kitchen, dining area, storage compartments, and a cozy interior designed for efficient travel. The biggest surprise is the price — around $54,000 in China.

Could this be one of the most interesting budget 4x4 motorhomes coming from China? Let’s take a detailed look.

Model: Livezone / Lanzhong Freedom Poer A

description:
Video Definition of Technology
In modern times, the term technology is sometimes used to describe relatively recently invented devices, such as computers, cellular phones, and cars. Historically, however, technology has been defined much more broadly and includes more primitive machines, devices, and tools, such as hunting spears, the wheel, and the printing press. Technology is the application of systematic knowledge toward the development of a device, machine, or method of making or doing things.
Examples of Early Technology Spreading
Once technologies are used successfully, there is a tendency to spread the new method, device, or tool. With some technologies, it is difficult to pinpoint the details of technology adaptation because there are often many iterations or versions of a technology. The spread is also dependent on the characteristics of the technology itself, like how expensive and accessible it is.

The technology of writing, for instance, started in ancient Mesopotamia when accountants used the method to track the flow of goods. As the practice was established, more and more people started using writing to communicate. Soon after this development, as an independent event, people in Egypt started writing in hieroglyphics. These writing methods spread throughout their geographical regions over time. In ancient times, however, not every citizen wrote. Writing was time-consuming and was generally reserved for the upper portion of society.


Egyptian hieroglyphics are an early writing technology.

hieroglyphics are early writing technology

The spread of more recent technologies can be tracked by data showing when 50 percent of the population owned or used the technology. In the United States in the 1940s, 50 percent of the population had a refrigerator and a telephone, and in the 1990s 50 percent of the population had a dishwasher and a computer. Some of these technologies spread more quickly than others. The time that it takes for a technology to spread can depend on the price of the item, the value it creates for the consumer, and how accessible the item is, among other factors.

The globalization of the earth has increased the speed of the spread of many modern technologies. Globalization generally links countries so that knowledge is more easily spread among various regions. Additionally, it is thought that globalization increases competition for technological developments among nations.

Examples of Revised Technology
Over time, many technologies are changed and revised. Additionally, new technologies are built on the foundation of older technologies. Humans use existing building blocks to assemble new technologies. One example is the printing press. Gutenberg, who created technology for printing with movable type in the 15th century, was not the first to use moveable pieces in the printing process. In 11th century China, separate porcelain pieces were used for printing. Additionally, in 13th century Korea, individual metal characters were part of the printing process. Gutenburg was able to use previously developed technologies, like the Roman alphabet, metal-smelting, the wooden press, ink, and paper to create a new technology. Following Gutenberg's printing press, paper printing became an important way of disseminating information. Eventually, technologies such as the mass printing of books, magazines, and newspapers followed.

title: 🇲🇰 Ohridde İlk Kağıt Baskı Makinesinin Olduğu Yer #ohrid #makedonya #johannesgutenberg #kağıtbaskısı
description:
Johannes Gutenberg (Johannes Gensfleisch zur Laden zum Gutenberg) (1398 - 3 Şubat 1468), 1447 yılında hareketli parçalar ile yazı baskısını Avrupa'da başlatan Alman kuyumcu, matbaacı ve yayıncıdır.
Johannes Gutenberg iletişim tarihinin en önemli gelişmelerinden biri sayılan tipo baskı yöntemini 1438'de Avrupa'ya getirerek uygulamalarını yaygınlaştırmıştır. Bu yöntem, önceleri tahtadan daha sonraları bir kurşun alaşımından yapılan dökme harflerin, baskıdan sonra başka bir yazıda kullanılmak üzere saklandığı bir basım yöntemidir. Bu, yüzyıllardır Çin ve Kore'de kullanılmaktaydı. Gutenberg, hız ve dayanıklılığı arttıran, karakterlerin metalden yapıldığı ve isteğe göre değiştirilebildiği bir sistem tasarlamıştır.[1] Gutenberg'in buluşu modern dönemin en önemli olayı ve matbaa devriminin başlangıcı, kendisi de modern matbaacılığın babası kabul edilir.[2] Buluşu Avrupa'da metinlerin ve bilgilerin yayılmasında belirleyici bir unsur olmuştur.
Gutenberg, Rönesans döneminin en önemli buluşlarından birini yapmasına rağmen yoksul bir hayat geçirmiştir. Yoksulluktan, kendisine 1465 yılında "sarayın beyefendisi" unvanını ve ömür boyu emekli maaşını veren Nassaulu Adolphe II sayesinde kurtulmuştur. Üç yıl sonra 1468 yılında öldüğü bilinmektedir. Ölüm günüyle ilgili kesin bir bilgi bulunmamakla beraber, ölüm yıldönümü 3 Şubat tarihinde anılmaktadır.
Ohrid’deki bu küçük atölye, Balkanlar’da geleneksel yöntemle üretime devam eden sayılı yerlerden biri ve en eski el yapımı kağıt atölyesi. 500 yılı aşkın süredir geleneksel yöntemlerle kağıt üretiyorlar. Ljupcho ailesi, Ohrid’in geleneksel kâğıtçılık mirasını nesilden nesile aktarıyor. Şehirde bu mesleği devam ettiren tek aile konumundalar. 
Giriş ücretsiz. Atölyede kullanılan baskı aletleri, Orta Çağ’daki matbaaların birebir replikaları. Kağıt yapımında kullanılan su, Ohrid gölünden gelen saf kaynak suyu.İşlem tamamen el ile yapılıyor: lifler suya karıştırılıyor, tahta kalıplar üzerine seriliyor ve güneşte kurutuluyor.
Ziyaretçiler kendi kağıtlarını burada damgalayıp hatıra olarak alabiliyor. Doğal lifler sayesinde kağıt, sıradan kağıtlara göre daha kalın ve dayanıklı. Atölye, Ohrid’in tarihi bölgesinde olduğu için, atölye binası da dolaylı olarak UNESCO kapsamında korunuyor.
Johannes Gutenberg (1398 – February 3, 1468) was a German goldsmith, printer, and publisher who pioneered movable type printing in Europe in 1447. He introduced letterpress printing, considered one of the most important developments in the history of communication, to Europe in 1438 and popularized its application. This method uses cast letters, initially made of wood and later of a lead alloy, which are stored for later use in another text. This had been used in China and Korea for centuries. Gutenberg designed a system that increased speed and durability, with characters made of metal and changeable as needed.[1] Gutenberg's invention is considered the most important event of the modern era and the beginning of the printing revolution; he is regarded as the father of modern printing.[2] His invention was a decisive factor in the dissemination of texts and information in Europe. Gutenberg, despite creating one of the most important inventions of the Renaissance, lived a life of poverty. He escaped poverty thanks to Adolphe II of Nassau, who granted him the title of "gentleman of the court" and a lifetime pension in 1465. He is known to have died three years later, in 1468. While there is no definitive information about the exact date of his death, his anniversary is commemorated on February 3rd.
This small workshop in Ohrid is one of the few places in the Balkans that continues to produce paper using traditional methods and is the oldest handmade paper workshop. They have been producing paper using traditional methods for over 500 years. The Ljupcho family passes on Ohrid's traditional papermaking heritage from generation to generation. They are the only family in the city that continues this profession.

Entry is free. The printing equipment used in the workshop is a faithful replica of medieval printing presses. The water used in paper making is pure spring water from Lake Ohrid. The process is entirely handcrafted: the fibers are mixed with the water, spread onto wooden molds, and dried in the sun. Visitors can stamp their own paper here and take it as a souvenir. Thanks to the natural fibers, the paper is thicker and more durable than ordinary paper. Because the workshop is located in the historic district of Ohrid, the workshop building is also indirectly protected under UNESCO.

title: Print and Modern Culture Explained SimplyPrint Culture in Easy English | Full Chapter
description:
The Hook: The Era of "No Copy-Paste"
Imagine living in a world without the internet, smartphones, or even photocopiers. If you wanted a copy of a book a thousand years ago, you had to sit down and write every single word by hand. These handwritten documents were called manuscripts. Because they took months to create, books were incredibly rare, extremely expensive, and restricted only to kings, wealthy elites, and monks. Knowledge was heavily controlled. This chapter is the story of how one machine broke that monopoly and caused the first great "Information Revolution" in human history.

Phase 1: The Asian Origins (Woodblock Printing)
The Concept: The story of print does not begin in Europe; it begins in East Asia—specifically China, Japan, and Korea.

The Method: They invented Woodblock Printing. Artisans would carve an entire page of text in reverse onto a block of wood, apply ink, and press paper against it. Think of it like a giant, hand-carved rubber stamp.

The Purpose: In China, the imperial state sponsored this. They needed a massive number of textbooks to help citizens study for the civil service examinations, making China the first major hub of mass-produced printed material.

Phase 2: The Catalyst of Change (The Gutenberg Press)
The Concept: While woodblock printing was a leap forward, carving a new wooden block for every single page was still incredibly slow. Human society needed a catalyst to speed things up.

The Innovation: In the 1430s, a German goldsmith named Johann Gutenberg invented the first mechanical printing press. His greatest innovation was Movable Type. Instead of carving a whole page, he created individual metal pieces for each letter of the alphabet. You could arrange these metal letters to form words, print a page, and then rearrange the exact same letters to print a completely different page the next day.

The Impact: The first book he printed was the Gutenberg Bible. This invention brought the cost of books down drastically and increased the speed of production. Knowledge was finally escaping the hands of the elite.

Phase 3: The Chain Reaction (Martin Luther & The Reformation)
The Concept: Print allowed ideas to travel faster than the people who thought of them.

The Event: In 1517, a religious reformer named Martin Luther wrote the '95 Theses', a document heavily criticizing the corrupt practices of the Roman Catholic Church.

The Viral Effect: Before the printing press, the Church would have simply silenced him. But Luther’s writings were immediately printed, translated, and distributed across Europe in thousands of copies. It was the 16th-century equivalent of a viral post. This explosion of printed ideas directly triggered the Protestant Reformation, permanently dividing the Church.

Phase 4: The Psychological Shift (Reading Mania & Revolution)
The Concept: By the 17th and 18th centuries, literacy rates went up, creating a "Reading Mania" across Europe. People became obsessed with reading books, journals, and newspapers.

The Impact: This created a massive psychological shift. Instead of just believing what the King or the Church told them, people began reading the ideas of Enlightenment thinkers like Voltaire and Rousseau. They started discussing logic, reason, equality, and democracy. This widespread circulation of revolutionary ideas created the intellectual foundation for the French Revolution.

Phase 5: The Indian Awakening (Print in India)
The Concept: The printing press came to India via Portuguese missionaries in Goa. When it spread, it became a powerful weapon for social reform and democratization.

The Democratization of Voice: Print allowed marginalized communities to finally be heard. Thinkers like Jyotiba Phule used print to expose the injustices of the caste system.

Women and Print: At a time when society believed educated women would be cursed, women like Rashsundari Debi secretly learned to read in their kitchens and wrote autobiographies. Print gave women a platform to share their experiences and demand equality.

The Final Takeaway
To conclude the lesson, emphasize this core idea: The printing press was not just a machine that put ink on paper. It was the original information technology that democratized knowledge, challenged absolute power, and shaped the modern, thinking world we live in today.

title: Discovery of Printing Press: Renaissance | Industrial Revolution | Text Books | Middle Class Life
description:
The printing press, one of the most significant inventions in human history, revolutionized the way information was disseminated and greatly influenced the course of history. The invention of the printing press is primarily attributed to Johannes Gutenberg, a German blacksmith, goldsmith, printer, and publisher, who developed the technology around 1440.

Early Printing Methods
Before the advent of the printing press, the reproduction of books and documents was a laborious and time-consuming process. Manuscripts were copied by hand, predominantly by monks in monasteries, which made books rare and expensive. This limited access to knowledge and literacy to the privileged few.

Movable Type and Early Innovations
While printing methods such as woodblock printing had been used in Asia for centuries, the innovation that set the stage for the printing revolution in Europe was the development of movable type. The concept of movable type was first developed in China by Bi Sheng around 1040 AD, but it did not spread widely or have a profound impact in the West at that time.

Johannes Gutenberg and the Printing Press
Johannes Gutenberg, born in Mainz, Germany, is credited with combining several existing technologies and ideas to create the printing press around 1440. His key innovation was the development of a mechanical movable type system. Gutenberg's press utilized metal movable type, which was more durable and capable of producing clearer, more consistent print than the earlier wooden blocks.

Key components of Gutenberg's printing press included:

Movable Type: Individual letters and characters cast in metal that could be arranged and reused.
Ink: An oil-based ink that adhered well to metal type and paper, producing high-quality prints.
Printing Press: A screw press adapted from agricultural presses, which applied even pressure to transfer the ink from the type to paper.
The Gutenberg Bible
One of Gutenberg's most famous works is the "Gutenberg Bible," also known as the 42-line Bible, printed around 1455. This was the first major book printed using movable type and is often cited as the beginning of the "Gutenberg Revolution" and the "Age of the Printed Book." The Gutenberg Bible demonstrated the press's potential for producing books quickly and in large quantities, dramatically reducing the cost of books and making them more accessible to the public.

Impact of the Printing Press
The invention of the printing press had profound and wide-reaching effects on society:

Spread of Knowledge: The printing press enabled the rapid and wide dissemination of knowledge, ideas, and information. This facilitated the spread of the Renaissance, Reformation, and the Scientific Revolution.
Literacy and Education: Books became more affordable and widely available, leading to an increase in literacy rates and the democratization of knowledge.
Standardization: Printed materials helped standardize languages and texts, contributing to the development of national languages and literatures.
Cultural and Religious Changes: The ability to print religious texts, such as the Bible, allowed for the spread of different interpretations and contributed to religious reforms, including the Protestant Reformation.

Conclusion
The printing press is a landmark invention that changed the course of history by making information more accessible and fostering an environment of learning and inquiry. Johannes Gutenberg’s contribution through the development of the mechanical movable type printing press cannot be overstated, as it laid the foundation for the modern knowledge-based society we live in today.

description:
Definition of Technology
In modern times, the term technology is sometimes used to describe relatively recently invented devices, such as computers, cellular phones, and cars. Historically, however, technology has been defined much more broadly and includes more primitive machines, devices, and tools, such as hunting spears, the wheel, and the printing press. Technology is the application of systematic knowledge toward the development of a device, machine, or method of making or doing things.
Examples of Early Technology Spreading
Once technologies are used successfully, there is a tendency to spread the new method, device, or tool. With some technologies, it is difficult to pinpoint the details of technology adaptation because there are often many iterations or versions of a technology. The spread is also dependent on the characteristics of the technology itself, like how expensive and accessible it is.

The technology of writing, for instance, started in ancient Mesopotamia when accountants used the method to track the flow of goods. As the practice was established, more and more people started using writing to communicate. Soon after this development, as an independent event, people in Egypt started writing in hieroglyphics. These writing methods spread throughout their geographical regions over time. In ancient times, however, not every citizen wrote. Writing was time-consuming and was generally reserved for the upper portion of society.


Egyptian hieroglyphics are an early writing technology.

hieroglyphics are early writing technology

The spread of more recent technologies can be tracked by data showing when 50 percent of the population owned or used the technology. In the United States in the 1940s, 50 percent of the population had a refrigerator and a telephone, and in the 1990s 50 percent of the population had a dishwasher and a computer. Some of these technologies spread more quickly than others. The time that it takes for a technology to spread can depend on the price of the item, the value it creates for the consumer, and how accessible the item is, among other factors.

The globalization of the earth has increased the speed of the spread of many modern technologies. Globalization generally links countries so that knowledge is more easily spread among various regions. Additionally, it is thought that globalization increases competition for technological developments among nations.

Examples of Revised Technology
Over time, many technologies are changed and revised. Additionally, new technologies are built on the foundation of older technologies. Humans use existing building blocks to assemble new technologies. One example is the printing press. Gutenberg, who created technology for printing with movable type in the 15th century, was not the first to use moveable pieces in the printing process. In 11th century China, separate porcelain pieces were used for printing. Additionally, in 13th century Korea, individual metal characters were part of the printing process. Gutenburg was able to use previously developed technologies, like the Roman alphabet, metal-smelting, the wooden press, ink, and paper to create a new technology. Following Gutenberg's printing press, paper printing became an important way of disseminating information. Eventually, technologies such as the mass printing of books, magazines, and newspapers followed.

title: #Blockprint
description:
History of Woodblock Prints
Woodblocks represent one of the oldest printing techniques, initially employed in carved stamps and seals. However, the advent of paper was essential for the evolution of printmaking. Scientists trace the origins of papermaking back to approximately 105 AD in China.

Stone carving likely emerged as the earliest printing technology: stones were rubbed with moist paper and ink, resulting in engraved calligraphy. Subsequently or concurrently, woodblock printing developed. The first Chinese woodblock printed book, the Diamant-Sutra from Dunhuang, dates back to 868 and showcases such technical advancement that it's assumed woodblocks must have existed long before that time.

There is a Japanese print, "Dharanis," dating back to 770, strongly influenced by Chinese prints. The "Dharanis" consisted of Buddhist prayers and had a circulation of one million. However, it remains unclear whether they were created using woodblock printing plates. Initially, woodcuts in China were exclusively used to disseminate Buddhist texts and amulets during the early centuries.

Woodblocks in Europe
Woodblock printing arrived much later in Europe. Printing on cloth using a wooden matrix was common for several centuries. However, woodblock printing had to wait until the introduction of paper production in paper mills in the 1390s.

The first woodblock prints were single-page prints depicting holy figures, often invoked during difficult times for protection. They were affixed to book covers or pinned to walls. Sometimes entire doors and ceilings were adorned with these prints.

Early prints were handcrafted using a muller. Occasionally, they were hand-colored by "letter painters" using patterns, leaving visible lines intentionally.

Woodblock prints in Europe began to develop their distinctive style towards the end of the High Gothic period. Line cuts were prevalent during this period. The style resembled the black lead drawing of colored Gothic glass windows. It wasn't until the 19th century that finely detailed master prints emerged, often utilized as book illustrations.

Following single-page prints, block books emerged, where prints were pressed or rubbed onto pages. Since printing was possible on only one side, two blank sheets were glued together to form a single page. Text was also carved into woodblocks, accompanying printed illustrations. Common block book themes included the poor's Bible, the Dance of Death, or books about planets.

Despite the invention of movable type around 1440, block books endured. Pictures began to dominate over text, reversing the ratio seen in previous block books. They were produced using printing presses, with woodblocks cut to the height of lead letters and fitted into the printing press.
Hartmann Schedel and Albrecht Dürer
Around 1500, woodcut illustration flourished in central Germany (Augsburg, Ulm, Nuremberg) and the Netherlands, notably in book illustrations. One of the pivotal works from this era is Schedel's Weltchronik, which boasted 2,000 woodblocks crafted by about 100 artisans using 24 printing presses in Nuremberg.

During this period, it was customary to differentiate between the individuals involved in the woodblock creation process: the artist who drew, the carver who cut, and the printer who transferred the image onto paper.

The designers often remained anonymous, and only a few artists stood out. Foremost among them was Albrecht Dürer (1471-1528) from Nuremberg, who revolutionized woodblock making, elevating the art form to new heights. His expansive woodblocks, notably "Apocalypse" with its monumental and dramatic composition, earned him fame and infused woodblock printing with fresh significance.

description:
Definition of Technology
In modern times, the term technology is sometimes used to describe relatively recently invented devices, such as computers, cellular phones, and cars. Historically, however, technology has been defined much more broadly and includes more primitive machines, devices, and tools, such as hunting spears, the wheel, and the printing press. Technology is the application of systematic knowledge toward the development of a device, machine, or method of making or doing things.
Examples of Early Technology Spreading
Once technologies are used successfully, there is a tendency to spread the new method, device, or tool. With some technologies, it is difficult to pinpoint the details of technology adaptation because there are often many iterations or versions of a technology. The spread is also dependent on the characteristics of the technology itself, like how expensive and accessible it is.

The technology of writing, for instance, started in ancient Mesopotamia when accountants used the method to track the flow of goods. As the practice was established, more and more people started using writing to communicate. Soon after this development, as an independent event, people in Egypt started writing in hieroglyphics. These writing methods spread throughout their geographical regions over time. In ancient times, however, not every citizen wrote. Writing was time-consuming and was generally reserved for the upper portion of society.


Egyptian hieroglyphics are an early writing technology.

hieroglyphics are early writing technology

The spread of more recent technologies can be tracked by data showing when 50 percent of the population owned or used the technology. In the United States in the 1940s, 50 percent of the population had a refrigerator and a telephone, and in the 1990s 50 percent of the population had a dishwasher and a computer. Some of these technologies spread more quickly than others. The time that it takes for a technology to spread can depend on the price of the item, the value it creates for the consumer, and how accessible the item is, among other factors.

The globalization of the earth has increased the speed of the spread of many modern technologies. Globalization generally links countries so that knowledge is more easily spread among various regions. Additionally, it is thought that globalization increases competition for technological developments among nations.

Examples of Revised Technology
Over time, many technologies are changed and revised. Additionally, new technologies are built on the foundation of older technologies. Humans use existing building blocks to assemble new technologies. One example is the printing press. Gutenberg, who created technology for printing with movable type in the 15th century, was not the first to use moveable pieces in the printing process. In 11th century China, separate porcelain pieces were used for printing. Additionally, in 13th century Korea, individual metal characters were part of the printing process. Gutenburg was able to use previously developed technologies, like the Roman alphabet, metal-smelting, the wooden press, ink, and paper to create a new technology. Following Gutenberg's printing press, paper printing became an important way of disseminating information. Eventually, technologies such as the mass printing of books, magazines, and newspapers followed.
